SHRI K. E. PRAKASH (ERODE): Madam Chairperson, Vanakkam. In my Erode
parliamentary constituency, stray or infected dog menace is a major issue affecting
the people living in the areas of Dharapuram, Kangeyam, Modakkurichi, Erode-
East, Erode-West and Kumarapalayam Assembly constituencies including the
Chennimalai area, People of these areas live in fear and insecurity due to this
menace. Farmers are very much affected in rural areas due to this. Domestic
animals and home-reared animals like goats, cows, hens etc. are often attacked
by the furious, un-owned infected dogs which result in the deaths of such domestic
animals and thereby affecting the livelihood of farmers, at large. Domestic dogs,
once infected, become furious and attack children living in the vicinity resulting in
even loss of their lives.
I, therefore, urge upon the Union Government to take a census of such
infected and rabies-affected dogs besides providing compensation to the farmers
as against the loss of their cattle in the dog attacks. Moreover, I urge the Union
Government to bring a law to tackle the problem of infected dog menace. I want
the Union Government to clarify and inform the measures undertaken by
organizations like the Blue Cross and the Animal Welfare Board of India in
controlling the infected dog menace in protecting the lives of the innocent people.
Similarly, on behalf of the Government of Tamil Nadu and its people, I urge
upon the Union government to bring home-reared animals like goat, cow, hen, etc.
under life insurance cover in order to mitigate the sorrows and financial loss faced
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
19.03.2025 1081
by the farmers, Tamil Nadu will always fight for its rights. Tamil Nadu will win. Thank
you.
SHRI Y. S. AVINASH REDDY (KADAPA): Thank you, Madam. I would like to draw
the attention of the House to the decision of the Government to relocate the
headquarters of Andhra Pragathi Grameena Bank from Kadapa to Amaravati.
Madam, Andhra Pragathi Grameena Bank, established in 2006 with Kadapa
as headquarters, manages a business volume of Rs.46,000 crore with nearly 3,000
staff members. It plays a crucial role in the financial inclusion of rural and
underserved communities, making it one of the largest RRBs in India.
APGB has a wide network of 551 branches spread over ten districts of the
Rayalaseema backward region, namely Ananthapuram, Sri Sathya Sai,
Annamayya, YSR Kadapa, Kurnool, Nandyal, Tirupati, Nellore, Bapatla, and
Prakasham districts of Andhra Pradesh.
Madam, the Government’s decision to shift the headquarters to Amaravati
will increase operational expenses, reduce accessibility, and alienate rural
customers. The central location of the headquarters of this Bank at Kadapa within
its operational area is more conducive to fulfil the Bank’s mandate. In view of the
inconvenience and possible financial loss, on behalf of the people of backward
Rayalaseema region, we strongly urge the Government not to shift the
headquarters of Andhra Pragathi Grameena Bank from Kadapa. Thank you.

SHRI SHREYAS M. PATEL (HASSAN): I need to highlight a pressing issue in my
constituency, which needs urgent attention from the Forest Department. It is about
the human and animal conflict. In just one month, four people have lost their lives
in Arehalli and Bikkodu Hobli due to elephant attacks. The situation is out of control
in Belur, Sakleshpur, and Alur taluks. Families are grieving; farmers are scared;
and livelihoods are at risk. In the past 25 years, around 82 lives have been lost.
The farmers no longer grow paddy; the coffee plantations are destroyed; and the
workers lose their wages because they fear for their lives. The elephant population
has now risen to around 80 in the region. Removing them from one restricted place
is the only temporary relief. We need a permanent solution.
I urge the Ministry to create a dedicated elephant corridor grant for
Sakleshpur and Alur taluks. An ideal habitat should be created so that elephants
do not enter villages. Most importantly, I request a dedicated fund under NDRF for
Hassan district to compensate families affected by elephant attacks. This is a crisis.
The people of Hassan are tired of living in fear. We request an action. This is my
humble request. Kindly ask the Forest Department to take an action.

SHRI N. K. PREMACHANDRAN (KOLLAM): Thank you very much, Madam
Chairperson, for allowing me to raise a very important matter regarding the parallel
colleges in the State of Kerala and the GST imposed on the parallel colleges.
Madam Chairperson, the parallel colleges in the State of Kerala are
contributing exemplary service in the field of higher education.
Those students, who are not getting an opportunity to get an admission in
the regular colleges, have the option to approach the parallel colleges where they
can continue their higher studies. Wonderful and exemplary coaching is being
provided to the students by the parallel colleges. However, unfortunately, the
19.03.2025 1092
parallel colleges are now following the curriculum directed or guided by the
university, Government colleges, and aided colleges. The curriculum and the
syllabus are the same as those of the Government and aided colleges.
Unfortunately, the GST Department of the Kerala State has issued a circular
classifying the parallel colleges as commercial coaching centres. This classification
means that the parallel colleges will not come within the purview of the commercial
coaching centres, and thereby GST is imposed on these parallel colleges. Madam
Chairperson, what would be the effect? The effect will be that it will directly hit the
poor students. The parallel colleges will have to increase their fees, which will
indirectly place a greater burden on the shoulders of the poor students and their
guardians. Classifying them as commercial coaching centres is neither fair nor right
nor legal.
Madam Chairperson, I was also a teacher and lecturer in the parallel
colleges, and I know the situation very well. Therefore, I urge the Government of
India to withdraw the GST on parallel colleges that are being well run in the State
of Kerala, thereby protecting the poor students. Thank you.
SHRIMATI PRATIMA MONDAL (JAYNAGAR): Madam, I would like to draw the
attention of this House to a matter of public importance. Madam, Joynagar-II block
and Kultali, both fall under my Parliamentary Constituency, which is a completely
rural area. The total population of the said area is more than five lakhs. The people
have been deprived of availing the railway facility since Independence.
The total distance from the last station from Joynagar to Moipith is around
50 kilometres. Therefore, the students have no option but to shift to other locations
19.03.2025 1093
for their higher studies. I would like to request the hon. Railway Minister to introduce
a new railway line project from Joynagar to Moipith via Jamtala for the greater
interest of the lakhs of people living in this local area. Thank you.

SHRI OMPRAKASH BHUPALSINH ALIAS PAVAN RAJENIMBALKAR
(OSMANABAD): Hon. Madam Chairperson, thank you. Through you, I would like
to draw Hon. Home Minister Shri Amit Shahji’s attention towards a very serious
issue pertaining to my Lok Sabha Constituency. A narcotic substance called MD
drug is being sold and supplied in Tuljapur, Paranda area of my constituency. It is
found selling for the first time on 14th February and later on 4th March for the second
time.
Madam, few offenders were arrested and then a shocking truth was
revealed. It is being sold for the last 2-3 years and a woman who is running this
racket, has stashed around Rs. 5 crore in her bank account. Some amount of gold
was also seized. My question is very simple, ‘who has been supporting and
protecting this illegal activity for the last 2-3 years? Even local police are also
involved in it and that is why no serious investigation has been initiated in this
regard till date. They are taking this serious issue very lightly.
I have sent written complaints to the State Home Minister as well as Union
Home Minister. This drug racket should be banned and destroyed immediately and
Central Narcotics Bureau must investigate it to unearth the roots of this drug cartel
to save our young generation. Hence, I request you to take an immediate action
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
19.03.2025 1094
in this connection and put all the culprits behind bars.
Thank you.

(ii) Need to implement periodic cancer screening programme in the country
SHRI P. P. CHAUDHARY (PALI): Under Prime Minister’s visionary leadership,
India has taken significant steps in healthcare, including the recent allocation of
₹3,200 crore for 200 Day Care Cancer Centres. Today, I draw the attention of the
Hon'ble Minister of Health and Family Welfare to the critical need for a national
cancer screening program. Cancer prevalence in India has risen by over 50% in
three decades, with a mortality rate of approximately 64%. Alarmingly, over 60% of
cancers in India are detected at Stage III, unlike developed nations where detection
occurs predominantly at Stages I or II. This late detection significantly reduces
survival rates and breast cancer survival in India is only 68% compared to 90% in
developed countries. Early detection is cost-effective and life-saving. Research
indicates that breast cancer screening can avert up to 105.8 'disability-adjusted life
years' per 1,000 women. However, our screening rates remain abysmally low–2%
19.03.2025 1098
for cervical cancer and just 1% for breast and oral cancers, compared to over 70%
in countries with national screening programs. I urge the Government to implement
periodic cancer screening program, initially for Govt. & Public Sector employees
aged above 40, and gradually expanding nationwide. This approach would reduce
mortality rates, healthcare costs, and normalize screenings to overcome
psychological barriers.
(iii) Need to include various temples and pilgrimage sites in Telangana
under PRASAD Scheme
SHRIMATI D. K. ARUNA (MAHBUBNAGAR): I would like to bring to kind notice
of the Government the need to consider the following proposals for inclusion of
prominent temples from erstwhile Mahabubnagar district under PRASAD Scheme
1st Phase for Pilgrimage Site Development in Telangana state which are essential.
Sri Kurumurthy Srinivasa Swamy Temple in Kurumurthy, Mahabubnagar District,
is a major attraction for devotees of Lord Vishnu, and it is often referred to as the
"Second Tirupati" due to its significance among devotees; Sri Venkateswara
Swamy Temple - Manyam Konda in Mahabubnagar Mandal District is dedicated
to Lord Venkateswara; Sri Timmappa Temple in Maldakal Mandal, Jogulamba
Gadwal District holds significant spiritual importance in the hearts of devotees
across Telangana, Karnataka, and Maharashtra and hosts an annual Jathara
(Tirunala) on December Pournami, drawing nearly 20 lakh devotees; Sri Rama
Temple - Sirisanagundla in Kalwakurthy - Devarakonda Line, Nagarkurnool District
is known for its exquisite stone sculpture and remarkable architecture; Beechupally
Hanuman Temple in Itikyala Mandal, Jogulamba Gadwal District is located on the
19.03.2025 1099
banks of Krishna River above National Highway No.44 and about 30 kilometers
downstream after Jurala Water Project in Jogulamba Gadwal district, Telangana
and lakhs of devotees will visit this place for the Krishna Pushkaralu to be held in
2028. Therefore, several development programs need to be undertaken for the
convenience of the devotees and Tourism's support for these pilgrimage sites will
significantly boost religious tourism in the region, providing employment
opportunities and fostering cultural preservation. The financial assistance under the
PRASAD scheme will ensure that these sacred sites become world-class
pilgrimage destinations. Therefore, I request the Hon'ble Minister of Tourism, to
kindly include above said 5 projects under PRASAD Scheme.
(iv) Need for widening of Thumpala-KB road in Anakapalli district of Andhra
Pradesh under Central Road and Infrastructure Fund Scheme
DR. C. M. RAMESH (ANAKAPALLE): Rural Areas in Anakapalli District are largely
dependent on Thumapala-KB Road as it connects two important State Highways
of Anakapalli District i.e., AC Road (SH 150) & KB Road (SH 148). The Villages in
this area use the road for transport of agriculture products to Vizianagaram,
Visakhapatnam, Anakkapalli & nearby towns. Besides that metal & other materials
are also transported on this road for development works in Visakhapatnam,
Vizianagaram & Anakapalli as there are many quarries and brick industries
alongside this road. This road also joins more than 9 R&B roads. Many students
who study in the district take this road for commuting to Visakhapatnam, Anakapalli,
Sabbavaram & Vizianagaram towns. Widening of this road assumes critical
importance as it benefits more than 500 villages & one lakh people. There is a great
19.03.2025 1100
need to widen the narrow road as many fatal accidents are taking place. I,
therefore, request the Hon'ble Minister of Road Transport and Highways to take up
the improvement work of this road under Central Road and Infrastructure Funds
scheme.
(v) Need to release approved funds under Development of Wildlife Habitats
Scheme to Odisha
SHRI BHARTRUHARI MAHTAB (CUTTACK): One National Park, sixteen
Sanctuaries, two Conservation Reserves, two Outside Protected Areas and two
Recovery programmes for saving critically endangered species are being operated
under the Central Sector Schemes-Development of Wildlife Habitats in the State of
Odisha. Government provides financial assistance for implementation of the
scheme every year according to the specified Central and State ratio. The Annual
Plan of Operation (APO) under the above Central Sector Scheme for Rs.37.33
crore was submitted to the Government by the State for according the
Administrative approval for the year 2024-25. Though Union Government have
accorded their Administrative approval for Rs.30.12 crore towards implementation
of the said Central Sector Scheme during the year 2024-25 and accordingly
Rs.11.29 crore have been released so far as on date 12.02.2025. Although the
current financial year 2024-25 is approaching to its end very fast, no further funds
are yet to be released during this current financial year. I would urge upon the Union
Government to release immediately the approved funds under the CSS-
Development of Wildlife Habitat in favour of Odisha.
19.03.2025 1101

sanction a new railway line between Khedbrahma and Hadad

(vii) Need to impress upon the State Government of Karnataka to extend
affordable healthcare facilities to the patients of the state through Health
Card Scheme
SHRI KOTA SRINIVASA POOJARY (UDUPI CHIKMAGALUR): The Central
Government's Ayushman Bharat scheme is very useful for poor patients. But, in
Karnataka because of the amount paid by Govt. is very low, private hospitals are
19.03.2025 1102
not attending patients properly. The Karnataka Government has introduced a
health card scheme on the model of the Central Government’s Ayushman Bharat
Scheme and private hospitals are complaining that the amount paid by the State
Government is very less. Therefore, I hereby request the Union Minister of Health
and Family Welfare to hold a meeting with the Karnataka Government and help the
poor patients of the state.
(viii) Need to strengthen bilateral agreements to ensure protection of
migrant workers abroad
SHRI RAJIV PRATAP RUDY (SARAN): I draw attention to the hardships faced
by migrant workers from Bihar, especially in the Middle East with latest reports
indicating that workers from Bihar and Uttar Pradesh continue to migrate to Gulf
countries in large numbers. Between 2012 and mid-2018, over 24,570 Indian
workers died in Gulf nations—an average of 10 deaths per day. For every US $ 1
billion remitted to India, at least 117 workers lost their lives. Bureaucratic delays
often leave bodies unclaimed, causing immense suffering. Even within India, Bihari
migrants face discrimination and exploitation, with 40% of Bihar’s workforce
migrating for work. I urge the Government to create a welfare fund for families of
deceased workers and strengthen bilateral agreements to ensure worker’s
protection abroad. Our workers deserve dignity and protection. Immediate action
is needed.

(xii) Need to re-open the Fishery Survey of India base office at
Paradip, Odisha
SHRI BIBHU PRASAD TARAI (JAGATSINGHPUR): The State of Odisha has a
coastline of 480 km in the North East (NE) coast of India. Coast of Odisha and
West Bengal are the major player in marine capture fisheries in North East coast
contributing nearly 10% of total marine catch of the country. The NE coast is highly
vulnerable and fishermen community are mostly affected due to the natural
disaster. The Fishery Survey of India an institute under the Union Ministry of
Fisheries, Animal Husbandry and Dairying plays vital role in providing relevant
information on quick policy action, but FSI is no more existing in Odisha now. In
the absence of FSI, fishery resources survey, assessment and monitoring etc. are
severely affected. It is true that FSI’s presence in Odisha was way back to 1973 as
Exploratory Fisheries Project and was located at Paradip in Odisha. However, due
to various reasons in the year 1991, Paradip base of FSI was closed. Hence, I
would like to draw the attention of the Union Minister to kindly initiate re-opening of
the FSI base office at Paradip, Odisha at the earliest possible considering the
greater interest of the fishing community of Odisha which was closed 34 years ago
without any valid reason.
(xiii) Need for establishment of Kendriya Vidyalaya in Jarmundi (Dumka),
Deoghar and Mahagama (Godda) in Santhal Pargana region of Jharkhand
DR. NISHIKANT DUBEY (GODDA): Jharkhand state is having two major regions,
19.03.2025 1106
Santhal Pargana and Chhota Nagpur. Keeping the socio-economic problem being
faced by Santhal Pargana in mind where agriculture is only the main source of
income, we see an urgent need for a comprehensive plan of action where special
emphasis on good and equal opportunity of employment friendly education is given
utmost importance. Santhal Pargana districts of Deoghar, Godda, Jamtara, Pakur,
Sahibganj and Dumka count amongst the socially, educationally and economically
backward districts of the country. A look at the statistics of health, education,
income, etc., gives an appalling picture of the poor state of the people. Jharkhand
is a rich state. It possesses 40% of India’s mineral resources, but the access to
resources has made little difference to the lives of the ordinary people. Poverty and
ignorance are still the causes for low literacy rates, poor school attendance and
large-scale drop-outs. I, therefore seek attention of Hon’ble Minister of Education
towards the need to set up three Kendriya Vidyalayas. An ideal location would be
Jarmundi (DUMKA), Deoghar and Mahagama (GODDA) in the Santhal Pargana
Region.

(xix) Need to expedite the completion of construction work on the stretch of
National Highway No. 66 between Kazahakkoottam and Kadambattukonam
in Kerala
ADV. ADOOR PRAKASH (ATTINGAL): The slow pace of works of the
construction of National Highway 66 between Kazhakkoottam and
Kadambattukonam is highly concerning. Immediate intervention of the Government
is necessary to avoid delay and achieving the deadline of NH 66 works in Kerala.
The inefficient handling of this project by the contracting company is the main
reason for works delay on this stretch. There are lot of traffic diversions at various
places on this stretch and proper planning was necessary before starting the works.
But NHAI and the contracting company has not properly studied about the project
implementation in this reach. The contracting company was black listed for major
faults in a bridge construction project in Kerala they had undertaken earlier. The
case against this company is still in court. It is doubtful that how this company was
again selected for construction of NH 66. NHAI needs to take measures for further
study about the site and its topography. It is necessary to ensure coordination with
the local governing bodies and Government departments to avoid further delay.
People are suffering with great difficulties due to incomplete works. I request
immediate intervention of the Government for timely completion of this project.

(xxii) Need to provide free treatment to cancer patients in the country
SHRIMATI PRATIMA MONDAL (JAYNAGAR): As per National Cancer Registry
Programme India, the estimated number of incident cases of cancer in India for the
year 2022 was found to be 14, 61,427(crude rate 100.4) per 1, 00,000. In India one
in nine people are likely to develop cancer in his or her life time. The incidents of
cancer cases are estimated to increase by 12.8% in 2025 as compared to 2020.
While these statistics are alarming, private hospitals are charging exorbitant fees
for the treatment of cancer. This is also true in case of the Government empanelled
19.03.2025 1111
private hospitals. Patients are fighting to win their battle against the disease as well
as struggling to earn sufficient means to survive. Being a public representative, I
place on an average 10 to 12 letters every month to the Prime Minister National
relief fund for getting financial assistance for cancer patients. As per Government
criteria every Member of Parliament is entitled to receive financial assistance for
35 patients in a year. I therefore, request the honourable Prime Minister to provide
completely free treatment for cancer patients in Government hospitals and Central
Government empanelled private hospitals.
(xxiii) Need to expedite completion of laying of new broad gauge railway
line between Dindigul and Sabarimalai in Theni Parliamentary Constituency,
Tamil Nadu
SHRI TAMILSELVAN THANGA (THENI): Expedite completion of broad-gauge
line in my Theni constituency between Dindigul and Sabarimalai. The Sabarimala
temple is situated on a hilltop in Pathanamthitta district Kerala which is unique. It is
the most famous and prominent among all Sastha temples in Kerala. The temple
is open to people belonging to all religions. It is said that the pilgrims have to
observe celibacy for 41 days before going Sabarimala. Pilgrims take the traditional
forest routes as well as the one from Pamba which is less physically challenging to
reach the temple. Every year more than 1 crore pilgrims are travelling to
Sabarimala from various parts of the country including Tamil Nadu through
Dindigul-Theni routes. In 2014, the Railway Board had done the survey for a new
broad-gauge line from Dindigul to Sabarimalai. No further action has been taken
by Railway Board till now. If this railway line is constructed, lakhs of pilgrimage from
19.03.2025 1112
Tamil Nadu will be benefitted and lot of money and time will be saved. Therefore, I
urge upon the Union Government to take immediate steps for completion of this
new line without further delay.
(xxiv) Need to increase number of Parking bays and resting rooms along
National Highways for better traffic management
SHRI ARUN NEHRU (PERAMBALUR): I wish to draw the attention of the
Government to the increasing problem of heavy vehicles being parked on roads
along National Highways due to inadequate parking bays and rest areas. The
presence of shops, wayside amenities, eateries, and bars without proper parking
infrastructure has led to heavy vehicles occupying road space. This has resulted in
frequent accidents, endangering the lives of commuters and disrupting smooth
traffic flow. To mitigate this critical issue, it is imperative to increase the number of
parking bays for trucks and cars at strategic intervals along National Highways;
develop well-equipped rest area complexes with essential amenities for drivers,
including restrooms, eateries, and medical facilities; and implement strict
enforcement measures to ensure vehicles are not parked illegally on roads. I urge
the Government to take immediate action to ensure the safety of road users and to
enhance the infrastructure of National Highways for better traffic management.
(xxv) Need to undertake extensive awareness campaigns and training
programmes to promote sustainable aquaculture in Andhra Pradesh
SHRI G. M. HARISH BALAYOGI (AMALAPURAM): Andhra Pradesh holds a
dominant position in India's aquaculture industry, accounting for 72% of the
country’s shrimp production while utilizing 64% of the designated land. Spanning
19.03.2025 1113
over nearly 4 lakh acres, the state recorded marine product exports worth Rs.
24,523 crores in 2023-24. However, rapid expansion of aquaculture farming has
led to severe environmental and social consequences. Groundwater contamination
from these farms has resulted in soil degradation, declining agricultural
productivity, and health hazards, including skin allergies. Violations of
environmental regulations further exacerbate these challenges, as aquaculture
farming is frequently conducted illegally in non-aquaculture zones. The
unauthorized conversion of agricultural land into aquaculture ponds has led to long-
term ecological damage and significant disruptions to livelihoods. In Konaseema
district, over 400 aquaculture farms have been ordered to be demolished following
directives from the NGT due to violations of CRZ regulations. This enforcement has
triggered community conflicts, as aquaculture farming remains a highly attractive
livelihood. The primary cause is lack of awareness regarding regulatory
requirements, and proper wastewater management. Without adequate knowledge,
farmers face legal repercussions and financial losses. Therefore, it is imperative
that the Ministry implements extensive awareness campaigns and training
initiatives to promote responsible and sustainable aquaculture practices.

(xxvii) Need for setting up of Coconut Board in Andhra Pradesh
SHRI BALASHOWRY VALLABHANENI (MACHILIPATNAM): Even though
Andhra Pradesh is 4th largest in terms of area of coconut cultivation and
production, but when it comes to productivity i.e., number of coconuts per hectare,
Andhra Pradesh is on the top in the entire world. Sir, with nearly 16,000 coconuts
per hectare, AP’s productivity is highest in the world. No doubt, India’s area of
cultivation and production are going up, but productivity is coming down.
Productivity came down from 11,481 coconuts per hectare in 2016-17 to just 9,018
coconuts in 2022-23. This clearly indicates that there is a need to set up Coconut
Board in AP for integrated development of coconut production, productivity and
product diversification. The absence of Coconut Board, Regional Office or even
State Centre in AP which has highest productivity of coconut is unfair and impacting
coconut farmers, production, productivity and R&D. Hence, I appeal for setting up
of Coconut Board in Andhra Pradesh.

(xxix) Need to ensure enforcement of Panchayats Extension of

SHRI T. R. BAALU (SRIPERUMBUDUR): Hon. Chairperson, Sir, with your
permission, I would like to discuss the Demands for Grants under the Ministry of
Jal Shakti for 2025-26. ....(Interruptions)
The Ministry of Jal Sakti has got two Departments – the Department of
Drinking Water and Sanitation, and the Department of Water Resources, River
Development and Ganga Rejuvenation.
The Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) intends to provide 55 litres of potable water
per person per day to every rural household through functional household tap
connections. This scheme had to be completed by 2024. But today we are in 2025.
As of now, only 80 per cent of the scheme has been completed as far as Jal Jeevan
Mission is concerned. As only 15 crore of households have been given tap
connections, the balance 20 per cent households are yet to be provided the
connection. By which year the remaining connections will be completed has not
been quoted anywhere by the Ministry. So, nearly 20 per cent household
connections have yet to be done. This is very important and the Government should
come forward to see that this is done on time.
I have only one question in this regard. In 2023-24, an amount of Rs.69,992
19.03.2025 1140
crore have been spent. Last year, the earmarked amount to be provided was
Rs.70,000 crore whereas the Government spent only Rs.22,000 crore. So, nearly
an amount of Rs.50,000 crore have not been spent. That means, the Government
is not really interested to see that the project is completed in 2024-25 itself. They
have not spent Rs.50,000 crore earmarked last year for the project. I want to know
why this amount sanctioned has not been spent so far.
Sir, initially in 2019, when the programme was launched, the total outlay was
Rs. 7 lakh crore. Now, they have spent only Rs. 3 lakh crore, and another Rs. 4
lakh crore need to be spent to complete the project. This is the status of the Jal
Jeevan Mission. The Jal Jeevan Mission is intended to cover the entire rural India,
from the source to the habitation. However, so far, things have not developed as
such. In the 2014 election, our friend Modiji promised during the election campaign
that he would ensure the inter-State river linking would be established. He
mentioned about Himalayan rivers as well as Peninsular rivers. But so far, they
have not brought out any scheme and no DPR has been created for the Himalayan
and Peninsular rivers. There is no scheme at all. As far as the Peninsular rivers are
concerned, this is a water-starving area, a water-deficit area, whereas the
Himalayan rivers are water-surplus areas. Water surplus areas must be connected
to water-starving, water-deficit areas.
Just now, my friend, Mr. Jagdambika mentioned that Arthur Cotton, the
British engineer, initiated the project. He created many forums and discussed the
matter with various technical groups, but nothing could be accomplished by himself.
Now, afterwards, in independent India, many forums and groups of engineers have
19.03.2025 1141
discussed this, and even Shri K. L. Rao came forward in Parliament, stating that
he would ensure that the rivers of Peninsular India would be connected as early as
possible. However, nothing has been done so far. The Indian rivers in Peninsular
India, especially the Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Pennar, and Kaveri, need to be
connected so that maximum benefit can be derived for Peninsular India. But so far,
things are not moving in the correct direction. It is moving at a snail’s speed. Prime
Minister Modi came forward to connect Ken and Betwa, a distance of 221
kilometres between UP and Madhya Pradesh. I am not against it; I support it.
However, more initiatives need to be taken. The Ken and Betwa project between
UP and Madhya Pradesh is being implemented, with more than Rs. 9,000 crore
invested so far. But we do not know when that particular project will be completed.
That is one thing.
Another issue in Peninsular India is that things must be taken in the correct
perspective. So far, nothing has been developed, and even the project report has
not been created. When Shri K. L. Rao was a Minister, he categorically stated that
this could be done shortly. However, we do not know how far ‘shortly’ is. He has
passed away, but things are not moving in the correct direction. After Ken and
Betwa, we thought the Government would connect the Peninsular rivers of India,
but so far, there has been no progress. There has been no discussion during the
Question Hour or in discussions like this. The Minister concerned, the new Minister,
should clarify when they will take up the Peninsular India inter-connection. What
are the additional benefits of connecting north-south rivers? I believe the maximum
transport system can be established in river waters, allowing river waters to
19.03.2025 1142
transport cargo from north to south and south to north. This matter must be
considered positively because the cash flow and the benefits from this project will
be enormous.
15.00 hrs
Not only that, the power utilized for this purpose can be recovered by
developing power stations here and there according to the levels of water flow. So,
power generation can be done; pollution control could be done; and fisheries can
be developed. These are all the benefits out of it, but the Government is hesitant
to do it.
The Government has taken up the Mumbai-Ahmedabad Bullet Train project

SHRI HIBI EDEN (ERNAKULAM): I wish to raise certain critical issues in respect
of the demand for grants for the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
The Ministry of Jal Shakti is responsible for management of water across India and
for making sure that water is available for diverse needs such as domestic use,
sanitation, irrigation etc. However, the Government has been severely
underperforming when it comes to carrying out tasks relating to water management
and utilization of funds for development of water resources and projects in India.
I wish to point out in this regard that the Standing Committee on Water Resources
in its Report tabled a few days ago has highlighted that nearly 40% of the allocated
funds have remained unspent and that there were massive delays in fund disbursal
and project execution. In fact, while assessing the budgetary planning of the Jal
Shakti Department of the Central Government, the committee noted as follows:
The Committee observe that frequent drastic reduction of budgetary provisions of
the Department at RE stage and subsequently enhancing the BE for next Financial
Year indicates lack of proper planning for budgetary requirements by the
Department. The Committee, therefore, recommend that the Department
undertake their pre-budget exercise with much more care and prudence for making
more pragmatic and conservative budgetary estimates. [2.1-recommendations In
this regard, I must point out that the Jal Jeevan Mission, which is one of the flagship
programs of the Central Government, had seen huge dip in the revised budgetary
estimates stage which has resulted in the scheme not being able to be as effective
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1144
as it was aimed to be. The Parliamentary Standing Committee has also taken a
sharp note on this situation:
The Committee would like to be apprised of the specific reasons for such a huge
reduction at RE stage for FY 2024-25 and its impact on execution of this ambitious
Programme. In view of the fact that JJM has been extended till 2028, the
Committee urge upon the Department to make concerted efforts to utilize the
budgetary allocations optimally in order to obviate the possibility of huge reduction
at RE stage which has been witnessed in current Financial Year (2024-25).
I must point out that the Central Government has failed to cover large number of
households in Kerala with functional tap water connections which is one of the key
aspects included in the Jal Jeevan Mission. The Central Government had a target
of covering all households by 2024, however, this target is far from being achieved
till date. The Standing Committee on Water Resources, in its report tabled in
Parliament in February 2025 had noted that the progress of the Jal Jeevan Mission
in many states was quite low. In fact, the panel report categorically stated that it
was 'concerned and dismayed' and since the launch of the mission in 2019, only
11 states had been able to achieve the 'Har Ghar Jal' Status.
If we take States such as Kerala, Jharkhand, Rajasthan and West Bengal, then
less than 60% of the households have functional taps in these states. It is important
to note that most of these States are opposition ruled states and therefore a clear
bias of the government in implementing central schemes comes out when one sees
these figures. I believe that no government should discriminate when it comes to
resources such as water, however, this government has not even spared this
19.03.2025 1145
domain. The Parliamentary Standing Committee on Water Resources has taken a
strong note of this situation in its Report:
"However, the Committee note that still a major part of the work remains to be
completed in West Bengal, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Kerala and Jharkhand
which have so far achieved coverage of only 54.51%, 55.39%, 67.29%, 54.25%
and 54.66% respectively.
Being of the view that active participation of the States is essential to make the
Programme a success, the Committee urge upon the Department to look into the
particular problems of each of the lagging States hampering timely completion of
the Projects and take remedial measures for faster implementation of the Projects
in these States."
Additionally, many project clearance proposal of states such as Kerala (in relation
to Jal Jeevan Mission) have been pending with the Central Government and have
not been cleared till date which further adds to the problem of effective
implementation of the Jal Jeevan Mission across India. This aspect has yet again
been noted in the Parliamentary Committee Report:
"Similarly, in case of Kerala, out of 67 proposals submitted with National Highways
Authority of India (NHAI), 29 are pending. The Committee in view of the objective
of the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) of providing assured potable tap water in adequate
quantity on a regular and long-term basis to every rural household urge the
Department to make necessary endeavours to expedite the clearance of the
pending projects of rural water supply within a reasonable timeframe"
I wish to also highlight that in view of the budgetary discrepancies and unclear
19.03.2025 1146
policies by both the Central and the State Government, the works in relation to
many Jal Jeevan projects have been abandoned by contractors in Kerala due to
huge pending dues and therefore there is a complete stalemate in such projects.
Another major issue is the provision of good quality drinking water to people all
over the country. Till date, the Government has not been able to implement a
mechanism through which quality drinking water is reaching each and every
household in India. If we take the State of Kerala, almost 40% households would
not meet quality drinking water standards (as per an Assessment Report of 2022).
Accordingly, the government surely needs to work on this to improve this situation.
I wish to highlight that there are about 11000 affected habitations / affected
areas where the water is completely contaminated due to iron, salinity, nitrate and
heavy metals where short term measures need to be immediately provided to help
the affected population. In Kerala, the districts of Allappuzha, Idukki, Kannur,
Kasaragod, Kozhikode, Malapurram, Palakkad, Thiruvanthampuram, Thissur and
Wayanad are such affected areas and therefore the government needs to take
immediate action to improve the contaminated water situation in such places.
We live in a world where resources are scarce and therefore conservation and
effective utilization of water becomes very important in today's day and age.
Rainwater harvesting should therefore be promoted by the Central Government
and I would urge the Central Government to increase the funding and direct
incentives to states and local bodies so that they can make provisions and create
adequate infrastructure for rainwater harvesting.
This Government has time and again made tall claims that it wants to clean and
19.03.2025 1147
sanitize Ganga, however, the reality is quite different and the facts show that the
government is not interested in rejuvenating Ganga at all.
i. Funds allocated towards Namami Gange have been underutilised every
year since 2014-15, except two years (2020-21 and 2021-22).
ii. ii. 69% of the budgeted amount has been spent up to 2024-25. In 2015,
the Clean Ganga Fund was established, to enable the public, non-
resident Indians, corporates, and trusts to contribute towards Ganga
conservation efforts.
iii. iii. The PAC (2024) had observed that this fund was lying largely
unutilised. As of March 31, 2024, the Fund had Rs 876 crore. Of this, Rs
383 crores have been sanctioned for various projects.
Therefore, the government needs to put concerted efforts to reduce pollution in
water bodies like the Ganga and make cohesive plans along with adequate
budgetary allocations to implement such plans.
The allocations for Jal Shakti should also take into account certain disaster
management requirements.
i. Mechanisms and plans need to be devised to prevent landslides and
floods all over India.
ii. ii. Climate change is increasing the melting rate of Himalayan glaciers.
Floods caused by glacial outbursts release vast quantities of water in a
short time. The Standing Committee on Water Resources (2023) has
noted that there are many gaps in glacier monitoring and research. No
studies have been taken up to examine the loss of glacial volume in the
19.03.2025 1148
Himalayas, changes in glacial lakes over time or the impact of
atmospheric pollution on glaciers Accordingly, steps need to be taken to
mitigate such issues.

SHRIMATI SUPRIYA SULE (BARAMATI): In the grand narrative of governance,
numbers can often mask the realities they are meant to represent. Nowhere is this
more apparent than in the Union Government's repeated proclamations around the
Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) and rural irrigation schemes. Every year, the Ministry of
Jal Shakti's budget is presented with celebratory figures, citing near-universal tap
water coverage, capital expenditure hikes, and infrastructure creation. But for
states like Maharashtra, particularly in its drought-prone belts, these numbers
conceal a far more unsettling story-one of underutilization, non-functionality, and
quiet abandonment.
The 2025-26 budget, when examined closely, reveals serious cracks beneath the
surface. Two critical decisions stand out: the first is the persistent
underperformance and qualitative failings of the Jal Jeevan Mission in
Maharashtra; the second, more alarming, is the complete discontinuation of the
Special Package for Irrigation Projects meant to address agrarian distress in the
state's chronically drought-affected districts. Together, these reflect a policy
approach that prioritizes headline achievements over sustainable, long-term water
security for the most vulnerable.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1149
The Jal Jeevan Mission: Prioritising Quantity Over Quality
The Jal Jeevan has been hailed as a revolutionary step towards providing every
rural household with tap water connections, but its implementation in Maharashtra
as well as across the country raises uncomfortable questions.
In Maharashtra, the funds actually withdrawn under the Jal Jeevan Mission have
consistently remained well below even 50% of the budgetary allocation. In most
financial years, utilization has stagnated around 20-30%, indicating serious
bottlenecks in fund absorption and implementation capacity. This underutilization
raises fundamental questions about not just administrative efficiency but also the
feasibility of targets being showcased.
Moreover, the quality and functionality of these tap water connections is far from
satisfactory. A 2022 evaluation by the Ministry itself revealed that although most
rural households in Maharashtra had functional tap connections, only 62% received
adequate, regular, and safe water. In reality, many villages receive tap water barely
once a week, forcing residents to continue purchasing water from private sources.
This not only places a financial burden on rural families but also undermines the
core objective of universal, reliable water access.
Equally worrying is the issue of water quality and safety. The Ministry's own
National Report on Functionality Assessment 2022 highlights critical gaps in water
quality surveillance. Testing infrastructure remains inadequate, with several
districts lacking access to NABL-accredited laboratories. There is also no robust,
decentralized system to regularly test water at the community level. As a result,
contamination-whether from fluoride, arsenic, iron, or microbial sources-often goes
19.03.2025 1150
undetected for long periods.
In FY 2023-24, Maharashtra reported 9.73% chemical contamination and, even
more concerningly, biological contamination, nearly 1/3 of water samples, and over
17% of submitted samples were not tested at all. Rural households are, therefore,
exposed to the risk of serious health hazards, ranging from gastrointestinal
diseases to long-term developmental impacts.
Additionally, there is little effort to address water safety at the household level. The
Mission stops at the tap, ignoring the need for household-level filtration solutions,
storage hygiene, or public awareness campaigns. Across many villages,
dissatisfaction is palpable-residents report foul taste, odour, and discolouration of
water, and several communities have linked these lapses to recurring illnesses,
forcing them to revert to unsafe traditional water sources despite having tap
connections on paper.
The systemic flaw runs deeper. 74% of Jal Jeevan Mission connections rely on
groundwater sources, yet the budget lacks proportionate investment in
groundwater recharge, catchment protection, or community-led maintenance
efforts. Without ensuring sustainability, these schemes risk becoming yet another
case of infrastructure slippage-where connections exist on paper but cease to
function in reality.
These deficiencies are best illustrated by the case of a Jammu and Kashmir- based
IAS officer who, in July 2024, flagged irregularities of about Rs 690 crores in the
procurement of substandard pipes that were unsuitable for the hilly conditions of
the state. This was not merely a case of alleged financial misconduct-it illustrates
19.03.2025 1151
how compromised quality and lack of contextual planning can sabotage the long-
term viability of such essential infrastructure. If pipes cannot withstand the climate
of J&K, or the droughts of Maharashtra, then no amount of headline coverage can
compensate for non-functional systems on the ground.
If the Jal Jeevan Mission is to achieve its objectives in spirit, rather than merely on
paper, the government must strengthen water quality surveillance, ensuring all
districts have adequate accredited laboratories and decentralized testing. In the
long term, it must invest in sustainability-groundwater recharge, catchment
protection, and maintenance budgets-so that Jal Jeevan Mission does not become
yet another short-lived scheme.
Discontinuation of Special Package for Irrigation Projects in Drought-Prone
Areas of Maharashtra
This pattern of withdrawal and neglect extends beyond the Jal Jeevan Mission.
Another deeply concerning decision reflected in this year's Budget is the complete
discontinuation of the Special Package for Irrigation Projects for drought-prone
districts of Maharashtra. This package, introduced in 2016 to address chronic
agrarian distress in various parts of Maharashtra, had last year received ₹600
crore, of which only 2/3 was utilised. This year, its allocation stands at zero.
This is a matter of serious concern. Most of Maharashtra has not even come close
to overcoming water stress. Less than 20% of cultivable land in these regions is
under assured irrigation. Rain-fed agriculture continues to dominate, leaving
farmers highly vulnerable to monsoon variability and crop failures. Less than one
week ago, an award-winning farmer from one of the areas covered under this
19.03.2025 1152
scheme died of suicide after he was unable to support himself and his family due
to a lack of sustainable water supply to his farmland and his repeated appeals to
the government were ignored.
The decision to withdraw funding, without providing status updates or indicating
alternative financing mechanisms, puts the fate of thousands of ongoing irrigation
works in jeopardy. These projects were lifelines designed to give farmers some
respite from recurring drought and debt cycles and I urge the government to restore
this scheme to avoid worsening the crisis faced by the farmers of Maharashtra.


SHRI RAO RAJENDRA SINGH (JAIPUR RURAL): I would firstly like to express
our sincere commendation to the Ministry of Jal Shakti for the outstanding
achievements it has made under the able stewardship of Hon. PM Shri Narendra
Modi Ji. As my contribution to this deliberation, I seek to address to you a few
matters of immense public importance that pertain with the long-term policy goals
of the Ministry.
Kev Achievements in the Last 6 Years
First and foremost, let us acknowledge the remarkable strides that the Ministry of
Jal Shakti has made in its mission to secure water for all. In the last 11 years in the
domain of water resources, and the last 6 years of the existence of the Ministry,
under the visionary stewardship of Hon. PM Narendra Modi Ji, several landmark
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1160
initiatives have been launched to address the water crisis across the nation, such
as:
1. Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM): The Jal Jeevan Mission, which aims to provide
functional household tap connections to all rural households by 2024, has brought
clean drinking water to millions. As of now, millions of rural households, including
those in Rajasthan, have gained access to reliable tap water, which is a significant
milestone in improving the quality of life for our people.
2. Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM): Another revolutionary initiative, the Swachh
Bharat Mission, has focused on rural sanitation and the construction of household
toilets. This has not only contributed to cleaner villages but has also had a direct
impact on health, particularly in regions like Rajasthan where sanitation facilities
were once scarce.
3. National River Conservation Plan (NRCP): Efforts to clean and rejuvenate our
rivers have been a key achievement. The Ganga and Yamuna have received
significant attention, but the larger goal is to ensure all rivers, especially in the
desert regions like Rajasthan, are treated with care and conservation programs.
4. Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation (AMRUT): This
initiative, targeting urban areas, has helped in rejuvenating water supply systems,
sewerage networks, and green spaces, which are crucial for Rajasthan's growing
urban centers such as Jaipur and Udaipur.
5. Rural Water Supply Projects: The government has worked tirelessly to ensure
water availability in remote and rural regions, including Rajasthan, which is prone
to both droughts and water scarcity. Through schemes like the National Rural
19.03.2025 1161
Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP), the Ministry has supported states in
improving rural water supply infrastructure.
Jal Jeevan Mission: Policy in Mission Mode
Over the last ten years, 76% of the budget allocation to the Ministry has gone to
the Department of Drinking Water & Sanitation (DDWS), largely driven by
allocations to the Jal Jeevan Mission. At the start of Jal Jeevan Mission in August
2019, only 3.23 Crore (16.7%) rural households were reported to have tap water
connections. So far, as reported by States/ UTs as on 03.02.2025, under Jal
Jeevan Mission (JJM) - Har Ghar Jal, around 12.21 Crore additional rural
households have been provided with tap water connections. Thus, as on
03.02.2025, out of 19.36 Crore rural households in the country, more than 15.44
Crore (79.69%) households are reported to have tap water supply in their homes.
As reported by the state government of Rajasthan on JJM IMIS (Integrated
Management Information System), on 15th August 2019, only 11.68 lakh (11%)
rural households had access to tap water connections. Under JJM, 46.68 lakhs
additional rural households have been provided with tap water connections and
thus, as on 20.11.2024, the provision of tap water supply is available to more than
58.37 lakhs (54.40%) rural households in Rajasthan. Jal Jeevan Mission is a Prime
Example of the "Art of Implementation" that the Hon. PM has taught the world, by
implementing the fine print of legislative business, effectively, on ground. The
Government has recently extended the Jal Jeevan Mission until 2028 and that too
with an enhanced outlay of Rs. 67,000 Cr., I, along with the entire state of
Rajasthan would extend its gratitude to Hon. PM Shri Narendra Modi Ji for the
19.03.2025 1162
same. Hon. Finance Minister Smt. Nirmala Sitharaman stated that 15 crore
households representing 80 per cent of India's rural population have benefitted by
the Jal Jeevan Mission since 2019, which should be considered a tremendous
achievement and the entire state machinery must be congratulated for it.
Technological Innovations to further the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)
The use of technology has been critical to the success of JJM. IMIS (Integrated
Management Information System) was created as the single point of contact for all
data pertaining to the Jal Jeevan Mission. A JJM dashboard has also been
implemented to monitor all key parameters of the grievance regime and procedure.
Geolocation is used to monitor system progress at grade level. The Government
has also developed the Rashtriya Jan Jeevan Kosh (RJJK) portal for
individuals/organisations to make a donation for the provision of drinking water in
a village of their choice. Social networks and webinars (such as the Jal Jeevan
samvaad) are used for communication in connection with mass understanding.The
use of modern technology within the system is a realisation of the vision of Hon.
PM Narendra Modi's, Digital India.
Enhancing Community Involvement
Embodying the Gandhian spirit of the scheme, the project seeks to instill a sense
of ownership in its beneficiaries. This feature was missing from the previous 2009
National Rural Drinking Water Program and became a major reason for its failure.
The scheme provides for 10% of the total fund to be contributed by the members
of the village. Contributions may be payable in cash or kind (or work). This provision
has been maintained in all villages, except for hilly areas and northeastern states.
19.03.2025 1163
Not just common folk but even the respective Member of Parliaments (MPs) of
these districts have actively participated in the District Level District Development
Coordination & Monitoring Committee (DISHA) of the Ministry of Rural
Development and their participation has truly made the Jal Jeevan Mission a Jan
Andolan. The positive public perception of the mission has not only given it more
impetus but has been instrumental in inculcating a feeling of Nationalism and
Patriotism in the community.
Eastern Rajasthan Canal Project (ERCP) and River- Interlinking
Eastern Rajasthan Canal Project is an ambitious project of Rajasthan, which
envisages the transfer of surplus water of Kunnu, Kul, Parwati, Kalisindh, Mej river
sub-basin to Banas, Morel, Banganga, Kalisindh, Gambhir and Parbati river sub-
basin during Monsoon period. The Government of India formulated a National
Perspective Plan (NPP) of interlinking of rivers for transferring water from surplus
basins to deficit basin/areas in the year 1980.
The National Water Development Agency (NWDA) has identified 30 Inter- Linking
of Rivers (ILR) projects across the country, including 3 in the state of Rajasthan.
The Yamuna-Rajasthan link project, the Rajasthan-Sabarmati (RS) link project and
the Modified Parbati-Kalisindh Chambal (PKC) link project. Hon. Prime Minister
Narendra Modi in his address to the nation on 2nd October 2021 called the Jal
Jeevan Mission "A movement for decentralization". This is evident from the
mission's focus on empowering the village community for water resource
management in the village and water supply infrastructure. Very recently, in the
year 2024, a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) has been signed amongst the
19.03.2025 1164
States of Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh and the Ministry of Jal Shakti, Government
of India on 28.01.2024, for broad planning of the link project and preparation of its
Detailed Project Report. The Modified-PKC project is a prime example of how
various state governments can come together in order to further the interest of their
constituent population. This link project proposes to provide drinking and industrial
water in 13 districts of eastern Rajasthan, Malwa and Chambal regions of Madhya
Pradesh apart from providing irrigation in 2.8 lakh ha. area (or more) each in both
the states (total of 5.6 lakh ha or more) including supplementation of enroute tanks
in the states. The Modified PKC link project will help in utilizing the available water
resources of Chambal basin optimally and economically.
The implementation of the river-interlinking projects is touted to increase our
irrigation potential by 35 million hectares, and generate 34,000 megawatts of
hydropower. This house shall be delighted to note that according to the Economic
Survey 2024-25, the coverage of area under irrigation has increased between
FY16 and FY21 FROM 49.3% to 55.3% of the Gross Cropped Area (GCA), while
irrigation intensity has risen from 144.2% to 154.5%. The Economic Survey also
states that since the FY16 fiscal year, the government has been implementing the
Per Drop More Crop (PDMC) initiative, a component of the Pradhan Mantri Krishi
Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY), to promote water efficiency. From FY16 to FY25 (end
of Dec. 2024), ₹21968.75 crore was released to states for implementation of PDMC
Scheme and an area of 95.58 lakh ha has been covered under which is about
104.67 per cent higher as compared to the Pre-PDMC period.
19.03.2025 1165
Important issues concerning Rajasthan
While these achievements are commendable, we must not ignore the unique water
challenges that Rajasthan faces. As the driest state in India, Rajasthan is grappling
with an acute water scarcity issue. The situation is exacerbated by several factors,
including:
1. Droughts and Water Scarcity:
The Central Water Commission (CWC) estimates show that in 2021, 1,486
cubic metres of water was available per person in India. Annual per capita water
availability less than 1,700 cubic meters indicates a water-stressed condition.
The CWC has observed that while India is not a water-deficit country, severe
neglect and lack of water resource monitoring have led some regions, like
Barmer in Rajasthan, to experience persistent water stress. Rajasthan is highly
susceptible to droughts, which impact agriculture, livelihoods, and drinking
water availability. The per-capita water availability in Rajasthan is even lesser
and considered to be about 780 cubic metres. The state's dependence on
rainfall is highly unreliable, and thus, solutions for water conservation and
management are critical.
2. Over-exploitation of Groundwater
80% of India's rural drinking water, 50% of urban drinking water, and two- thirds
of water for irrigation comes from groundwater resources. The Standing
Committee on Water Resources (2023) has noted that there is a growing
dependence on ground water due to its decentralised availability, changes in
rainfall patterns, and increasing water demand. This has led to a severe
19.03.2025 1166
depletion of groundwater reserves. The total annual extractable groundwater in
India has been assessed as 406 billion cubic metres (BCM). As of 2024, the
annual groundwater extraction is 246 BCM. Average ground water extraction in
India is 60%. In 1995, this figure was 32%.
Due to a lack of surface water, Rajasthan heavily relies on groundwater.
Unfortunately, this over-exploitation has led to declining water tables, which is
a cause of concern for both drinking water and agricultural needs. States like
Rajasthan, Punjab and Haryana have a Groundwater Extraction Percentage of
over 140% today, which should raise way more alarm bells than it currently is.
Over exploitation of groundwater has become an even more grave issue in
states like Punjab, which does not suffer from a traditional scarcity of
groundwater resources. In states like Punjab, over extraction of groundwater
resources is directly proportional to the electricity subsidies provided to farmers
which removes any disincentives to conserve electricity. This leads to depletion
of state finances and water resources at the same time. This strategy, adopted
by a few states, deserves a hard reconsideration if we are to effectively deal
with the issue of water scarcity and ensure sustainable development for our
people.
3. Release of Rajasthan's Water Share according to agreement dated
31.12.1981
An agreement was signed amongst Hon'ble Chief Ministers of Rajasthan,
Punjab and Haryana on 31.12.1981 for distribution of surplus Ravi-Beas
waters. According to this agreement, Rajasthan's share was fixed as 8.60
19.03.2025 1167
MAF out of allocated 17.17 MAF surplus Ravi-Beas water. It was stipulated
in the agreement that:
"Until such time as Rajasthan is in a position to utilize its full share, Punjab
shall be free to utilize water surplus to Rajasthan's requirements. As
Rajasthan will soon be able to utilize its share, Punjab shall make adequate
alternative arrangements expeditiously for irrigation of its own lands by the
time Rajasthan is in a position to utilize its full share. As a result, it is expected
that during this transitional period when Rajasthan's requirements would not
exceed 8 MAF, 4.82 MAF of water should be available to Punjab in a mean
year when the availability is 17.17 MAF."
Due to extensive development of the Indira Gandhi canal system, Rajasthan
has been in a position since the past many years to utilize its entire share.
However, Bhakra Beas Management Board (BBMB) is giving Rajasthan an
allocation of 8.00 MAF. Rajasthan has repeatedly submitted its
representation to the Government of India and also to the BBMB and
Government of Punjab to restore its remaining share of 0.60 MAF. Punjab
has passed "Punjab Termination of Agreement Act, 2004" terminating and
discharging the Government of Punjab from its obligations under the
agreement dated 31.12.1981 and all other agreements relating to waters of
Ravi-Beas. Even after multiple requests from the Government of Rajasthan,
the state of Punjab refuses to give us our share of water, as decided in our
agreement dated 31.12.1981. A state like Rajasthan, which natively lacks
water resources, deserves its share of Water as agreed upon by both states
19.03.2025 1168
at one point of time.
4. Modification of Central Share Provided to the State of Rajasthan
underJJM:
The Government of India has kept the provision of funds to states under the
Jal Jeevan Missio in a ratio of 50:50 between Centre and State, as per
guidelines issued under the Mission. In this regard, I would like to draw the
attention of the house towards the uniqueness of the state of Rajasthan,
when compared with other States of the Country, as Rajasthan is not only
the largest State in terms of area but also has consistently ranked among
the states with lowest per-capita water availability.
About one third of quality affected villages/ dhanies of the country are
situated in Rajasthan. The villages/ dhanies in the State, especially in desert
areas, are located in scattered manner due to which the cost of drinking
water supply projects is very high in comparison to other States of the
country. There is also a critical problem of availability of water in 13 districts
of Eastern Rajasthan for which the project of interlinking of rivers would have
to be taken up for providing water at 55 liters per capita per day under Jal
Jeevan Mission, the cost of which would be very high. It is also mentioned
here that, Government of India was earlier providing central assistance in a
ratio of 90:10 under National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP) for
desert areas, until the year 2013, which was subsequently reduced to 60:40
and further reduced to 50:50. Therefore, the Government of India is
requested to consider the request for funding of not only the schemes of DDP
19.03.2025 1169
blocks but for all JJM schemes of Rajasthan in 90:10 ratio sympathetically,
to enable Rajasthan to move forward for expeditious implementation and
timely completion of Jal Jeevan Mission.
5. Quality of Water distributed under JJM in Rajasthan:
Despite significant progress in the supply of water, concerns around the
quality of water remain. Contaminants such as fluoride and arsenic,
especially in certain parts of Rajasthan, continue to affect public health. In
2024-25, 66 lakh samples were tested, of which 3.3 lakh (5%) lakh were
found contaminated. Remedial action was taken on 59% of the contaminated
samples. The Standing Committee recommended that states separate the
responsibilities of water supply and water quality monitoring. This would
increase trust, transparency, and accountability. This proposal by the
respective Standing Committee must be surely considered, especially for the
state of Rajasthan. According to the 'Annual Groundwater Quality Report
2024' released by the Central Ground Water Board (CGWB), Rajasthan had
the highest percentage of samples testing positive for the concentration of
Nitrate, Fluoride and Uranium, including being severely affected by high
Electrical Conductivity (EC) values. Barmer and Jodhpur in Rajasthan have
consistently shown a rising trend in Electrical Conductivity (EC) values. All
this data presents severely ominous signs for the state of Rajasthan. As we
strive towards our collective goal of 'Har Ghar Jal' we must also pay heed to
the quality of water that reaches the consumer.
19.03.2025 1170
Conclusion
In conclusion, while we must recognize the great strides made by the Ministry
of Jal Shakti in addressing water-related issues across India, it is equally
important that we focus on addressing the specific challenges of states like
Rajasthan. With the right blend of innovation, policy reform, and grassroots
participation, Rajasthan has the potential to overcome its water crisis and set
an example for sustainable water management for the entire nation.
As I put forth these demands, I would also like to express my confidence in
the visionary leadership of Hon. Prime Minister Shri. Narendra Modi Ji and
your able stewardship, in your ability to address all the demands of my
constituency and the people of India at large.
Thank you.

पािटल जी का (cid:323)दय से आभार (cid:255)कट करते ह(cid:242)ए जल शि(cid:265) म(cid:253)ं ालय क(cid:236) अनदु ान मांग(cid:332) का पूरजोर समथ(cid:170)न
करता ह(cid:243)ं । ध(cid:198)यवाद ।

DR. T. SUMATHY ALIAS THAMIZHACHI THANGAPANDIAN (CHENNAI
SOUTH): The Ministry of Jal Shakti, has been allocated Rs 99,503 crore in budget
2025-26. While this figure appears substantial, a closer examination reveals a
landscape riddled with inefficiencies, underachievement, and systemic issues that
demand immediate attention and reform.
The Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation (DDWS), is entrusted with
enormous financial responsibility.
The revised estimate for expenditure of DDWS is a staggering 48% lower than the
budget allocation, primarily due to reduced allocation to the Jal Jeevan Mission.
This glaring underutilization of funds raises serious questions about the
government's capacity to effectively implement its much hyped Jal Jeevan Mission.
In the much hyped Jal Jeevan Mission, there are two schemes
1. Ground Water Based Schemes,
2. Surface Water Based Schemes
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1197
Both of them are categorised into three types; 1. PWS, 2.H.P/ Borewell/Tube Well
& Other spot sources and others.
As far as Tamil Nadu under the Ground water Based Schemes, through PWS
94,408 habitants are covered. Through H.P/Borewell/Tubewell & other spot
sources. This is awfully inadequate. Fortunately for Tamil Nadu inspite of hardships
to supply water, the Dravidian model rule has achieved many years ago what the
union government is targetting to achieve by 2030.
The Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM), launched in 2019 with the ambitious goal of
providing assured tap water supply to all 19 crore rural households by 2024, has
failed to meet its target and the scheme has now been extended to 2028. This
shortfall is not merely a statistical lapse; it is a failure of governance that directly
impacts the lives of millions.
Even more concerning is the functionality assessment report, which reveals that
only 62% of households have fully functional tap connections. Full functionality
implies not just the presence of a tap, but also a regular supply of water in adequate
quantities and of acceptable quality.
Shockingly, in many states more than 40% of households receive water that does
not meet quality standards. In 2024-25, 5% of the 66 lakh samples tested were
found to be contaminated, and remedial action was taken on only 59% of these.
Also, in 2024-25, only 31% of the central share allocated has been released.
Budget allocations for JJM have oscillated wildly. For the fiscal year 2025-26, Rs
67,000 crore has been earmarked-a figure that, on paper, projects confidence and
ambition. Yet, when contrasted with the revised expenditure estimates of the
19.03.2025 1198
previous year, which hovered around a dismal Rs 22,694 crore, the discrepancy is
glaring. Such a vast difference between budgetary intent and actual spending
speaks volumes about the inefficiencies that plague project implementation. The
government's inability to translate funds into tangible results has created an
environment where financial pledges are rendered impotent by procedural delays,
administrative apathy, and a lack of genuine commitment to reform.
While the budget estimate for 2025-26 is nearly double that of 2024-25's revised
estimate, the actual expenditure in previous years has been shockingly low. Such
inconsistencies suggest that the allocation of funds is less a reflection of on-the-
ground needs and more a symbolic gesture-an attempt to present a façade of
progress while the underlying issues remain unresolved.
The most important natural wealth of our country is the large number of rivers
flowing across different states. Most of the Rivers, particularly the peninsular rivers
are flooded during the heavy wet monsoons and dry during the other seasons due
to scarce rainfall in the catchment areas of these rivers. Hence the Farmers are in
the clutches of either a severe flood or drought. This is a very bad situation for
farmers of this country. It not only affects their crops but also make them suffer
most to repay their loans resulting in the suicides of farmers in the country.
The Nationalization of rivers and inter-linking of the rivers are very important as
they not only solve the inter-state water sharing disputes among the Indian States
but is also the only way through which adequate water for irrigation in various states
could be ensured and the problems faced by the farmers due to lack of water and
drought could be solved permanently. Also the government should consider
19.03.2025 1199
creating a comprehensive National Policy on Indian rivers and National river water
Grid to enhance the process of inter-linking of rivers flowing in various states. In
this way the government can harness the excess water in the rivers cleverly and
judiciously to benefit drought hit parts of the country.
Water is indispensable for Agriculture. Irrigation is the blood circulation of
Agriculture. Without water nothing could be done, without adequate water even if
we spend crores of rupees, without water, farmers have no chance of flourish. The
River Cauvery is the lifeline of Tamil Nadu, particularly the millions of farmers in
the delta regions in Tamil Nadu. But unfortunately year after year Tamil Nadu
farmers are betrayed due to the non release of adequate water from river Cauvery.
The unconcerned attitude of the higher riparian State of Karnataka has killed
hundreds of farmers in Tamil Nadu and has driven several thousand out of the
agriculture. The total loss due to the non release of Cauvery water for crop seasons
in Tamil Nadu has taken heavy toll on Agriculture and farmers of Tamil Nadu.
The Union Government should urge the Cauvery Management Board to direct the
Karnataka state to release rightful share of water from Cauvery river in accordance
with the award of the Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal (CWDT) in monthly
deliveries to ensure adequate release of Cauvery water from Karnataka for
irrigation in Tamil Nadu.
The Union Government has to allocate more funds for the proper maintenance and
use of existing dams and water reservoirs in the country. The deepening and de-
silting of local water bodies and the lakes is crucial for growth of agriculture. The
Union Government has to allocate more funds for the river restoration projects in
19.03.2025 1200
the country and also to increase and enhance the water catchment capacity in the
country. The development of social forestry and increase of green cover in the
country is also part of the agro development activity only.
Tamil Nadu always perform better when it comes to people and reserved the best
for adverse conditions. The Hogenakkal Integrated Drinking Water project and the
Athikadavu Avinashi project are standing testimonial to the commitment of the DMK
government headed by Hon'ble Chief Minister Thalapathi M.K. Stalin in providing
water to underprivileged people living in dry regions. The Veeranam Water Supply
scheme, The Palar water Supply scheme cater the water needs of the people of
Greater Chennai region and adjoining districts of Thiruvallur, Chengalpattu and
Kanchipuram are also the brain child of Muthamilarignar Dr Kalaignar. The
southern Districts of Ramanathapuram, Thoothukudi, Virudhunagar too benefitted
through the far sighted water management of the Government of Tamil Nadu.
Water Resources is the most indispensable thing in the development of
civilizations. History teach us about the River valley Civilisations that thrived on the
banks of the perennial rivers.
North Indian States are blessed with perennial rivers from the Himalayas and South
Indian States depend on rain fed rivers. In this situation to provide equitable
distribution of water for both drinking and irrigation purposes requires a meticulous
planning, bold decisions and impeccable execution.
Unfortunately the Modi BJP government in the last 10 years prioritize ambition over
accountability, spectacle over sustainable progress and political gains over
productivity. This government neither addresses the perils due to spate of
19.03.2025 1201
Himalayan rivers and penury due peninsular rivers.
Water is one of the basic gifts of nature to mankind. There is no life without water.
The origin of life began in Water. Around 1 in 4 people lacked safely managed
drinking water in their homes and nearly half the world's population lacked safely
managed sanitation. COVID-19 has highlighted the urgent need to ensure
everyone can access good hand hygiene. We need to learn our lessons from such
dreaded pandemic and be prepared for the future pandemic and calamities.
Billions of people will lack access to safe water, sanitation and hygiene in 2030
unless progress quadruples.
By 2030, only 70 per cent of the country's population will have access to safe
drinking water at home, leaving millions without; Only 60 per cent will have safe
sanitation services.
As per the latest reports, vast inequalities with vulnerable children and families
suffering the most due to lack of access to safe water and sanitation. To achieve
universal access to safely managed drinking water by 2030, the current rate of
progress in the Least Developed States in India would need to increase ten-fold. In
fragile contexts, where people were twice as likely to lack safe drinking water, it
would need to accelerate twenty times more.
Ancient Tamil literature provides fascinating insights into water. management
practices and the cultural significance of water in Tamil Nadu.
Thousands of years ago, Tamils possessed remarkable knowledge of water
management. Kings built tanks and lakes to store water, some of which still exist
today.
19.03.2025 1202
Water was considered sacred, and its judicious use was emphasized. Ancient
Tamil literature mentions 47 water bodies, reflecting the prescient wisdom of Tamils
in harvesting water.
Different terms were used to describe various types of water: Munneer: Refers to
the sea, formed by water from rains, rivers, and springs. It symbolized antiquity and
purity.
Nanneer: Denoted good water from rivers.
Inneer: Referred to sweet water suitable for drinking.
The Sangam era celebrated water through the festival of "Munneer Vizhavu,"
recognizing its role in soil creation, nourishment, and erosion.
Sangam literature believed that a person's character was shaped by water and soil.
Life revolved around water, and villages were built around water bodies. Ancient
Rituals from birth to death are centred on water. Houses were constructed around
wells, emphasizing water's importance.
Tamil literature highlights the deep connection between water, culture, and
sustainable practices. While challenges persist today, learning from ancient
wisdom can guide better water management in the future.
Thank you.
