DR. BYREDDY SHABARI (NANDYAL): At the very outset, I would like to
commend Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi ji and Jal Shakti Minister Shri C.R.
Patil ji for their unwavering commitment to India's water security. The historic
allocation of Rs. 99,503 crore for the Jal Shakti Ministry in this year's budget is a
testament to their vision and dedication. This is not just a budgetary provision-it is
an investment in the future of our country, in ensuring that no village, no town, and
no citizen is left behind when it comes to access to clean and sustainable water
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1206
resources.
Over the past ten years, under PM Modi ji's leadership, we have seen a paradigm
shift in water management. When we speak of transformative governance, Jal
Jeevan Mission stands out as a shining example. From just a handful of homes
having tap connections in 2019, today, 14 crore households across India have
access to functional tap water connections. This is not just an achievement in
numbers but a revolution in improving public health, women's empowerment, and
rural development. Atal Bhujal Yojana has been another landmark initiative,
empowering local communities to manage groundwater resources and ensuring
sustainable water use. The Namami Gange Mission has rejuvenated our sacred
river Ganga, making it one of the most successful river rejuvenation programs in
the world. And beyond these, the government's focus on interlinking rivers,
increasing irrigation efficiency, and holistic watershed management has brought a
new era of water security in India.
Sir, while these national initiatives have transformed lives across the country,
Andhra Pradesh is at a critical juncture in its water management journey. Our state
is home to Polavaram, a national project that is crucial for irrigation, drinking water,
and flood control. However, it is also a project that was neglected and stalled under
the previous state government. Today, I am proud to say that under our new Chief
Minister's decisive leadership, we have put Polavaram back on track. A crucial
sanction of Rs. 12,127 crore has been secured from the Central Government for
Phase 1, ensuring that this project-the lifeline of Andhra Pradesh-finally moves
forward with full momentum.
19.03.2025 1207
But Polavaram alone is not enough. Andhra Pradesh has the potential to become
a model state for sustainable water use, provided we receive the right support.
Unfortunately, the previous government failed to utilize even 13% of the ₹16,855
crore allocated to the state under the Jal Jeevan Mission. This was not just
inefficiency-it was a betrayal of the people's trust. Now, with new Detailed Project
Reports (DPRs) prepared, we are committed to fixing these past mistakes. We
seek an enhanced allocation of ₹70,000 crore under Jal Jeevan Mission, and I urge
the Hon'ble Minister to approve this at the earliest, so that Andhra Pradesh can
achieve 100% tap water coverage and ensure clean drinking water for every
household.
I come from Rayalaseema, a region that has long struggled with droughts and
water scarcity. Despite its severe groundwater depletion, Andhra Pradesh has not
been included in the Atal Bhujal Yojana. I urge the Hon'ble Minister to rectify this
and include Andhra Pradesh in the scheme, so that Rayalaseema and other water-
stressed districts can receive critical funding and technical support to replenish
groundwater and secure their future.
While the Jal Jeevan Mission is ensuring tap water, and Atal Bhujal Yojana is
working on groundwater sustainability, our Chief Minister has a bold vision for intra-
state river interlinking. The Polavaram-Banakacherla river interlinking project is a
game-changer for Andhra Pradesh. It aims to transfer surplus Godavari water to
the Penna River via Krishna, ensuring equitable distribution of water across the
state. By constructing key reservoirs at Bollapalli in Guntur and Banakacherla in
Nandyal, this project will provide drinking water to 80 lakh people and irrigate 7.5
19.03.2025 1208
lakh acres of farmland. Every year, nearly 2,000 TMC of monsoon water from the
Godavari flows into the Bay of Bengal, unused. Instead of letting it go to waste, we
must channel it to the regions that need it the most. But such an ambitious project
requires a massive investment of ₹80,112 crore, and I urge the Central Government
to provide financial support to make this vision a reality.
I conclude by once again expressing my gratitude to Prime Minister Shri Narendra
Modi ji and Jal Shakti Minister Shri C.R. Patil ji for their visionary leadership and
unwavering commitment to water security. Under their guidance, India is moving
towards a future where no citizen is deprived of clean drinking water. It is now our
responsibility to ensure that hese ambitious initiatives reach the last mile and truly
transform lives. I look forward to the continued support of the Central Government
in strengthening water security in Andhra Pradesh, particularly for Rayalaseema
and other drought-prone regions, ensuring that every drop counts, every resource
is utilized, and every citizen benefits.
Thank you!

SHRI CHAMALA KIRAN KUMAR REDDY (BHONGIR): I am expressing my
views on THE DEMANDS FOR GRANTS FOR THE YEAR 2025-26 FOR THE
MINISTRY OF JAL SHAKTI. On this occasion, I also want to thank our beloved
Leader Smt. Sonia Gandhi Ji and our Leader of Opposition, Shri Rahul Gandhi Ji
for giving me this opportunity to raise various issues.
With regard to Telangana State, I would like to raise some pending issues. Several
representations in this regard have been submitted from time to time by our Hon'ble
Chief Minister, Shri Anumula Revanth Reddy Garu to take necessary action in the
matter which I am going to mention hereunder:-
First and foremost issue is - A letter has been represented to the Hon'ble Prime
Minister of India by the Hon'ble Chief Minister of Telangana Vide Letter Ref.
No.57/PSCM/2023 dt. 26.12.2023 - Requesting to declare Palamuru- Rangareddy
Lift Irrigation Scheme as a National Project. The scheme is an important Irrigation
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1215
Project for Telangana as it envisages supplying water to drought prone areas of
the State and more than 12 lakh acres besides providing water for drinking and
industrial areas.
In this regard, Hydrology, ISM, I.P., Cost Clearance are yet to be cleared from
CWC.
Second issue is - Providing drinking water facilities to rural households through
Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTCs) under Jal Jeevan Mission for
Financial Year- 2024-25 in the State of Telangana.
Third issue is - There is need to Link River Godavari from Mallanna Sagar reservoir
with Musi river to provide 5 TMC water to clean river Musi and supply of 15 TMC
drinking water to Hyderabad Urban Agglomeration (HUA). In this regard, a proposal
was submitted to Jal Shakti Ministry to facilitate the linking of Godavari with Musi
to clean river Musi in Telangana State.
Fourth issue is - A proposal was sent to the Ministry of Jal Shakti by our Telangana
Government to kindly consider Drinking water project of Godavari Phase-2 either
under AMRUT 2.0 or a special project and also requested to sanction Rs.7,400
Crores as financial assistance to take up these projects.
With these few words, I would like to conclude my speech.

Pradesh, Sri Nara Chandrababu Naidu Garu, I would like to appreciate and applaud
the vision of our Prime Minister, Sri Narendra Modi ji, Finance Minister, Smt.
Nirmala Sitharaman ji, and Jal Shakti Minister, Sri Gajendra Singh Shekhawat ji,
for their unwavering commitment to ensuring water security and sustainable water
management across the nation. Under the leadership of the NDA government,
India's water sector has seen unprecedented progress, with transformational
initiatives such as Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM), which has significantly improved rural
water supply by providing tap connections to millions of households; Atal Bhujal
Yojana, which has strengthened groundwater management in critical and over-
exploited regions; Namami Gange, which has revitalized and rejuvenated India's
sacred rivers; and Mission Amrit Sarovar, which has focused on water conservation
and the revival of traditional water bodies. However, despite these commendable
efforts, several critical challenges remain in Andhra Pradesh, particularly in the
Konaseema district and the broader Godavari basin, requiring urgent attention and
adequate budgetary allocations.
I am highlighting the critical gaps in funding and implementation of water resource
management in Andhra Pradesh, with a special focus on the Konaseema region.
Despite the ambitious targets set under Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) and other water-
related schemes, the state continues to struggle with safe drinking water
accessibility, groundwater sustainability, and flood management.
While Jal Jeevan Mission aims to provide functional household tap connections to
all rural households, Andhra Pradesh has lagged behind the national average in its
implementation. The recent budget allocations reveal a concerning reduction in
19.03.2025 1217
expenditure under JJM, raising doubts about the timely completion of projects in
the state. The Committee on Water Resources has already noted that Andhra
Pradesh, along with a few other states, may not achieve full coverage before 2027,
extending well beyond the initial 2024 deadline. Moreover, the state's groundwater
quality is severely compromised. The Annual Groundwater Quality Report
highlights that 23.5% of samples exceed permissible nitrate levels, 27.68% of
groundwater is unsuitable for irrigation due to high residual sodium carbonate, and
23 districts report salinity beyond permissible limits. Yet, the state currently lacks
mobile water testing laboratories to effectively monitor and address these
concerns. This gap leaves communities vulnerable to unsafe drinking water and
further delays necessary interventions.
In the Konaseema region, the Godavari delta faces unique and urgent water-
related issues. The district is highly prone to flooding, which disrupts access to
clean drinking water and damages existing infrastructure. Furthermore, rapid
groundwater depletion due to unscrupulous extraction and illegal tapping of supply
pipelines has exacerbated the water crisis. Several peripheral mandals in the
constituency still lack functional tap water connections, despite budgetary
allocations under JJM.
A key concern in Konaseema is the increasing water pollution from aquaculture
farms and untreated waste discharge. Over the past two decades, the region has
grappled with the discharge of untreated waste into irrigation canals. This
malpractice has led to contaminated water sources, adversely affecting both
agriculture and public health. Furthermore, brackish water discharge from
19.03.2025 1218
aquaculture farms into agricultural canals has exacerbated the situation,
contaminating freshwater supplies and threatening paddy cultivation and soil
health, thereby jeopardizing the livelihoods of local farmers. This issue needs
immediate intervention through stricter regulations and improved wastewater
treatment mechanisms.
Additionally, the recurring floods in the Godavari basin pose a severe risk to
Konaseema, leading to displacement, infrastructure damage, and water
contamination. The government must prioritize flood management programs
through measures such as strengthening embankments and modernizing drainage
systems to prevent overflow and waterlogging, implementing early warning
systems to enhance disaster preparedness, and increasing investments in flood
prevention infrastructure, particularly in Konaseema, to mitigate the adverse impact
of seasonal floods on water quality and availability.
To address these pressing concerns, I urge the Ministry of Jal Shakti to take the
following immediate steps: establish mobile water testing laboratories for real-time
monitoring of drinking water quality and groundwater contamination; set up an
Electric Defluoridation Plant to combat fluoride contamination in 17 affected
districts; strengthen JJM implementation by ensuring full tap water coverage in all
mandals, with a special focus on flood-prone and remote areas of Konaseema;
regulate industrial and aquaculture waste by preventing direct discharge of
pollutants into irrigation canals and implementing sustainable waste treatment
solutions; enhance flood management infrastructure by improving embankments,
drainage systems, and early warning mechanisms to mitigate flood damage and
19.03.2025 1219
water supply disruptions; and develop a comprehensive Godavari Basin Water
Management Plan to ensure flood control, sustainable water utilization, and long-
term water security for vulnerable districts like Konaseema.
Furthermore, coordination between the Atal Bhujal Yojana and Jal Jeevan Mission
in Konaseema is essential for tackling water stress and ensuring long-term
sustainability in groundwater management. A well-integrated approach between
these flagship schemes will enhance groundwater recharge, prevent depletion, and
secure drinking water sources for future generations.
Honourable Speaker, while India is moving towards achieving Har Ghar Jal, it is
imperative that the Ministry of Jal Shakti ensures targeted interventions in
vulnerable districts like Konaseema. I urge the Honourable Minister to allocate
adequate resources, expedite project implementation, and adopt a more integrated
approach to ensure a resilient and sustainable water future for Andhra Pradesh.
Thank You.

आ पाता है ।

क(cid:236) (cid:211)यव(cid:214)था क(cid:236) जा सकती है ।

4. जल जीवन िमशन के तहत हर घर नल कने(cid:179)शन म(cid:164) सुधारः
महोदय, म(cid:167) आपके मा(cid:197)यम से माननीय म(cid:253)ं ी जी का (cid:197)यान जल जीवन िमशन के तहत धनबाद
संसदीय (cid:177)े(cid:253) के अंतग(cid:170)त हर घर नल कन(cid:179)े शन क(cid:236) ि(cid:214)थित पर (cid:197)यान आकिष(cid:170)त करना चाहता ह(cid:243) ँ । वत(cid:170)मान

और दबाव म(cid:164) िनरंतर कमी देखी जा रही है ।

आपूित(cid:170) नह(cid:233) हो रही है । इस ि(cid:214)थित के कारण (cid:214)थानीय िनवािसय(cid:332) को गंभीर किठनाइय(cid:332) का सामना
करना पड़ रहा है. और उ(cid:198)ह(cid:164) पानी क(cid:236) भारी कमी का सामना करना पड़ रहा है । जल जीवन िमशन का
उ(cid:293)े(cid:212)य हर घर को गणु व(cid:176)ापूण(cid:170) जल आपूित(cid:170) सिु नि(cid:306)त करना है, इसिलए म(cid:167) आपसे िनवेदन करता ह(cid:243) ँ िक
इस म(cid:293)ु े क(cid:236) गंभीरता को देखते ह(cid:242)ए शी(cid:325) एक िव(cid:214)तृत िनरी(cid:177)ण िकया जाए तािक जल आपूित(cid:170) म(cid:164) सुधार
िकया जा सके और (cid:214)थानीय जनता को पया(cid:170)(cid:302) जल िमल सके ।
5. पय(cid:170)टन (cid:214)थल के (cid:322)प म(cid:164) मैथन, पंचेत और तोपचांची का िवकासः

 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1224

SUSHRI PRANITI SUSHILKUMAR SHINDE (SOLAPUR): The underutilization of
funds and inefficiencies in the implementation of the schemes of the Ministry of Jal
Shakti have been constant issues. The revised estimate for 2024-25 was 48%
lower than the budgeted allocation. This was mainly due to reduced allocation to
the Jal Jeevan Mission (JM).
Regarding JJM, the scheme's targets have not been met, leading to an extension
of the deadline to 2028. As of January 2025, 15 crore households have been
covered under JJM, with many households still remaining. The Standing
Committee on Water Resources (2024) observed that under the JJM, certain
surveys were carried out without physically visiting households. This resulted in
inaccurate data reporting and the exclusion of numerous households.
According to a 2022 functionality assessment of JJM, only 62% rural households
had fully functional tap connections (ie. working taps with adequate and regular
water supply). Also, the quality of water remains a pressing issue. The Pey Jal
Survekshan, last year, highlighted that only 46 cities out of the 485 AMRUT cities
had good water quality.
Some of the issues affecting Solapur-
 Solapur has been facing severe water scarcity, affecting both rural and urban
populations. A semi-arid climate, irregular rainfall, and over extraction of
groundwater have worsened the crisis. The Standing Committee on Water
Resources also raised concern about groundwater depletion.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1225
 There is a need for timely maintenance and repair of water bodies in the
constituency, including at Huljanti, Mangalwedha. Regular desilting,
cleaning, and reinforcement of embankments can help maintain water quality
and storage capacity of these bodies. Additionally, the government must
implement robust monitoring mechanisms to identify potential risks early and
allocate sufficient resources for repairs when needed.
 The government should consider removing the Rs.20 lakh cap on public
works under the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee
Act (MGNREGA) to enhance the scope and effectiveness of developmental
activities, particularly in the area of water conservation and maintenance of
water bodies.
 A significant portion of the public works carried out under MGNREGA
focuses on improving water infrastructure, such as desilting ponds,
constructing check dams,strengthening embankments, and restoring
traditional water sources. These activities are crucial for ensuring water
security, improving agricultural productivity, and supporting livelihoods in
rural areas. However, the current financial cap of Rs.20 lakh severely limits
the scale and impact of such projects.
 The Maharashtra Industrial Development Corporation (MIDC) facility in
Solapur constituency has been associated with the release of untreated
industrial effluents, leading to significant environmental and public health
concerns.
19.03.2025 1226
The effects linked with the discharge of such untreated effluents are highly
detrimental to the local water bodies, ultimately posing a significant threat to
the local environment and people. The underground water has been polluted
because of this. In agriculture, contaminated water used for irrigation has led
to the potential uptake of toxins into the food chain. Furthermore, soil quality
degradation due to the accumulation of pollutants reduces agricultural
productivity.
The persistent issue of untreated effluent discharge by MIDC highlights an
urgent need for the government to enforce stricter environmental regulations
and mandate the implementation of effective effluent treatment solutions. It
is imperative for authorities to act decisively to ensure that industrial activities
do not jeopardize the health and well-being of local communities or harm the
environment.
 Given the various challenges faced in water conservation, and maintenance
of water bodies, it is concerning that there has been persistent
underspending in the Ministry's schemes. In the financial year 2024-25, only
31% of the allocated central share under JJM had been released as of
February 2025. This underutilization of funds hampers the effective
implementation of crucial projects, delaying essential maintenance work and
limiting efforts to address water scarcity and rural development.

SHRI ANUP SANJAY DHOTRE (AKOLA): I express my views on Demand for
Grants for the Jal Shakti Ministry.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1233
As we gather here today, our nation faces significant challenges in ensuring access
to clean water and sanitation for all citizens. The Jal Shakti Ministry plays a vital
role in addressing these challenges, and it is our responsibility to provide the
necessary resources to support its efforts.
The Demand for Grants for the Jal Shakti Ministry for the year 2024-25 focuses on
several key areas, including:
Jal Jeevan Mission: Providing clean drinking water to every household in rural
areas.
Swachh Bharat Mission: Ensuring sanitation and hygiene in rural and urban areas.
Water Conservation: Promoting efficient use of water resources and reducing
waste.
These initiatives require significant investment, and I urge the government to
allocate sufficient funds to support the ministry's efforts. I request that the Demand
for Grants be approved, enabling the Jal Shakti Ministry to continue its vital work.
As we consider this Demand for Grants, I would like to emphasize the importance
of transparency and accountability in the allocation and utilization of funds. As
members of Parliament, it is our duty to ensure that the funds allocated are used
efficiently and effectively to achieve the intended objectives.
For Akola, Maharashtra, I propose the following key initiatives:
 We must ensure effective implementation of the Jal Jeevan Mission,
providing functional household tap connections to every rural household in
Akola. This will significantly improve access to clean drinking water for our
19.03.2025 1234
citizens.
 To address water scarcity issues in Akola, we must implement water
conservation measures, such as rainwater harvesting and watershed
development. This will help reduce our reliance on groundwater and promote
sustainable water management practices.
 Restoring and renovating traditional water bodies, such as ponds and lakes,
is crucial to increase water storage capacity and recharge groundwater
levels. This will not only improve water availability but also support
biodiversity and ecosystem services.
 Improving drinking water quality in Akola is essential, and we must establish
water testing laboratories and implement measures to reduce contamination.
This will ensure that our citizens have access to safe and clean drinking
water.
 Developing sanitation and hygiene infrastructure, including individual
household toilets and community toilets, is critical to achieving Open
Defecation Free status in Akola. We must prioritize this initiative to improve
public health and hygiene.
 Flood control and management measures, such as river embankments and
flood protection walls, are necessary to protect Akola from frequent floods.
We must invest in these measures to safeguard our citizens and their
properties.
 To address declining groundwater levels, we must implement groundwater
19.03.2025 1235
recharge and management measures, such as artificial recharge structures
and groundwater monitoring systems.
 Implementing watershed development and soil conservation measures,
such as contour bunding and terracing, will reduce soil erosion and improve
water retention.
 Providing capacity building and training programs for stakeholders,
including government officials, gram panchayats, and community members,
is essential to ensure effective implementation of Jal Shakti schemes.
 Finally, we must establish a robust monitoring and evaluation mechanism to
track progress, identify gaps, and make necessary adjustments to ensure
successful implementation of Jal Shakti schemes in Akola.
 I urge the government to allocate sufficient funds to support these initiatives
and ensure that the Jal Shakti Ministry has the necessary resources to
address the water and sanitation challenges facing Akola.
I conclude by urging my fellow members to support the Demand for Grants for the
Jal Shakti Ministry, and I request the government to provide the necessary
resources to support the ministry's efforts to ensure access to clean water and
sanitation for all citizens.

SHRI YADUVEER WADIYAR (MYSORE): Thank you very much for allowing me
to express my views on a very important matter regarding the Demand for Grants
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1236
for the Ministry of Jal Shakti. The Ministry of Jal Shakti and its demand for grants
is amongst the most important matters when allocating of Budget, as it deals
directly with the fundamental need of every human of access to clean and pure
Wafer, furthermore, the Ministry also deals with the important matter of ensuring
that our rivers and other sources of water, are also kept clean and conserved in a
manner befitting the aspirations of modern India.
Under the Modi led governments, Jal Shakti has received a tremendous boost,
especially, with innovative policy framework such as the Jal Jeevan Mission.
Additionally, the impetus given to cleaning of our rivers and ensuring that clean
drinking water is available to all is a particularly laudable initiative of the Modi led

Given the need to conserve time, I would like to focus on the needs and priorities
of our state, Karnataka. As we all know, water is not just a basic necessity, it is the
lifeblood of our agricultural, industrial, and social ecosystems. Karnataka, with its
vast agricultural landscape, rapidly urbanizing cities, and diverse climatic
conditions, faces unique challenges and opportunities in water management. It is
imperative that we address these challenges through strategic planning and
targeted investments.
Addressing the Water Crisis in Karnataka has been facing recurring
droughts, fluctuating rainfall patterns, and depleting groundwater levels. The
districts of northern Karnataka, in particular, are grappling with severe water
scarcity. These regions, which are primarily dependent on rain-fed agriculture,
suffer from unpredictable monsoons and erratic rainfall, affecting the livelihood of
19.03.2025 1237
millions. The demand for grants for 2025-26 seeks to prioritize the revival of
irrigation systems, the construction of new reservoirs, and the recharging of
groundwater tables in these vulnerable areas. We must urgently focus on
augmenting the capacity of water bodies through integrated watershed
management practices, ensuring that our farmers, who are the backbone of our
state, have access to reliable irrigation and water for drinking. The Atal Bhujal
Yojana in particular requires urgent reviewing as the state has not fully utilized the
opportunities available in this scheme to revive and conserve our groundwater.
Support for the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) Karnataka has made significant
progress in the implementation of the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM), aiming to provide
tap water to every rural household. However, there remains a gap in certain regions
where accessibility and infrastructure are still lacking. The demand for grants in
2025-26 includes critical support to bridge these gaps by expanding water
distribution networks and providing adequate drinking water infrastructure in
underserved areas, especially in tribal and backward regions of Karnataka. This
will especially benefit my constituency, as Kodagu being a very hilly terrain has
faced issues in the past of incomplete connections and delays in infrastructure
related to the Jal Jeevan Mission.
I would also like to add that strengthening community participation in the
management of water resources, empowering local bodies to take ownership of
water supply systems, and ensuring that water quality is regularly monitored will
ensure that the community is an active stakeholder. This will not only enhance
secess to water but also ensure its sustainability in the long term.
19.03.2025 1238
Water Quality and Pollution Control Water quality is an issue that requires
our urgent attention. In Karnataka, rivers such as the Krishna, Cauvery, and
Tungabhadra are crucial for both drinking water and agriculture. However,
industrial and domestic waste, as well as the over-extraction of water, have led to
severe degradation in water quality. Over 17 rivers have overly polluted stretches,
including the 1 akshman Tirtha, which flows in my constituency. The demand for
grants includes substantial support for the Namami Cauvery Programme and other
river rejuvenation projects, aimed at cleaning and restoring these vital water
sources, which will be a big boost. It is a matter of pride that the Cauvery originates
in my constituency and this policy will ensure that the river continues to be the life-
force for our people. A dedicated effort is required to improve wastewater treatment
infrastructure will ensure that our rivers, lakes, and groundwater remain clean and
safe for consumption.
Harnessing Water from Rainfall: Rainwater Harvesting and Storage
Given Karnataka's reliance on the monsoon and the challenges posed by
fluctuating rainfall, rainwater harvesting remains a vital component of our strategy
for water conservation. The 2025-26 demand for grants will focus on enhancing
state-wide efforts for rainwater harvesting infrastructure, particularly in urban areas
like Bengaluru, which faces increasing pressure on its water resources due to rapid
population growth.
Promoting Sustainable Water Use in Agriculture, Agriculture in Karnataka,
especially in the drought-prone regions, is highly dependent on water availability.
To ensure the long-term sustainability of farming practices, the demand for grants
19.03.2025 1239
emphasizes investment in micro-irrigation systems like drip and sprinkler irrigation,
which can drastically improve water use efficiency. Karnataka has already seen
positive results from schemes like the Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana
(PMKSY), and continued funding for such programs is essential for extending
irrigation to small and marginal farmers.
Urban Water Supply and Distribution Karnataka's urban centers, particularly
Bengaluru, and sometimes even my hometown of Mysore, face water supply
challenges due to a rapidly growing population, industrialization, and urbanization.
The demand for grants will include enhanced funding for urban water supply
systems, ensuring that cities across Karnataka have the necessary infrastructure
to meet their water needs. This includes improving wastewater treatment facilities,
recycling of water, and better management of urban water distribution networks.
Incentivizing Water-Efficient Technologies and Public Awareness The future of
water security in Karnataka lies not just in infrastructure but also in the adoption of
innovative technologies and the promotion of public awareness about water
conservation. The demand for grants for 2025-26 seeks to support the promotion
of water-efficient technologies and encourage the private sector, farmers, and local
communities to adopt sustainable water use practices. Awareness campaigns will
be essential in educating citizens on the importance of water conservation,
especially in urban areas where water consumption per capita is higher. Honorable
Speaker and esteemed colleagues in conclusion, Karnataka is at a critical juncture
in its water management journey. The demand for grants in the Ministry of Jal
Shakti for 2025-26 is not just about addressing the immediate water needs of our
19.03.2025 1240
state but also about ensuring that future generations inherit a sustainable and
resilient water supply. The investments we make today will determine whether
Karnataka remains a thriving agricultural powerhouse and an economically vibrant
state.
Thank you.

Sir, Water can create peace or spark conflict. History will teach us many lessons

 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1241
More than 3 billion people worldwide depend on water that crosses national
borders. Yet, only 24 countries have cooperation agreements for all their shared
water. The plight of lower riparian States in the inter-state water sharing is
precarious and often end with protest and pandemonium.
As climate change impacts increase and populations grow, there is an urgent need,
within and between countries, states to unite around protecting and conserving our
most precious resource, Water.
Public health and prosperity, food and energy systems, economic productivity and
environmental integrity all rely on a well-functioning and equitably managed water
cycle.
When we cooperate on water, we create a positive ripple effect fostering harmony.
generating prosperity and building resilience to shared challenges.
We must act upon the realization that water is not only a resource to be used and
competed over - it is a human right, intrinsic to every aspect of life.Today we all
need to unite around water and use water for peace, laying the foundations of a
more stable and prosperous tomorrow.
Trans-boundary waters account for 60 per cent of the world's freshwater flows, and
153 countries have territory within at least 1 of the 310 trans-boundary river and
lake basins and inventoried 468 trans-boundary aquifer systems.
Water-related disasters have dominated the list of disasters over the past 50 years
and account for 70% of all deaths related to natural disasters.
Prosperity and peace rely on water. As nations manage climate change, mass
migration and political unrest, they must put water cooperation at the heart of their
19.03.2025 1242
plans.
The issue of water tenure affects social cohesion and peace among different water
users. Water can lead us out of crisis. We can foster harmony between
communities and countries by uniting around the fair and sustainable use of water
from United Nations conventions at the international level, to actions at the local
level.
Safe drinking water and sanitation are human rights. Without access to these
services, a life of dignity, stability and good health is virtually impossible. Water,
when managed sustainably and equitably can be a source of peace and prosperity.
It is also the literal lifeblood of agriculture, the major socio-economic driver for
billions of people.
It can promote community stability and peace building especially in fragile
situations and contribute to migration management and disaster risk reduction.
In an unstable world where security threats are growing, we must all recognize that
ensuring the availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for
all -the aim of Sustainable Development Goal 6 is essential for global prosperity
and peace.
The most important natural wealth of our country is the large number of rivers

19.03.2025 1243

The Nationalization of rivers and inter-linking of the rivers are very important as

country is also part of the agro development activity only.
Tamil Nadu always perform better when it comes to people and reserved the best
for adverse conditions. The Hogenakkal Integrated Drinking Water project and the
Athikadavu Avinashi project are standing testimonial to the commitment of the DMK
government headed by Hon'ble Chief Minister Thalapathi M.K. Stalin in providing
water to underprivileged people living in dry regions. The Veeranam Water Supply
scheme, The Palar water Supply scheme cater the water needs of the people of
Greater Chennai region and adjoining districts of Thiruvallur, Chengalpattu and
Kanchipuram are also the brain child of Muthamilarignar Dr Kalaignar. The
southern Districts of Ramanathapuram, Thoothukudi, Virudhunagar too benefitted
through the far sighted water management of the Government of Tamil Nadu.
Ancient Tamil literature provides fascinating insights into water management
practices and the cultural significance of water in Tamil Nadu.
Thousands of years ago, Tamils possessed remarkable knowledge of water
management. Kings built tanks and lakes to store water, some of which still exist
today.
19.03.2025 1245
Water was considered sacred, and its judicious use was emphasized. Ancient
Tamil literature mentions 47 water bodies, reflecting the prescient wisdom of Tamils
in harvesting water.
Different terms were used to describe various types of water: Munneer: Refers to
the sen, formed by water from rains, rivers, and springs. It symbolized antiquity and
purity. Nanneer: Denoted good water from rivers.
Inneer: Referred to sweet water suitable for drinking.
The Sangam era celebrated water through the festival of "Munneer Vizhavu,"
recognizing its role in soil creation, nourishment, and erosion.
Sangam literature believed that a person's character was shaped by water and soil.
Life revolved around water, and villages were built around water bodies. Ancient
Rituals from birth to death are centred on water. Houses were constructed around
wells, emphasizing water's importance.
Tamil literature highlights the deep connection between water, culture, and
sustainable practices. While challenges persist today, learning from ancient
wisdom can guide better water management in the future
The water resources management policies of union government is ill planned with
misguided priorities and excessive spending on promotion and publicity.
One of the most damning observations comes from the Public Accounts
Committee, which noted excessive expenditure on advertising and promotion has
little to offer in terms of tangible environmental benefits.
Instead of channelling funds towards measures that directly improve water quality
or enhance wastewater treatment capacity, the programme has, in many instances,
19.03.2025 1246
prioritize superficial activities that generate political mileage but fail to address the
underlying environmental degradation.
The amount underutilised and unspent in the Namami Gange Programme can be
used to clean up a number of rivers such as the Kaveri and Thamirabarani, on
which millions of farmers in Tamil Nadu depend upon.
The ambitious project of river inter-linking, envisioned to link water-deficit river
basins with water-surplus basins, remains largely a distant dream.
India is facing a looming water crisis, with per capita water availability declining
steadily. In 2021, the per capita water availability was 1,486 cubic meters, below
the water-stressed threshold of 1,700 cubic meters..
In rural villages across India, the repercussions of inadequate water management
are felt daily. Families still struggle to secure clean water for drinking, cooking, and
sanitation. Farmers, who form the backbone of our society, face the twin challenges
of unreliable irrigation and dwindling groundwater reserves. Communities that once
thrived along the banks of sacred rivers now confront the dual burdens of pollution
and neglect.
Imagine the despair of a village where, despite being declared "Har Ghar Jal" or
"ODF," residents continue to face erratic water supply and deteriorating sanitation
conditions. This government is only a slogan mongering government not bothered
about the good execution of the projects.
Consider the plight of a farmer, forced to depend on depleting groundwater
resources, whose yields are diminishing with every passing season.
These are not abstract statistics-they are real lives, hopes, and dreams that are
19.03.2025 1247
slipping away in the wake of systemic mismanagement of resources by the union
government.
This structural ambiguity is further compounded by the reliance on self-certification
by local bodies. For instance, even after achieving 100% FHTC coverage, villages
are allowed to self-certify their "Har Ghar Jal" status without any independent
verification. Out of the 2.5 lakh villages that reported full connectivity, a staggering
61% have self-certified, raising serious concerns about the integrity of the data and
the actual quality of service provided. When local governance is entrusted with
such critical responsibilities without rigorous oversight, it opens the door for
complacency, misreporting, and, ultimately, a disconnect between reported
progress and the lived reality of citizens.
The Swachh Bharat Mission - Grameen (SBM-G), launched in 2014, aimed to end
open defecation by providing all rural households access to toilets. While the
government declared that Phase-I met its target in October 2019, the National
Family Health Survey 5 reveals that 19% of households (26% rural and 6% urban)
still practiced open defecation between 2019 and 2021.
Phase II of the scheme, which aims to make all villages ODF Plus Model villages
by 2024-25, also faces significant challenges. As of January 30, 2025, only 44% of
SBM-G villages have had their ODF Plus Model certification verified. Furthermore,
the majority of toilets in 14 states and Union Territories continue to be single-pit,
hindering effective faecal sludge management.
Plastic waste management, a key objective under SBM-G Phase II, is also lagging.
As of December 2024, there were only 978 operational Plastic Waste Management
19.03.2025 1248
Units (PWMUs) in the country, with 13 states and Union Territories having none.
Financial mismanagement continues to plague SBM-G, with less than 50% of funds
allocated by the Centre to states/UTs being released between 2020-21 and 2022-
23. Delays in states releasing their share of funds, procedural delays, and
insufficient resources are cited as reasons for this dismal performance.
While sanitation remains a critical pillar of public health, the nation's agricultural
backbone is equally imperiled by mismanagement in irrigation. The Pradhan Mantri
Krishi Sinchai Yojana (PMKSY) was envisioned as a transformative scheme to
increase cultivable land under assured irrigation, improve water use efficiency, and
support the livelihoods of millions of farmers. Yet, the promise of a water-abundant
future for our agrarian economy remains unfulfilled.
Ambitious Targets versus Harsh Realities: PMKSY's goal was to significantly
expand the irrigation potential of India. Despite the allocation of substantial
resources-budget estimates have nearly doubled in recent fiscal cycles-the
scheme's actual impact falls far short of its promise. For instance, under the
Accelerated Irrigation Benefit Programme (AIBP), while the target was to create
irrigation potential for millions of hectares, only a fraction of this potential has been
achieved. Cost overruns, delays in land acquisition and protracted clearance
procedure have hampered progress, leaving farmers in water-scarce regions
stranded.
Delays and Cost Overruns: A detailed review reveals that many projects under
PMKSY have suffered significant delays. Cited delays ranging from one to 18 years
not only inflate the cost of projects but also render the original designs and
19.03.2025 1249
estimates obsolete. The lack of effective project management has resulted in a
situation where planned improvements remain merely aspirational while the real
needs of farmers go unmet.
The plight of PMKSY is a sobering reminder that the government's ambitious
projects, despite their grand scale on paper, often falter at the implementation
stage. The continuing gap between policy pronouncements and tangible outcomes
has left our agrarian communities grappling with water scarcity, reduced crop
yields, and mounting economic distress.
Our nation's dependence on groundwater is both a blessing and a curse. While
groundwater has long served as a critical buffer against the uncertainties of rainfall,
its over-extraction and mismanagement have now emerged as a crisis of
monumental proportions. The Atal Bhujal Yojana (ABY) was conceived as a
corrective measure-a strategy to manage and rejuvenate groundwater resources
in water-stressed regions.
Insufficient Fund Release and Utilization: The ABY, with a total outlay of Rs 6,000
crore, was launched with the promise of community-led groundwater management
and a sustainable approach to water use. Yet, the report indicates that only about
57% of the allocated funds have been released to states, and even of these, only
48% have been effectively utilized. This underutilization signals not just
bureaucratic lethargy but a profound disconnect between policy intent and ground-
level execution.
Slow Progress on Critical Targets: While equipment installation and the
construction of piezometers have, in some instances, surpassed targets, the
19.03.2025 1250
overall improvement in groundwater decline rates remains abysmally low.
The slow pace of training and capacity building at block and district levels leaves
communities ill-equipped to manage their own water resources. Without the
necessary expertise and local empowerment, even the most well-funded initiatives
will flounder.
Systemic Issues in Groundwater Governance: Beyond the technical aspects, there
lies a deeper issue-the fragmentation of responsibility between central and state
agencies. Groundwater, which accounts for 80% of rural drinking water and the
bulk of agricultural irrigation, remains largely unmonitored due to overlapping
jurisdictions and a lack of integrated policy frameworks. The failure to enact and
enforce robust groundwater management laws has allowed unsustainable
practices to continue unchecked, further depleting this vital resource.
The case of ABY illustrates a recurring theme in our water governance framework:
the absence of a coherent, long-term strategy that brings together technological
innovation, community participation, and stringent regulatory oversight. Without
such integration, efforts to manage groundwater remain piecemeal and ineffective,
imperiling the very lifeline of our agricultural communities.
There are several pertinent questions in the minds of the people expecting the
answer from the union minister of Jal Sakthi.

preservation of River Palar and if so the details thereof? if not the reasons there

Vellore Parliamentary constituency. If so the details thereof
c) Whether the union government has any plans to support for the Construction of

what action was taken by the union government in this regard.The Hon'ble Minister

d) Whether it is a fact that the Water Resources Department and Central water
commission has chalked out any new schemes for the safe potable drinking water
in rural and arid regions and if so the details thereof, if not the reasons there for.
We need a categorical answer from the union government.
Sir, there are many harmful effects of drinking of contaminated ground water.
19.03.2025 1252
Whether the government is aware of the harmful effects on human and animals
through high levels of contamination of Arsenic, Mercury and Fluoride in ground
water
b) If so the details thereof
c) Whether the government has any new proposal or project for control of
contamination of Arsenic, Mercury and Fluoride and if so the details thereof and
the total amount sanctioned in this regard
d) Whether it is a fact that the Water Resources Department and Central water
commission has chalked out any new schemes for the safe potable drinking water
in rural and arid regions and if so the details thereof, if not the reasons there for
There are many such questions lingering in the minds of the people.
Before I conclude, I would like to end with few couplets from Tirukkural equating
the importance of Water in the life of the people and country.
Vinninru Poippin Virineer Viyanulakaththu
Ulninru Udatrum Pasi
If the clouds do not provide us with rain there would be famine all around in this
world
Keduppadooum kettaarkku Saarvaay Mattraange
Eduppathooum ellam mazhai
Rain has the power to ruin us and it also has the power to bring back transformation
in the ruined life.
OoruNi Neer NiRainthatre Ulakavaam
PeraRivaalan Thiru
19.03.2025 1253
The wealth of a wise philanthropist is similar to a pond filled with water.
MaNi Neerum MaNNum Malaiyum aNinizhaR
Kaadum udaiyathu araN
Clean water, open space, high rise mountains and dense forests remain like a
fortress for a nation .

SHRIMATI ANITA SUBHADARSHINI (ASKA): I express my views on
the demand for grants of the Ministry of Jal Shakti for the year 2025-26.
The Budget allocation for the Ministry of Jal Shakti is about Rs. 99503 crores for
the year 2025-26. During the last 10 years 76% of the budget of the Ministry of Jal
Shakti has allocated to the Department of Drinking water and Sanitation (DDWS)
with the commitment of hon'ble Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi Ji to provide
safe drinking water and sanitation facility in the rural villages of the country. The
aim of Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) is to provide 19 crore rural households in the
country with functional household tap water connections (FHTCs) by 2024. An
estimate of 15 crore households have been given functional household tap water
connection as on 21st January, 2025. This scheme is further extended until 2028
to reach the target of 100%. My State Odisha has achieved 76% of FHTCs target
till date.
The Department of Water Resources (DoWR) has been allocated Rs. 25277 crore
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1262
in the Budget 2025-26, which is 17% higher than the revised estimate of 2024-25.
The Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana (PMKSY) was launched in 2015-16 with
the aim to increase cultivable area under assured irrigation, improve water
availability on farms and increase water use efficiency. The Ministry of Jal Shakti
implements Accelerated Irrigation Benefit Programme (AIBP) and Har Khet Ko
Pani (HKKP). The Major & Medium irrigation projects are taken up under AIBP
while the minor irrigation projects are under HKKP.
As a Member of Parliament from ASKA, District Ganjam of the State of Odisha, my
constituency's demand is the inter linking of rivers. This project is expected to
increase the irrigation of 35 million hectares of land and will generate 34,000
megawatts of hydro power. The other benefits from inter linking of rivers are
irrigation, navigation, flood control and fisheries. The Nagarul Bansadhara
Rusikulya river link will be pathbreaking and ensure three crop cultivation in
Ganjam district in the state of Odisha. I request hon'ble Minister Jal Shakti to start
the above said river link project as well as allocation of fund for renovation of
Surada reservoir of my constituency.
The people of my constituency ASKA are very hopeful for the fulfillment of the basic
need of drinking water as there is a double engine Sarkar. The renovation of
Surada water reservoir is an essential project, many problems of my constituency
will be solved and people of my Parliamentary Constituency will be highly obliged.
Thanking you.
19.03.2025 1263

SHRIMATI MALVIKA DEVI (KALAHANDI): First, I would like to thank our hon.
Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modi ji, for his vision which has changed the country
in the last ten years. All the Ministries are developing the country like never before.
And now, even Odisha which was not developing since last 25 years is now
developing with our double engine Sarkar.
I would like to thank our hon. Jal Shakti Minster, Shri Patil ji. Your efforts to
allocate additional funds for various schemes and programs aimed at ensuring
water security, sanitation and hygiene are truly commendable. I must appreciate
the increased allocation towards the Jal Jeevan Mission. The focus of our Minister
to provide clean drinking water, water conservation and sustainable ground water
conservation is commendable. Our Ministry's emphasis on improving sanitation
facilities and promoting cleanliness through Swachh Bharat Mission Gramin is
laudable.
The budget allocation for Jal Shakti in 2025-26 is Rs. 99,503 crore, which is
higher than the previous year's allocation. This allocation is divided between the
following departments: Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation, and
Department of Water Resources, River Development, and Ganga Rejuvenation.
Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation: Rs. 74,226 crore, with a significant
portion allocated to the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) at Rs. 67,000 crore. Department
of Water Resources, River Development, and Ganga Rejuvenation: Rs. 25,277
crore, with allocations for schemes like Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayi Yojana
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1264
(PMKSY) at Rs. 8,260 crore, River Interlinking at Rs. 3,400 crore, and Namami
Gange at Rs. 2,400 crore.
I would like to make a request to our hon. Minister for my constituency of
Kalahandi with two districts of Kalahandi and Nuapada. Sir, Kalahandi constituency
is the second highest paddy producing constituency In Odisha and we are hoping
to become the number one constituency in Odisha with your help. The way work is
happening all over India is now happening in Odisha also after we have hot out
own double engine Sarkar, the hopes of the people are very high.
Kamal irrigation project or Motor irrigation project over the river motor at
Kuntia village in Koksara block. Its irrigation potential is 7500 hectare. It will irrigate
50 villages of 11 gps of both Koksara and Dharamgarh.
Uttar-roul irrigation project over the river Uttei Raul and Buda river. Its
irrigation potentiality is 60000 hectare. It will benefit the people of Kesinga, M
Rampur, Narla and Karla Munda.
Lower Uddanri irrigation project over the river Uddanri in Bordi at Golamunda
block. Its irrigation potential is 12000 hectare. It is a major project 40 villages of
Bhawanipatna, six villages of Golamunda block in Kalahandi constituency and 17
villages of Titlagarh in Bolangir district. This project is also held up die to Mahanadi
water dispute tribunal. Please look into that as well.
Upper Udanti Barrage Project over river Udanti at Patialapara village in
Sinapali block of Nuapada district. Its irrigation potentiality is 10000 hectare. It is a
major project. It will benefit 39 villages of Sinapali block and 14 villages of Borden
block a total of 53 villages.
19.03.2025 1265
Upper Jonk Irrigation Project located at Patora in Nuapada district needs
environmental clearance. Please look into this and pass it as it will irrigate 9200
hectares of land.
To conclude my speech, I must again thank our hon. Minister for the great
work being done in the Jal Shakti Ministry and also request our hon. Minister to
please look into the demands of people of my Kalahandi constituency. Thank you.

SHRI V. K. SREEKANDAN (PALAKKAD): I expressing my views on the
Demands for Grants under the control of the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
I am happy that I am participating in a subject which is most essential for a
human being i.e. Jal, in Malayalam is Jalam or Vellam.
It is good that the scheme Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) rural household tap
connectivity scheme has been extended till the year 2028.The State of Kerala will
be benefitted out of the extension provided in the Union Budget for 2025-2026 as
the State has been lagging behind in its implementation of the said scheme. To
implement the scheme Kerala needs to pump in about Rs.17,500 crore more as its
share over the next two years. Where is the money? The State is unable to provide
its share Kerala and whatever the work done in the State on account of the Central
share. The State has urged the Centre to allow the financing requirements for
Centrally-Sponsored Schemes over and above the limit of the State borrowings
and this aspect has not been addressed. Therefore, the State would not be able to
complete the works on time. Therefore, the Jal Shakti Ministry should come forward
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1266
to help the State of Kerala to implement the above scheme within the time extended
time period by taking up the part of financial requirement.
All over Kerala, the roads have been dug for laying of pipes and pipes have
been laid, but the main task of constructing water storage tanks for supply through
the laid pipelines have not been done in most of the places. The general public
questions what is the source of water for all these pipelines laid all over the State
and when will water flow through these laid pipelines.
The steady depletion of groundwater is a cause of concern. In many places
extraction of groundwater has become impossible as the groundwater level has
gone deep or dried up. Therefore, the Government needs to focus more on
rainwater harvesting, which will help to prevent the depletion level of groundwater.
The Government should come forward with a scheme to encourage rainwater
harvesting like we provide incentives in the case of solar energy.
There is another issue which is very vital as far as storage of water in our
dams is concerned. The storage capacity of water in most of our dams has been
reduced due to accumulation of soil that came with the water. These dams were
constructed many decades ago. Therefore, the Government should take immediate
steps to remove the accumulated soil from all our dams as well as components
such as nitrates, fluoride, uranium, etc. that contaminate the water. The Central
Government should formulate policy towards and aid the concerned State
Governments. There are eight dams in Palakkad district alone and the storage
capacity of these dams have got reduced due to the above factor. It is a well-known
fact that when the dams are full to its capacity, we can control inflation, so dams
19.03.2025 1267
play a pivotal role in our economy too.
Another issue which I would like to highlight here is about the dried-up water
resources. We need to rejuvenate the rivers and ponds, etc. on a time bound basis,
to make availability of water for drinking and irrigation purposes.
Sir, the water sharing pacts between the States were done many decades
ago in most of the cases. Like-wise, water sharing from Parambikulam - Aliyar
Project also done between Kerala and Tamil Nadu many decades ago. As per the
agreement, Kerala was to get 7.25 TMC of water, but Kerala has been getting only
half of the agreed share of water. The agreement was done when we were not
doing much agricultural activities in those water-flowing area of the project. But
now, it has increased by double or triple hectares in thousands. Water from the
project flows through Chittoorpuzha and finally merge into Bharathapuzha. Since
the area is not getting the agreed share of water, several places are facing a
drought like situation and the wells have been dried up.
The water resource for two taluks in Palakkad namely Attappadi and
Mannarkkad is from river Bhavani. But due to construction of check dams across
river Bhavani by Tamil Nadu, these taluks under Palakkad district are facing acute
shortage of water for drinking and irrigation purposes. Therefore, the Centre should
take steps to ensure that parties to water sharing agreements honour their
commitments and flow of water should not be stopped by creating check dams
across rivers as is the case of river Bhavani.
Another issue, which I would like to state is about excess nitrates, fluoride
and uranium. According to a Report by the Central Groundwater Board, 440
19.03.2025 1268
districts in the country have excess nitrates in groundwater which is a health
hazard, particularly for young children. The groundwater extraction is at peak in
many States. So, this contamination of ground water with these hazardous
materials will pose a threat to our population. Therefore, the Government should
create awareness among the people about this health hazard arising out of
contaminated groundwater with nitrates, fluoride and uranium.
It is also a demand that where there is a scarcity of water, no company or
institution should be allowed to function as they happen to extract more
groundwater for running of their units. For example, the people of Elappully
Panchayat in Palakkad district have been agitating against the decision of the
Government of Kerala to allow to set up of a brewery over an area of 26 acres in
Elappully Panchayat in Palakkad district as it will seriously affect the farming
activities as well as the availability of water in the Panchayat. The people of this
area solely depend upon the groundwater for all their needs as it is a water scarce
area. The proposed brewery has to extract groundwater to run their units which will
result in the depletion of groundwater in the area, forcing the people to move out of
the Panchayat on this account alone.
The people of Elappully Panchayat had resisted the attempt to set up a
brewery in Elappully in the year 2020 on these accounts and the proposal had to
be withdrawn because of strict resistance of the people. Moreover, the proposal to
set up a brewery was cleared by the State Government without undertaking any
environmental impact study. On three issues, namely water deficit, loss of farming,
and impact on the environment, this proposal should not be allowed.
19.03.2025 1269
Therefore, it is urged that the Ministry of Jal Shakti should take a note of the
above and advise the State Government not to go ahead with the said proposal.
With this I conclude.

अब तक 11 करोड़ से अिधक घर(cid:332) को नल जल से जोड़ा जा चुका है । इसका फायदा न के वल (cid:250)ामीण

(cid:214)व(cid:184)छ पानी क(cid:236) उपल(cid:202)धता से जलजिनत बीमा(cid:229)रय(cid:332) म(cid:164) भी कमी आई है ।


SHRI SAPTAGIRI SANKAR ULAKA (KORAPUT): In 2025, we stand here
debating the failure of the Ministry of Jal Shakti to deliver this basic necessity to
millions across the country. Instead of ensuring access to clean drinking water,
efficient river management, and sustainable groundwater use, this Ministry has
become a symbol of inefficiency and misplaced priorities. The biggest failure
indicators are the massive budget cuts and underutilization of funds. In 2024-25,
the Ministry allocated Rs. 70,163 crore to JJM but spent only Rs. 22,694 crore –
the lowest expenditure since 2020-21. Now, in 2025-26, Rs. 67,000 crore has been
allocated, but can we trust that these funds will be effectively used, or will they once
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1273
again remain unspent while millions suffer?
Even when funds are released, the Centre makes it nearly impossible for
States to utilize them. In 2024-25, only 31 per cent of the Centre’s allocation for
JJM was released. Why? Because the States had to first contribute their share – a
condition that is impossible for many financially struggling States to meet. Odisha,
for instance, was allocated Rs. 2,456 crore but has been able to use only Rs. 1,056
crore because of bureaucratic roadblocks. This is not cooperative federalism; this
is fiscal strangulation.
India is facing a massive groundwater depletion crisis. Over 80 per cent of
rural drinking water and 50 per cent of urban water comes from groundwater, but
our aquifers are running dry. In Odisha, groundwater extraction has reached
dangerous levels of 48 per cent, and yet there is no serious policy response from
the Ministry of Jal Shakti. Both districts of my constituency Koraput and Rayagada,
where agriculture and daily life depend on groundwater, are on the verge of a water
catastrophe. The Government’s Atal Bhujal Yojana (ABY) was supposed to tackle
groundwater depletion, but as of 2025, only 60 per cent of its targets have been
met. The Centre claims to be focusing on conservation, but what it is really doing
is letting the crisis spiral out of control while making big claims on paper.
In Odisha, the failure of Jal Jeevan Mission is particularly glaring. In Koraput
and Rayagada, many villages still rely on contaminated groundwater because the
JJM pipeline projects have either not been completed or are lying defunct. The
Government has focused on installing pipelines, but has ignored water sources –
leading to drying reservoirs and dysfunctional supply networks. Fluoride and iron
19.03.2025 1274
contamination in drinking water remains a severe issue, affecting thousands of
families in these districts. The biggest flaw in this scheme is its sustainability. Even
in areas where pipes have been laid, the lack of water recharge plans means that
sources will run dry in just a few years. The Jal Jeevan Mission is a classic example
of this Government’s obsession with short-term numbers instead of long-term
impact.
Irrigation projects across the country have become synonymous with delays,
mismanagement, and unfulfilled promises, failing to deliver the intended benefits
to farmers and rural communities. One of the most recent failures is the Telengiri
Multi-Purpose Irrigation Project in Koraput. This project was meant to provide water
security to thousands of farmers, but farmers faced problems due to its leakage,
draining away the very water that was meant to sustain agricultural livelihoods. For
the last few months, water has been continuously flowing out of the reservoir due
to an unresolved leakage. The water level is falling drastically, and farmers now
fear they will not get water. The consequences are devastating – 24,000 farmers
in Koraput are on the verge of losing their crops. And what is the Government’s
response? Denial, inaction, and empty promises. Instead of immediately fixing the
problem, officials dismiss it as a minor issue, while the farmers are left to bear the
brunt of their incompetence. The additional chief engineer of the Telengiri project
has already admitted that plugging will be done only after the water level reaches
596 metres, which means farmers will not receive water soon.
Another glaring example of the Government’s apathy toward farmers is the
Parbati Giri Mega Lift Irrigation Project in Jajpur district. Designed to irrigate 12,800
19.03.2025 1275
hectares of farmland, it has been 15 years since its launch, yet 40 per cent of the
targeted area still does not receive water. Farmers have repeatedly reported
irregularities in the project, alleging that officials have manipulated data to claim
success, even when thousands of hectares remain dry. A local farmers’
organization, had to complain with the State Water Resources Department,
exposing the misrepresentation of irrigation coverage and the misallocation of
funds.
How is it possible that after 15 years, this Government still cannot provide
irrigation to farmers who depend on it? How is it possible that even after crores of
rupees have been allocated, irrigation systems are still non-functional? Farmers
from villages like Bhagirathpur, Sarangapur, and Tikarapada are still waiting for
water to reach their fields. Pipelines were laid on their land a decade ago, yet not
a single drop of water has flowed through them. These farmers were promised
prosperity and financial stability; instead, they have been left with debt, despair,
and loss.
This pattern of failure is not limited to a few projects. It is widespread. Take
the case of the Tulasipur Pump House project, which was supposed to lift water
from the Kharasrota River and irrigate 2,000 hectares of farmland. Farmers in
Barchana, Bari, Dharmasala, and Korei were supposed to benefit from this
initiative. But to this day, the water supply remains non-functional, leaving
thousands of farmers at the mercy of erratic monsoons. These failures are not
accidents. They are consequences of deliberate mismanagement, unchecked
corruption, and a lack of accountability. This Government’s irrigation policies are
19.03.2025 1276
nothing but a web of broken promises that serve bureaucrats and contractors while
destroying the lives of farmers.
The failures in irrigation management are not confined to a few States but
are a nationwide concern, affecting both farmers and the agricultural sector at
large. In Goa, over a period of 12 years, there has been a 20.9 per cent decline in
minor irrigation schemes, reducing the number to 6,133. This decline includes 739
groundwater schemes (15 per cent) and 883 surface water schemes (30 per cent)
that are no longer operational. Consequently, the irrigation potential utilized in Goa
has decreased by 3.7 per cent compared to the previous evaluation in 2013-14.
The financial burden of these irrigation schemes predominantly falls on the farmers
themselves. Farmers have to get their own pump sets and they take huge loans for
that which further exacerbates their plight.
One of the most hyped projects of this Government has been Namami
Gange, which was supposed to clean and rejuvenate the Ganga River. Yet, after
thousands of crores spent, the reality remains grim. The pollution levels in the
Ganga are still alarmingly high, sewage treatment plants remain non-functional,
and waste disposal continues unchecked. The Namami Gange programme has
turned into a financial black hole. Since 2014, only 69 per cent of the funds
allocated have actually been spent. The Government has shut down factories along
the river in the name of pollution control, but most sewage treatment plants remain
non-functional or poorly maintained. In 2024, only 52 per cent of the targeted
sewage treatment capacity had been achieved, while untreated sewage continues
to be dumped into the river. Even worse, of the Rs. 876 crore collected under the
19.03.2025 1277
Clean Ganga Fund, only Rs. 383 crore has been sanctioned for actual projects.
What about the rest? It is lying unused while the river continues to suffer. But the
failure is not limited to the Ganga. Other rivers – Yamuna, Krishna, Godavari, and
Mahanadi – are also dying due to pollution, encroachment, and mismanagement.
The Vamsadhara River, which is critical to Rayagada district, faces severe
contamination and water-sharing disputes, yet the Ministry of Jal Shakti has failed
to resolve these issues or initiate a proper rejuvenation plan.
Similarly, the Mahanadi River dispute remains unresolved, as Chhattisgarh
continues to divert water meant for Odisha, affecting thousands of farmers and
villages. The Ministry has refused to intervene effectively, failing to protect Odisha’s
water rights. While the Government boasts about cleaning one river, dozens of
other rivers across the country remain polluted, over-exploited, and neglected. If
this Government does not act now, India is heading toward a catastrophic water
crisis. And when that happens, history will remember this Ministry as the one that
drowned the country in its own incompetence.
India’s per capita water availability has now dropped to 1,486 cubic metres
per year, well below the 1,700 cubic metre threshold for water stress. By 2050, it is
projected to fall to 1,228 cubic metres. This means that in just 25 years, India could
be facing an all-out water emergency. Yet, what is the Centre doing? Instead of
tackling the root causes, this Government is busy making tall claims and deflecting
responsibility onto the States. While the Government boasts about urban
infrastructure projects, rural and tribal communities are still struggling for drinking
water. In Koraput and Rayagada, entire villages lack access to clean drinking
19.03.2025 1278
water, forcing people, especially women, to walk for miles just to fetch water from
unsafe sources.
Where is Swachh Bharat, Modiji? You go to real India, and you will find piles
of garbage on roads, clogged drains overflowing with filth, and waste
mismanagement at its peak. The Government claims India is open defecation free
(ODF), yet in rural areas, people still have to defecate in open fields due to lack of
functional toilets. You promised a clean India, but what has happened to Swachh
Bharat Cess, which was collected from the hard-earned money of citizens? Where
has that money gone?
Gandhi ji envisioned a clean and self-reliant India, but he would have been
heartbroken to see how his image has been misused by this Government to
promote a scheme riddled with corruption and mismanagement. Instead of true
cleanliness, Swachh Bharat Mission has become a propaganda tool, an excuse for
officials and contractors to pocket funds, and an obstacle for States in utilizing other
developmental grants. What is even more shocking is the cost of toilet construction.
The Government sets a minimal allocation per toilet, but every MP in this House
can tell you that the money is never fully disbursed. Corruption is rampant, from
local contractors to officials at the highest level. There are thousands of incomplete
and non-functional toilets across the country built on paper but not in reality.
While the Government talks about clean India, the reality is stinking landfills
in every major city, choked drainage systems, and incomplete waste management
projects. The Government claims that toilets have been built across India, but how
many of them are actually usable? In many rural areas, toilets have been built but
19.03.2025 1279
remain locked because there is no water supply. Sewage systems remain
unfinished, leading to overflowing drains that spread diseases. Waste collection is
irregular and inefficient, with garbage accumulating in urban and semi-urban areas.
Sanitation workers continue to work in inhumane conditions, with manual
scavenging still prevalent, despite being banned. Even under the Swachh Bharat
Mission, many toilets in rural areas lack running water, making sanitation unusable
and ineffective. How can we claim to have achieved Swachh Bharat when people
do not even have water to use these facilities?
This is the high time that we come together, not just as representatives of
our constituencies but as custodians of the future. For too long, we have witnessed
misuse of funds, unfinished irrigation projects, non-functional toilets, and worsening
groundwater depletion. The crisis is here, and we must act now with a sustainable,
transparent, and corruption-free approach. We must have an independent audit of
Swachh Bharat and Jal Jeevan Mission to expose corruption, mismanagement,
and non-functional projects. Ensure proper water supply to every constructed toilet
and invest in faecal sludge and sewage treatment instead of just building
infrastructure. Stop misuse of Swachh Bharat Cess and provide full transparency
on funds collected and spent. Fix failing irrigation projects like Telengiri Dam,
Parbati Giri Mega Lift, and Tulasipur Pump House to ensure farmers get water.
Resolve water-sharing disputes like the Polavaram and Mahanadi conflicts and
protect Odisha’s water rights. Strengthen groundwater conservation by enforcing
rainwater harvesting, curbing industrial over-extraction, and improving Atal Bhujal
Yojana. End water privatization to ensure drinking water and irrigation remain
19.03.2025 1280
affordable and accessible to all, especially rural and tribal communities. We
demand accountability; we demand action; and we demand justice for every citizen
still struggling for water.
