SHRI RAJU BISTA (DARJEELING): The vision of Antodaya-reaching benefit to
the most marginalised person is the governing philosophy championed by Hon'ble
Prime Minister Sh. Narendra Modi ji
For this, our government has worked tirelessly to ensure "Sabka Saath, Sabka
Vikas across our nation
Among the various rights, I believe right to food and right to safe drinking water are
the most fundamental and most important rights
Since COVID, our government has provided "free ration" to over 80 crore citizens
to ensure food safety for all
Poverty Reduction
I commend the dynamic leadership of PM Modi ji
A recent study by Economists has found that Extreme Poverty, which is measured
at Rs 150 per day Income
Has been reduced to below 1% of the population in India
When compared to World Bank's US3.65 Purchasing Power Parity - or Rs 350
India's poverty rate has fallen from 52% in 2011-12 to 15% in 2023-24.
This unprecedented growth in income has been made possible due to the dynamic
leadership of PM Modi ji
Har Ghar Jal
Like I said before, right to food and right to safe drinking water are the most
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1286
fundamental and most important rights
Along with food security, our government is working to secure clean and safe
drinking water for all
Towards this, our Govt has allocated Rs 99503 cr to Jal Shakti Minister
Through the "Jal Jeevan Mission - Har Ghar Jal Yojana" the Ministry is working to
provide drinking water for all - FY 2025-26 allocation Rs 67000 cr
In 2019 only 16% of the households had tap water connection
Today, over 80% of the households in the country have tap water
I thank and applaud PM Modi Ji, and Jal Shakti Minister CR Patil ji for their dynamic
leadership, which is making this possible.
Swachh Bharat Gramin
In addition to providing water, Jal Shakti Ministry is also working to provide modern
sewerage facilities for our villages
Towards this, our Govt has allocated Rs 7192 crore in FY 2025-26
Today, 96% of the villages have become Open Defection Free
73% of the villages have Solid Waste Management arrangements
Our nation is moving towards swachhta at a rapid pace.
Jal Jeevan Mission in West Bengal
Sir, despite adequate support from the Central Government, the WB Govt has
failed to implement "Har Ghar Jal" scheme properly
I am sad to report that today West Bengal is among the worst performing states in
India
Only 54% completed till date - that too only on paper
19.03.2025 1287
Along with this, there has been massive Ghotala
In my Darjeeling constituency alone 714 projects worth Rs. 2451 cr 'Har Ghar Jal’
schemes have been granted by the Central Government
But, if you come on the ground you will not even see 30% work completed
Money arrives in Kolkata and disappears
Ghotala in Darjeeling and Kalimpong Region
In Darjeeling and Kalimpong hill region The 'Jal Jeevan Mission' projects in the
region are being developed, managed and implemented by a separate division of
PHE created by the West Bengal Government - Neorakhola W/S & MTC Division
in collaboration with the Gorkhaland Territorial Administration.
The said agencies have created the DPRs for Har Ghar Jal projects in our region
without making any provisions for identifying the source of the water.
Almost all the DPRs are only concerned with laying of the pipes, constructing
storage tanks, however it has now come to fore that no provisions for identifying
the water source and how water will flow from the source to the tanks have been
undertaken.
The WB Govt has failed to develop any monitoring mechanism for the project
development and implementation
This has resulted in massive ghotala in almost all the 'Jal Jeevan Misison' projects
implemented in the region.
I have myself visited various parts of constituency extensively and witnessed the
shoddy work being undertaken first hand.
In addition, I have received over 100 complaints on phone, and around 20 written
19.03.2025 1288
complaints from across the constituency accompanied with pictures and videos
highlighting the shoddy work being undertaken.
Please note that the Darjeeling and Kalimpong districts are situated in the
mountains, with fragile ecology and environment and geological conditions. We get
very high degree of rainfall, and landslides are a regular occurrence.
Hence it is important for the pipes laid, the route they follow, the tanks being built
are constructed maintaining to the highest standards of engineering. However, that
is not happening.
Nature of Ghotalas
Instead of Gl or steel pipes, majority of the contractors are using cheap plastic
pipes, which won't even last for a year.
In almost all places, pipes are being laid down randomly without conducting any
sort of engineering survey.
Majority of the times, these pipes are being left hanging precariously, or supported
by thin pieces of wood instead of a proper platform being created.
It is but given that none of these pipes will survive the oncoming monsoon season,
and I fear they will be the cause of landslides in many places, as the pipes have
been laid blocking the natural drains through which rainwater flows.
In most of the cases, the contractors are using existing tanks, some built during the
British times, and merely painting over them or putting a thin layer of plaster and
passing them off as tanks built under the 'Har Ghar Jal' scheme.
In places where new tanks have been built, the quality of the material used has
been very low standard, and the tanks themselves will come undone after a year
19.03.2025 1289
or two.
Request on Har Ghar Jal
Since this is perhaps a one-time investment by the Govt of India to provide drinking
water to each and every household across the country, I am most worried that the
entire project will fail to make any actual change in Darjeeling Lok Sabha
Constituency due to the massive ghotala that is going on, and the shoddy nature
of the work that is being undertaken.
Given these, I am therefore requesting you to kindly have the allegations of
corruption duly inspected by Central Investigative Agencies and take due action
against the culprits as per the law.
Stop release of all payments to all 'Jal Jeevan Mission' projects until and unless
projects are given 'satisfied all criteria and was implemented properly’ certificate by
the DISHA Committee.
Teesta Floods
Further the absolute discrimination against Darjeeling hills, Teral, Dooars region by
the WB Govt is shameful.
In October 2023, massive floods on Teesta River severely impacted nearly 500
families, causing loss of homes, agricultural land, livelihoods, and claiming 12 lives
in Darjeeling and Kalimpong.
Despite this, the WB Government has refused to recognize it as a "disaster".
Because of which, people have been deprived of PMNRF and SDRF support.
Now Teesta is flooding again, the river-bed has risen by over 50-feet, WB Govt has
not given any support to the people.
19.03.2025 1290
We are also part of India; our people also pay taxes.
In fact, our region contributes the highest amount of revenue per capita to the WB
Govt. Revenue from Teesta's hydro-dams alone exceeds Rs 750 crores annually.
totalling over Rs 7500 crores in the past decade.
However, whenever we are in need, we are treated as second class citizens.
This is why, people have been demanding a Separate State Gorkhaland. Because,
people feel WB Govt will always discriminate against our region.
Request on Teesta
I request Central Govt to declare Teesta Floods as National Disaster, and provide
Central Support to the people, not only in Sikkim but also in Darjeeling and
Kalimpong.
Further, I have received a response from MoS Jal Shakti Sh. Raj Bhushan
Choudhary ji, informing me that "Brahmaputra Board has taken up the preparation
of master plan for Teesta river sub-basin for sustainable development and
utilization of Water Resources in the region including flood and erosion
management, catchment area treatment using State of the Art Technology."
In this regard, I am inviting the Board Members to visit Teesta river basin in
Darjeeling and Kalimpong Districts, to meet with the victims of 2023 Teesta Floods
and develop plans that will contribute towards preventing and/or minimizing the
impacts of floods on river Teesta.
I will be most grateful, if the Brahmaputra Board members visit Darjeeling and
Kalimpong to take stock of the situation, and physically verify the need for
embankments and developing of critical infrastructure along Teesta River.
19.03.2025 1291

SHRI BHARTRUHARI MAHTAB (CUTTACK): The Ministry of Jal Shakti has
been allocated Rs.99,503 crore in 2025-26, which is marginally higher than the
budget allocation in 2024-25 which was Rs.98,714 crore. The revised estimate for
expenditure in 2024-25 is lower than the budget allocation. It may be because of
the reduced allocation to the Jal Jeevan Mission at the revised stage. Over the last
ten years, 76 per cent of the budget allocation has gone to the Department of
Drinking Water and Sanitation. Until 2021-22 the gap between allocations to the
two departments remained less than Rs.50 crore. In 2021-22 allocation for Drinking
Water and Sanitation almost tripled over the previous year, largely driven by
increased allocation to the Jal Jeevan Mission.
Jal Jeevan Mission is a centrally sponsored scheme which was launched in 2019
to provide assured tap water supply to all 19 crore rural household and public
institutions by 2024. As on January 21, 2025, 15 crore households i.e. 80 percent
of all households have been provided with functional household tap water
connections. The scheme has been extended until 2028. Though all rural
households in eight states and three Union Territories have tap water connections,
less than 60 percent households in four states namely, Jharkhand, Kerala,
Rajasthan and West Bengal been covered as on January 2025. Around 2.5 lakh
villages have reported 100 percent tap connectivity, but 1.5 lakh villages have self-
certified their ‘Har Ghar Jal' status. For certification the Gram Panchayats need to
pass a resolution self-certifying the Har Ghar Jal' status.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1292
Jal Jeevan Mission guidelines require that at least 55 litres of water per person per
day be supplied. Water supply be considered regular if it is available daily, for all
12 months. The functionality Assessment Repor on 2022 states that in Kerala,
Sikkim and Tripura water supply to more than 40 percent of households did not
meet quality standards. As Jal Jeevan Mission is a Centrally sponsored scheme
funding is divided between Centre and States on 50:50 basis and for Himalayan
and North-Eastern states on 90:10 basis. But it is pertinent to note that in 2024-25
only 31 percent of the Central share allocated has been released as of February
25. The Standing Committee on Water Resources has noted in 2024 that cost
overruns have also created additional financial burden on States, which delayed
the release of the States' share.
Another flagship programme is Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana (PMKSY). As
large part of India agricultural land is rain-fed and only less than half of net sown
area is under irrigation, PMKSY was launched in 2015-16 to Increase cultivable
area under assured irrigation. Accelerated Benefit Programme (AIBP) AND Har
Khet Ko Pani (HKKP) are two components of this Scheme. Since 2016-17 a total
Rs.59,34 crore has been allocated toward PMKSY and 87% has been the actual
expenditure. AIBP's Irrigation potential target was 15 million hectares. Up to March
2023, 48 percent of the target i.e. 7.2 million hectares has been met. Since AIBP's
inclusion under PMKSY in 2016-17, 63 per cent of projects i.e. 62 out of 99 have
been completed. Odisha's Brahmani Left Canal is one of such project which is yet
to be completed. There has been cost over-run and needs attention of the Union
Government for speedy completion of this project and would irrigate large areas of
19.03.2025 1293
Dhenkanal and Cuttack districts of Odisha. In 2018, a CAG audit of AIBP found
delays in project completion ranging from one of 18 years. This has led to a cost
overrun almost thrice the original cost of 84 crores while targeted benefits were not
realised.
River Inter-linking have been a cherished dream of may visionaries of this country.
Thirty river-linking projects have been identified under the National Perspective
Plan of 1980. The aim of these projects is to link water deficit river basins with
water-surplus basins. As of now only one, namely, Ken-Betwa link project have
been completed. Odisha, in that aspect has number of river valleys which are rain-
fed rivers. At times, some rivers go dry. They are not perennial rivers. Yet during
non-monsoon times these valleys need water. Therefore, Dams been constructed
on river Mahanadi, Brahmani, Indravati. Accordingly, Barrages been constructed
too. There is a proposal to link these Barrages through canals. The Anandpur
Barrage over Baltarani need to be connected with Bidydharpur Barrage over
Salandi river and further be linked with Buddha Balanga river is a long-cherished
dream of Bhadrak and Baleswar district of Odisha. Similarly, connecting Samal
Barrage over river Brahmani with Anandapur Barrage over Baitarani in the North
and with Mahanadi in the South will increase the irrigation potential of Jajpur and
Cuttack district of Odisha. For project like these, Union Government's active
support is required. I would say, primarily inter-linking of rivers within the State
should be taken up first instead of drawing ambitious projects of inter-State in
nature.
Central Water Commission's estimate show that in 2021, 1486 cubic metres of
19.03.2025 1294
water was available per person in India. Annual per capita water availability less
than 1700 cubic metres indicates a water-stressed condition. Our country receives
water from river, groundwater and rain. A calculation says 61 percent of utilizable
water is from surface water sources, and 39 percent from ground water. In India 90
percent of water is used for agriculture, 7 percent for domestic use, and 2 percent
for industrial purposes. For the last four decades ground water has been the main
source of water for irrigation. Though India is not a water deficit country, severe
neglect and lack of water resources monitoring have led some regions to
experience persistent water stress. 80 percent of India's rural drinking water, 50
percent of urban drinking water and two-thirds of water for irrigation comes from
groundwater resources. Since almost 90 percent of extracted groundwater is used
for irrigation, it becomes important to examine water-use efficiency in agriculture.
Free electricity supply for agricultural use has led to indiscriminate water extraction
and subsequent wastage.
Water in all its forms is a single entity, with groundwater and surface water
continuously interacting with each other. Therefore, management and governance
of water resources needs an integrated, holistic and multidisciplinary approach.
Central agencies and State departments should work together to conserve and
manage groundwater. Water management is an inter-disciplinary field, connecting
geosciences, environmental sciences and socio-economic sciences. The Central
Water Commission, established since 1945 and Central Ground Water Board
established since 1971 have continued unreformed for decades are working in an
isolated manner. It is high time a National Water Commission be established as
19.03.2025 1295
the apex body dealing with water policy, data and governance in India.
Pollution of water is another issue which needs attention of the Government. We
have Central Pollution Control Board which monitors and assesses the quality of
river stretches across the country. Release of effluents into the rivers is the main
reasons that pollute the river waters. As most of the factories release pollutant
waste into the river and sewage of cities and towns are left into the rivers untreated,
some rivers have become pollutant to such an extent that its waters are not used
for bathing, what to talk of human consumption.
Groundwater assessment in India have identified the presence of contaminants like
fluoride, arsenic nitrate, iron and heavy metals beyond permissible limit. In Odisha,
river like Brahmai have become pollutant because of large discharge of effluent
into the river. There are areas in Khandapada and Bhapur Block of Nayagarh
district where groundwater is arsenic and it is causing health problems. Large
number of people facing kidney problem in Narasinghpur, Baramba, Tigiria of
Cuttack district of Odisha because of contaminant water, both ground and surface
water. I draw the attention of the Government to take steps to provide adequate,
safe potable water in this region.
Though management of floods and erosion falls under State jurisdiction, the Union
Government provides technical guidance and financial assistance. Hirakud Dam
construction was completed in 1957 which has tamed floods of Mahanadi to a great
extent. But number of big barrages been constructed in the catchment area of
Mahanadi in Chhattisgarh which has curtailed steady water flow and during the
non-Monsoon period, the reiver has become totally dry.
19.03.2025 1296
The new Jobra Barrage was constructed in 1979 and it also needs upkeepment.
Two things need to be considered on urgent basis. Firstly, Hirakud Dam needs
proper care and water flow into its reservoir be uninterrupted so that on the
downstream there would be no difficulty in getting water. Support be provided to
construct at least seven more barrages on the downstream of Hirakud on Mahanadi
where water reservoirs be constructed in the riverbed. Before I conclude I must
say, let us ensure that water is made available for diverse water needs, such as
domestic use, sanitation, irrigation and industrial use. Let us ensure conservation
of rivers and other water bodies.
Thank you.


ह ै उ(cid:198)ह(cid:164) ही िसंचाई हेतु पया(cid:170)(cid:302) पानी उपल(cid:202)ध नह(cid:233) ह ै ।

SHRI RAMESH AWASTHI (KANPUR): I express my views on the DFG for Jal
Shakti Ministry. At a time when the world is burdened by climate change, failing to
provide drinking water and struggling to manage its natural resources, India, under
the visionary leadership of Prime Minister Narendra Modi, has taken bold and
transformative steps to ensure water security, conservation, and equitable
distribution. Access to clean and safe drinking water is not just a basic necessity; it
is a fundamental right and a cornerstone of development. While many nations are
still grappling with the challenges of water scarcity, India has demonstrated an
unwavering commitment to tackling this crisis head- on through initiatives like the
Jal Jeevan Mission, Atal Bhujal Yojana, and the ambitious Namami Gange
Programme.
National Ganga Plan & the Kumbh Mela:
 At Mahakumbh 2025 in Prayagraj, the Namami Gange Pavilion has
emerged as a symbol of India's unwavering commitment to protecting and
rejuvenating the sacred Ganga River. Under the visionary leadership of
Prime Minister Narendra Modi, this grand event has witnessed unparalleled
success, bringing millions together in a spiritual and cultural confluence. The
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1301
Ministry of Jal Shakti's Namami Gange Pavilion has captivated visitors with
its cutting-edge technology, breathtaking displays, and deep cultural
significance. The Interactive Biodiversity Tunnel immersed people in the
sounds and sights of Ganga's rich ecosystem, reminding everyone of their
duty to protect its purity. The Prayag Manch platform provided real-time
insights into the river's health, while models of sewage treatment plants and
riverfront developments, showcase the government's relentless efforts in
conservation. The presence of a reading corner by the National Book Trust
and contributions from IIT Delhi, the Wildlife Institute of India, and the Ganga
Task Force made this pavilion an educational powerhouse for future
generations.
 A significant testament to the Modi government's dedication to Ganga
rejuvenation is the substantial budgetary increase for the National Ganga
Plan. The Budget Estimate (BE) for 2025-26 has been set at ₹3,400 crore,
marking an increase from the BE 2024-25 of ₹3,345.70 crore and a sharp
rise from the Revised Estimate (RE) 2024-25 of ₹3,000 crore. This
represents a 13.33% increase from the RE 2024-25, underscoring the
government's unwavering resolve to ensure cleaner, healthier rivers. This
financial commitment is further strengthened by nationwide public
awareness campaigns, ensuring that the mission is not just about policy but
a people's movement for safeguarding India's most sacred river.
 This spectacular initiative has not only raised awareness but has also forged
19.03.2025 1302
a deep emotional and cultural bond between the people and the river that
has nourished India for centuries. The Ganga is not just a river, it is the
lifeline of India, a sacred entity, and an economic backbone.
Jal Jeevan Mission:
 Since the launch of Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) - Har Ghar Jal in 2019 under
the visionary leadership of the BJP-led NDA government, India has
witnessed a historic transformation in rural water supply. Today it delivers
tap water to an astounding 12.20 crore additional rural households, a
monumental leap that now covers 79.74% of rural India. Today, 15.44 crore
families no longer have to struggle for clean drinking water, their children no
longer trek miles for a basic necessity. This mission is not just about
numbers, it's about dignity, health, and a promise fulfilled.
 A watershed moment has been reached, with 188 districts, 1,838 blocks,
1,09,996 Gram Panchayats, and 2,33,209 villages proudly reporting 'Har
Ghar Jal' status, ensuring every home has access to safe, clean water. In a
testament to efficiency and determination, 11 States and Union Territories,
including Goa, Gujarat, Punjab, and Arunachal Pradesh, have achieved
100% tap water coverage, proving that with strong leadership and
unwavering commitment, even the most ambitious dreams can become
reality.
 This is more than just a policy, it's a revolution that is quenching the thirst of
the nation, empowering women, securing children's health, and bringing
19.03.2025 1303
hope to every doorstep.
 Despite challenges like water scarcity, difficult terrains, and compliance
issues in some states, the Modi government has taken proactive steps such
as interest-free capital assistance to states, capacity-building programs like
the Nal Jal Mitra Programme, and convergence with schemes like
MGNREGS to ensure sustainability. The government has also launched the
Jal Shakti Abhiyan: Catch the Rain campaign, with the 2024 theme "Nari
Shakti se Jal Shakti", recognizing the crucial role of women in water
conservation. With the extension of JJM until 2028 and enhanced budgetary
support, the BJP-led government remains dedicated to ensuring universal
tap water access, transforming rural lives, and fulfilling its vision of a Viksit
Bharat.
Swachh Bharat Mission - Grameen (SBM-G)7:
 The mission achieved its first milestone in 2019 when 100% of villages
across all 36 States and UTs were declared Open Defecation Free (ODF),
building on this success, Phase II of SBM-G, launched in 2020, has made
extraordinary strides in ensuring ODF sustainability, solid and liquid waste
management, and holistic village cleanliness.
 As of today, a remarkable 460% increment in ODF Plus villages from 1 lakh
in 2022 to over 5.61 lakh in 2024, with over 69% of these having achieved
ODF Plus Model status. More than 11.77 crore Individual Household Latrines
(IHHLs) and 2.49 lakh Community Sanitary Complexes (CSCs) have been
19.03.2025 1304
constructed, ensuring universal sanitation access in rural India. The
Swachhata Hi Seva campaign (2024) witnessed record- breaking
participation of over 30 crore people, demonstrating how deeply this
movement has engaged the public. Moreover, a groundbreaking study
published in Nature (2024) confirms that SBM-G has directly contributed to
reducing infant and under-five mortality rates, saving 60,000-70,000 infant
lives annually, a monumental achievement in public health.
 To further accelerate this mission, the Budget Estimate (BE) for 2025-26
stands at 7,192 crore, reinforcing the government's unwavering commitment
to rural sanitation and hygiene. With a strong data-driven approach, real-time
monitoring through the SBM- G dashboard, and impactful public
engagement campaigns, the BJP and NDA government have ensured that
Swachh Bharat is not just a mission but a people's movement, bringing
cleanliness, dignity, and better health to every corner of rural India.
Ken-Betwa Interlinking Project (KBLP):
 India's unpredictable climate has caused immense loss of lives, livelihoods,
and hardships for farmers, especially in drought-prone regions. Recognizing
this crisis, Prime Minister Narendra Modi took a historic step in 2024 by
inaugurating the Ken-Betwa River Linking Project (KBLP), India's first under
the National Perspective Plan. This transformative project is set to irrigate
10.62 lakh hectares of land, bringing relief to farmers across the
Bundelkhand region of Madhya Pradesh (MP) and Uttar Pradesh (UP),
19.03.2025 1305
where recurring droughts have long plagued agriculture.
 KBLP will directly benefit districts¹º like Chhattarpur, Tikamgarh, Panna,
Sagar, Damoh, and Datia in MP, as well as Banda, Mahoba, Jhansi, and
Lalitpur in UP, ensuring reliable water supply for farming communities.
Additionally, 62 lakh people (41 lakh in MP and 21 lakh in UP) will gain
access to 194 Million Cubic Metres (MCM) of drinking water, drastically
improving living conditions. The project is also set to generate 130 MW of
renewable energy (103 MW hydropower, 27 MW solar), marking a major
step towards sustainability and energy self-sufficiency.
 Beyond irrigation and drinking water, KBLP is a game-changer for rural India,
creating jobs, revitalizing local economies, and empowering countless
farming families. This visionary initiative is not just about managing water,
it's about securing India's future, and ensuring that no farmer is left behind
in the fight against climate adversity.
Conclusion:
I would like to conclude by saying that the journey of Viksit Bharat is not a distant
dream but a reality unfolding before our own eyes. Under the decisive leadership
of the BJP-led-NDA government, India is witnessing an era of unprecedented
transformation where every drop of water, every strengthened household, and
every empowered village stands as proof of a nation- rising unstoppable and
resolute towards its destiny of greatness.
19.03.2025 1306

SHRI P. P. CHAUDHARY (PALI): I express my wholehearted support for the
Demand for Grants 2025-26 presented by our esteemed Jal Shakti Minister, Shri
C.R. Patil Ji. Under the visionary leadership of our Prime Minister, Shri Narendra
Modi Ji, the Jal Shakti Ministry has taken remarkable strides toward ensuring water
security for our nation.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1314
The total allocation of Rs. 99,503 crore to the Ministry of Jal Shakti, which is
93 per cent higher than the revised estimates for 2024-25, demonstrates the priority
our Government places on water resource management. This substantial
investment will undoubtedly accelerate our nation's progress toward
comprehensive water security.
Under the visionary leadership of our Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi Ji,
the Jal Jeevan Mission has transformed the rural landscape of India. From a mere
fraction of households with tap connections in 2019, today 15 crore households,
constituting 80 per cent of all rural households, have functional household tap water
connections. Eight States and three Union Territories have already achieved 100
per cent coverage, bringing the dream of "Har Ghar Jal" to reality. The allocation
of Rs. 67,000 crore to this mission, a 195 per cent increase over the revised
estimate for 2024-25, will further enhance our capabilities to reach every home.
The Swachh Bharat Mission-Grameen continues to make remarkable strides
with an allocation of Rs. 7,192 crore. It is heartening to note that 96 per cent of
SBM-G villages are now ODF Plus villages, and 73 per cent have self-certified as
ODF Plus Model villages. The establishment of 978 Plastic Waste Management
Units across the country further reinforces our commitment to comprehensive rural
sanitation. These achievements are a testament to Prime Minister Modi Ji's vision
of a clean and hygienic India, restoring dignity to millions of our citizens.
I commend the Department of Water Resources for its excellent work, which
is reflected in its increased allocation of Rs. 25,277 crore, 17 per cent higher than
the previous year. The Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana with an allocation of
19.03.2025 1315
Rs. 8,260 crore, marking a 25 per cent increase, has already enabled the
completion of 63 per cent of irrigation projects, bringing water to millions of hectares
of agricultural land and directly benefiting our farmers. This initiative has
significantly enhanced agricultural productivity and improved rural livelihoods
across the country.
I must highlight the remarkable progress made in the Namami Gange
programme, which has been allocated Rs. 2,400 crore, a 20 per cent increase over
the previous year. The improvement in water quality of our sacred river Ganga, with
dissolved oxygen levels and biochemical oxygen demand now within acceptable
limits for almost the entire stretch, reflects the success of this initiative. The laying
of 86 per cent of the targeted sewage network demonstrates our government's
commitment to preserving our national river and cultural heritage.
The Atal Bhujal Yojana, with an allocation of Rs. 1,780 crore, a remarkable
197 per cent increase in this budget, has shown impressive results in groundwater
management. With 57% of the total outlay already released to states and high
utilization rates, this scheme exemplifies the effectiveness of community-led
initiatives in natural resource management.
As we address the challenges of water stress in our country, I am confident
that the integrated approach adopted by our Government will yield positive results.
The formation of the National Inter-Departmental Steering Committee, with
representatives from State Governments and Central Ministries is a step in the right
direction for coordinated water management.
While I wholeheartedly support these national initiatives, I would like to bring
19.03.2025 1316
to the attention of the hon. Minister some critical water management issues facing
my Constituency of Pali. These challenges, if addressed within the framework of
the Ministry's excellent initiatives, would significantly enhance water security in this
water-stressed region of Rajasthan.
Firstly, I must highlight the critical condition of small and medium dams
across Pali and parts of Jodhpur Districts that fall within my constituency. Most
blocks in the region have one to three dams that serve as lifelines for irrigation and
drinking water supply. However, many of these structures have deteriorated
significantly due to sedimentation, structural degradation, and inadequate
maintenance. The water storage capacity has reduced by approximately 30 per
cent to 40 per cent in several cases, severely compromising their efficiency.
I request the Ministry for allocation of dedicated funds under the Dam
Rehabilitation and Improvement Programme for comprehensive structural
assessment and restoration of these vital water infrastructure assets. Modern
technologies such as geomembrane lining, automated spillway gates, and
advanced seepage control measures would significantly enhance their longevity
and operational efficiency. Additionally, scientific desilting operations and
catchment area treatment using watershed management approaches would
substantially improve the water retention capacity. These interventions would align
perfectly with our government's commitment to water conservation and
management.
Secondly, I propose the development of a model integrated dam system in
Pali District, implementing the concept of dam interlinking at the micro level. This
19.03.2025 1317
innovative approach would involve hydrological assessment, topographical
mapping, and the construction of reinforced gravity-flow water transfer
mechanisms between strategically located dams. Such a system would optimize
water distribution, minimize transmission losses, and significantly reduce the
distance between irrigation sources and agricultural fields, thereby enhancing
water use efficiency by an estimated 25 per cent 30 per cent.
This model dam network could serve as a pioneering demonstration project
for the entire Rajasthan region, incorporating water harvesting structures, solar-
powered pumping systems, and real-time monitoring through IoT-based sensors. I
request an allocation of approximately Rs. 75 to Rs. 100 crore for this integrated
water management system, which would directly benefit thousands of farmers in
the region.
My third point relates to the larger River Interlinking Project that our
Government has championed. The dam interlinking initiative in Pali could serve as
a valuable precursor and complementary component to the broader river
interlinking vision. The hydraulic engineering knowledge, water balance
methodologies, and environmental impact mitigation techniques developed
through our local project would provide valuable inputs for similar initiatives across
the country. I request the Ministry to consider allocating Rs. 25 crore to Rs. 30 crore
specifically for technical studies and pilot implementation of dam interlinking
structures in Pali District.
Perhaps the most pressing concern in my constituency is the severe
pollution of the Bandi River and Nehra Dam due to untreated effluents from textile
19.03.2025 1318
industries in Pali, Jodhpur, and Balotra.
I propose a three-pronged approach to address this critical issue. First, the
establishment of advanced Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETPs) with
tertiary treatment capabilities for the textile clusters. Second, the implementation of
a closed-loop water recycling system where treated water from these industries
could be channeled to the recently established refinery in Balotra, which currently
draws fresh water from the Rajasthan Canal. This would create a sustainable
industrial symbiosis, conserving approximately 10-15 million liters of fresh water
daily.
Third, I recommend the development of a comprehensive river rejuvenation
plan for the Bandi River, incorporating bioremediation techniques, constructed
wetlands, and riparian zone restoration. This integrated approach would not only
address the immediate pollution concerns but establish a sustainable water
management model that balances industrial requirements with ecological
preservation.
I urge the Ministry to consider allocating Rs. 150 crore to Rs. 200 crore under
the National River Conservation Plan specifically for the Bandi River Basin, which
would transform the water quality situation while preserving the economically vital
textile industry that provides livelihood to thousands of families.
These proposals from my constituency represent strategic interventions that
align perfectly with the Ministry's broader objectives of water conservation,
equitable distribution, pollution control, and sustainable management. The
implementation of these projects would directly benefit approximately lakhs of
19.03.2025 1319
citizens while establishing replicable models for similar water-stressed regions
across the country.
I express my sincere appreciation to the Hon'ble Minister Shri C. R. Patil Ji
for his exemplary leadership of this vital ministry. The transformative initiatives
outlined in this budget will undoubtedly propel our nation toward greater water
security and prosperity. Under the visionary leadership of our Prime Minister Shri
Narendra Modi Ji, who has elevated water conservation to a Jan Andolan a
people's movement India is witnessing an unprecedented water revolution. His
foresight in creating the integrated Ministry of Jal Shakti and his personal
commitment to ensuring "Har Ghar Jal" demonstrates his deep understanding that
water security is fundamental to our nation's development and the welfare of our
citizens.
I am confident that the hon. Minister, guided by the Prime Minister's vision of
"Sabka Saath, Sabka Vikas, Sabka Vishwas," will give due consideration to these
proposals that address both immediate challenges and long-term sustainability
goals.
With these words, I wholeheartedly support the Demand for Grants for the
Ministry of Jal Shakti for the year 2025-26.
Thank you.

SHRI AMAR SHARADRAO KALE (WARDHA): I am grateful to you and the party
leadership for giving me an opportunity to express my views on demands for grants
 English translation of the speech originally laid on the Table in Marathi.
19.03.2025 1320
under the control of the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
India's population is 17 per cent of the world's total population. But, we have
access to only 4 per cent of the world's fresh (drinking) water sources. Therefore,
conservation and sustainable use of water resources is very important for us. The
issue of water resources is very important and talking about this issue is a matter
of loss for me as a farmer.
It is said - "Water is life. Where there is no water there is no life." Water is
the most basic need of human life. It is a part of the Constitution of India Article 21
enshrines the right to life and inviolability of human rights. The biggest scheme of
the Ministry is Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM). Prime Minister Narendra Modi launched
this mission on August 15, 2019. It is promised to provide tap water to 19 crore
rural households and public institutions by 2024. Even today if you go to the website
of the Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation, Ministry of Jal Shakti India, it
clearly says- "Water for every household by 2024." But, this is also a part of a long
list of countless unfulfilled promises by the government as we all know. As of 16
March 2025, and this is data taken from your JJM dashboard, only 80 per cent of
households have tap water connections. According to official guidelines, a water
supply is considered fully functional only when the tap connections are working
regularly, there is sufficient water available, and the water quality is up to the
prescribed standards. The JJM guidelines also say that at least 55 liters of water
should be provided per person per day. Water supply will be considered regular
only if it is done daily or on a fixed schedule. Accordingly it should be available
throughout the year. But, how many houses have got the tap water connections
19.03.2025 1321
and whether the guidelines being followed?
Most of the rural households are falsely claimed by the government to have
been given water connection. If you admire its grand dashboard, if you look at it, it
shows that 80% households across India have been given tap water connection.
But, if you go and ask people, millions of people across the country are still
struggling with water scarcity. Sir, the pipelines have been laid, but there is no water
- they are just for show off and have become objects. Taps are installed, but no
water comes out of them. That is contrary to your own instructions. But, all paper
tap connections are shown as "active" on paper. Another problem is - if water does
come, the quality is very poor. A total of 66 lakh samples were tested in 2024-25,
out of which 3.3 lakh (5 per cent) samples were found to be contaminated. I have
to ask - who was this plan ultimately made for? Did not this scheme meant to help
the poor, weak and needy? Or NRR was only for those contractors - so they could
make more money laying pipelines. Can we make our profit by contracting?
In the year 2020-21, the second phase of SBM-G was launched, which aims
to convert all villages from ODF (Open Defecation Free) to ODF Plus by 2024-25.
The model had to be changed in the villages. But, this too turned out to be another
unfulfilled promise. under SBM-G Phase plastic waste management is a major
target. According to this, plastic which cannot be recycled will be sent to Plastic
Water Management Unit (PWMU) at block level. However, by December 2024,
there will be only 978 MWWUs across the country. Operations are performed. Sir,
there are more than 7200 blocks in the country. 13 states and union territories
including Assam, Haryana and Rajasthan have no MWMU. There are only 21
19.03.2025 1322
blocks selected in Maharashtra which has more than 300 blocks. These are
physical route management units. How are you planning to achieve the objectives
of any scheme with such a poor performance? The provision in the budget also
does not show hope. In 2022-23, SBM-G rural budget was reduced by 28 per cent
compared to 2021-22. From 2022-23, the Center will provide SBM-G to the States
and Union Territories to release less than 50 per cent of the amount allocated for
the scheme.
Sufficient funds, in 2022-23, for Maharashtra (including Haryana, Odisha and
Telangana) was not given, how to work without money?
Atal Bhujal Yojna (ABY) aims to provide water resources to seven water
stressed states. Groundwater resource management has to be improved through
community-based techniques. Between 2020 and 2025, only 57 per cent (Rs.
3,421 crore out of Rs. 6,000 crore) of the total sanctioned amount was disbursed
to states. Even Maharashtra, which has utilized 99 per cent of the funds so far, has
only issued 55 per cent of the funds. North Maharashtra and Marathwada have
been suffering from drought-like conditions for a long time. In 2018, NABARD rated
the availability of water resources in Maharashtra as 'critical' and classified as
'critically endangered'. Most of the agricultural areas in the state depend on rain
water. Poor farmers suffer the most from climate change and increased rainfall
uncertainty. Even today, the net irrigated area in Maharashtra is only 31.28 lakh
hectares, while the total sown area is 167.22 lakh hectares. The non-irrigated area
is only 18%, much lower than the national average of 50 per cent. Relief has been
repeatedly called for in drought-stricken areas, but no concrete steps have been
19.03.2025 1323
taken yet. According to the official statistics of the National Crime Records Bureau
(NCRB), Maharashtra has the highest rate of farmer suicides. Why has the
government ignored this serious question so far? I demand that additional funds be
allocated to improve availability and irrigation, which I hope will solve the acute
water crisis in Maharashtra.
Any additional financial demand raised at state level or parliamentary
constituency level can be added here. Jai Hind!. Jai Maharashtra.

SUSHRI S. JOTHIMANI (KARUR): I am grateful for this opportunity to present
my views on the Demands for Grants for Jal Shakti. The Ministry had set an
ambitious target of providing safe and adequate drinking water through individual
household tap connections by 2024 to all households in rural areas. But it has fallen
short of this target by around four crores. Despite this, the revised estimate of
expenditure on the scheme in 2024-25 was Rs. 22,694 crore, the lowest-ever
allocation. The budget estimate for 2025-26 is 5 per cent lower than the budget
estimate of 2024-25. It is also not enough to provide tap connections; it is also
necessary to ensure that these taps provide drinking water. Concerningly, only
about one-sixth of India’s roughly 192 million households across 600,000 villages
had a functional water tap. Further, the penetration of the scheme is concentrated
in a portion of States, while some States like Madhya Pradesh, Andhra Pradesh
and Manipur are lagging. The success of the scheme cannot be ensured without
addressing issues without addressing implementation issues like depleting
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1324
groundwater reserves, contamination, and terrain challenges.
Coming to Swachh Bharat Mission-Grameen (SBM-G) the budgeted
estimates have decreased drastically since 2019. In 2019-20, the estimated budget
allocation towards this scheme was 35 per cent less than in the previous year. It
decreased again, by 28 per cent in 2022-23. The actual expenditure has been even
less than the budget estimates for the last five years. SGB-M Phase II focuses on
plastic waste management and calls for the setting up of plastic waste management
units (PWMUs) at the block level. However, there are no operational PWMUs in 13
States and Union Territories.
The Finance Ministry’s decision to levy a 7 per cent interest rate in case
States delay transferring funds to the nodal account may be well-intentioned. But it
can prove counterproductive for States that are facing resource crunches.
Atal Bhujal Yojana must be expanded to cover more water-stressed areas
across the country. Training for efficient water use must be amped up at all levels.
And communities should be educated on simple methods of water conservation to
avoid overreliance on groundwater. India extracts more groundwater than any
other country in the world; but instead of conserving it, we are depleting it at an
alarming rate. The Atal Bhujal Yojana, meant to address this crisis, has been a
failure due to poor implementation.
Over 60 per cent of districts in India are facing severe groundwater depletion.
Unregulated industrial extraction continues unabated. Rainwater harvesting laws
exist on paper but are rarely enforced. The Government needs to wake up to this
crisis. Without urgent groundwater conservation measures, future generations will
19.03.2025 1325
be left with barren lands and dry wells.
Launched with much fanfare and thousands of crores in funding, yet the
Ganga remains polluted. The same is true for the Yamuna, Godavari, and Cauvery,
which are choking with industrial waste and untreated sewage.
Sewage treatment plants (STPs) are underperforming or non-functional.
Industries continue to dump pollutants into rivers with little to no accountability.
Despite huge investments, independent reports show little improvement in water
quality. Cleaning our rivers is not just about spending money – it requires strong
enforcement, transparency, and scientific planning, all of which are missing under
this Government.
India is already experiencing more frequent floods and droughts due to
climate change, yet the Government has no comprehensive strategy for climate-
resilient water management.
Drought-prone regions lack proper water conservation measures. Flood-
affected areas continue to suffer from poor infrastructure and disaster planning.
There is no long-term policy for managing extreme weather impacts on water
resources. The future of India’s water security depends on serious climate action
and sustainable planning – not just short-term, election-driven schemes.
Water is life. It is not just about infrastructure or budgets – it is about the
dignity, health, and survival of our people. But this Government has failed to provide
a real, long-term solution to India’s growing water crisis. I would like to make the
following suggestions:
1. A complete audit of Jal Jeevan Mission to assess actual progress on the
19.03.2025 1326
ground.
2. Strict groundwater regulations with penalties for overuse.
3. A scientific and accountable approach to river cleaning.
4. Decentralized, community-driven water conservation efforts.
5. A comprehensive, climate-resilient national water policy.
I would also like to take this opportunity to bring to the hon. Minister’s attention
a critical issue affecting my constituency.
The Cauvery Integrated Drinking Water Project for Manapparai and Kulithalai
Assembly constituencies is an urgent necessity for the people of this region. The
Government of Tamil Nadu has already announced this project in the State
Assembly and is committed to its implementation. However, for the project to move
forward without delay, I request the immediate release of the Union Government’s
share of Rs.590 crore.
This project will provide much-needed drinking water security to thousands of
residents, and I urge the hon. Minister to take swift action to ensure its timely
execution.
Thank you.

SHRI SACHITHANANTHAM R. (DINDIGUL): On behalf of CPI (M), I share my
views on the Demands for Grants of the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
The population growth and improving living standards will result in an ever-
increasing demand for high quality municipal and industrial water as well as an
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1327
ever-increasing volume of effluents.
The quality of the drinking water is a primary determinant of human health.
Brazil has the world’s greatest renewable water resources, estimated at
8,647 BCM. It is estimated that Russia has a second-largest resources in the globe
with volume of 4525 BCM. But India ranks eighth and produced 1.91 BCM of water.
According to the NITI Aayog’s survey, 70 per cent of the fresh water
resources were found to be contaminated and India ranked 120th out of 122
countries in terms of water quality. Furthermore, 387 districts in India were
contaminated by nitrate; 297 districts were contaminated by iron; 276 districts were
contaminated by fluoride; and 36 districts were contaminated by arsenic.
The major sources of contamination were landfills, septic tanks, leaking gas
tanks, and excessive fertilizer and pesticide use. A significant amount of untreated
domestic effluent was also discharged into local waterways. This is attributable to
the lack of adequate water treatment capacity.
A high-level multi-disciplinary technical panel within the Department of
Drinking Water and Sanitation has recommended five technologies, three for safe
drinking water and two for sanitation. These recommendations should be
implemented.
Households use a variety of water sources to meet domestic water demand
including wells, borewells, tankers, handpumps, ponds, and packaged drinking
water. Packaged drinking water is commonly known as RO water jars. As this water
is treated with reverse osmosis technology, packaged and refilled ROs are like
plastic jars.
19.03.2025 1328
Providing free piped water supply to households, especially for poor people,
is very essential.

SHRI TANGELLA UDAY SRINIVAS (KAKINADA): I express my views on the
Demand for Grants for the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
I extend my heartfelt congratulations to our hon. Prime Minister Shri
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1334
Narendra Modi Ji and the Minister of Jal Shakti Shri C.R. Patil Ji for their
unwavering commitment to water security. This commitment is reflected in the Rs.
99,503 crore allocated to the Ministry of Jal Shakti in the 2025-26 budget, a slight
increase from the previous year’s allocation of Rs. 98,714 crore. In the past few
years, we have witnessed transformative progress:
Under the Jal Jeevan Mission, 14 crore rural households now have access
to tap water-a staggering leap from just 3 crore in 2019.
The Swachh Bharat Mission (Gramin) has made India 100 per cent Open
Defecation Free (ODF), significantly reducing groundwater contamination risks.
The Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) has expanded
irrigation facilities to lakhs of hectares, ensuring that farmers get the water they
need to sustain their livelihoods.
all these achievements are commendable. But at the core of all these efforts
lies one fundamental resource-groundwater. It is the backbone of our water
security, fulfilling 60 per cent of irrigation needs, 85 per cent of rural drinking water
demand, and roughly 45 per cent to urban water consumption. Yet, while we have
focused on availability and conservation, one critical aspect remains largely
overlooked the quality of our groundwater.
Millions of Indians especially in rural areas are consuming contaminated
water. Their drinking water is polluted by fluoride, arsenic, heavy metals, and
industrial waste. Even my home state, Andhra Pradesh, is no exception to this
crisis.
All districts in Andhra Pradesh have recorded high nitrate levels in
19.03.2025 1335
groundwater, a major cause for concern.
Across India, the number of districts with excessive nitrate levels has risen
from 359 in 2017 to 440 in 2023, as per the latest Central Groundwater Board
report. This is a serious health hazard, particularly for young children, and a
growing source of environmental toxicity.
Additionally, 17 districts in Andhra Pradesh are struggling with excess
fluoride in groundwater, which is directly linked to skeletal and dental fluorosis-a
condition that leaves entire generations suffering from bone deformities and
chronic illness.
This problem is not just confined to Andhra Pradesh. Fluoride contamination
is a major concern in Rajasthan, Haryana, Karnataka, Telangana, and other parts
of the country.
Programs like Atal Bhujal Yojana and National Project on Aquifer
Management attempt to regulate and conserve groundwater, but they fall short in
addressing its deteriorating quality. We need dedicated, large-scale interventions-
not just to store water, but to ensure that every drop is free from toxins.
I take this opportunity to highlight the commitment of our Deputy Chief
Minister and Rural Water Supply Minister of Andhra Pradesh, Shri Pawan Kalyan
Garu, who has made clean and safe drinking water a top priority for the state. Under
his leadership, we are working towards comprehensive groundwater purification
measures, strengthening water quality monitoring, and implementing scientific
decontamination strategies to tackle fluoride and nitrate pollution. His vision is clear
every household in Andhra Pradesh must not only have access to water but also
19.03.2025 1336
be assured of its safety.
This fight cannot be left to the states alone. Groundwater contamination is a
national emergency that demands urgent and decisive action. I urge the
government to move beyond conservation and launch a National Mission for
Groundwater Purification.
Many nations have faced severe groundwater crises and turned their
situations around with strategic intervention. Australia, once grappling with high
salinity and contamination, implemented large-scale groundwater treatment and
recharge projects, combined with strict industrial regulations. Similarly, under this
mission, we must enforce strict pollution controls, ensuring industries and
agricultural sectors do not discharge toxins into groundwater. Real-time monitoring
systems must be deployed nationwide, allowing us to track contamination and act
swiftly.
At the regional level, we need large-scale groundwater purification plants in
critically affected areas, working alongside aquifer recharge projects to restore and
protect water sources. Decentralized treatment units must be set up at the
community level, ensuring that even the most remote villages get access to safe
drinking water. Nations like Israel have pioneered localized desalination and
filtration techniques, proving that technology-driven solutions can be game-
changers.
Finally, this mission must have dedicated funding and innovation support to
promote advanced water purification technologies and assist states in
implementing these solutions effectively.
19.03.2025 1337
Let us not wait for the crisis to deepen. Access to clean drinking water is not
a privilege it is a fundamental right. Ensuring safe groundwater is not just about
protecting health-it is about securing the future of our people, our farmers, and our
economy.
Thank you.

SHRI PRABHAKAR REDDY VEMIREDDY (NELLORE): I express my views on
the Demands pertaining to the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
Water is the soul of the Earth. We have not inherited it from our forefathers,
but on loan from our children and we have to handover to them, at least, as it was
handed over to us is what Gandhiji said. This small statement has a huge meaning
of what we have to do to optimally utilize, preserve and save this Mother Earth’s
Nectar. So, the Constitution has given this responsibility to two Departments River
Development and Ganga Rejuvenation and the Department of Drinking Water and
Sanitation of the Ministry of Jal Shakti and also to Stats.
The Ministry of Jal Shakti has been given the mandate for development and
management of water as a national resource and ensure that water is made
available, optimally, for domestic use, sanitation, irrigation, industrial use and
conserve rivers and other water bodies.
The hon. Minister’s efforts, I would say, paid off as he has got 93 per cent
higher allocation for 2025-26 when compared to 2024-25 RE. Sir, the Ministry has
got Rs.51,558 crore in 2024-25 RE and hon. FM allocated, for 2025-26, Rs.99,500
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1338
crore. So, I compliment the hon.Minister for getting sufficient money for next year
to implement various schemes and programmes of the Ministry.
This Ministry is very, very important for my State of Andhra Pradesh, not
because of Jal Jeevan Mission, Swachh Bharat Mission or PMKSY, but it is this
Ministry which is supporting my State for construction of Polavaram Project which,
once completed, transforms the face of Andhra Pradesh.
Before I make my submission on the Demands of the Ministry, I would like
to make my submissions relating to Polavaram Project. The hon. FM has given
Rs.5,936 crore for Polavaram for 2025-26. But, if one looks at the Expenditure
Budget document, the revised cost of Polavaram is proposed at Rs. 30,436 crore
and the balance Central grant for the project has been limited to Rs.12,157 crore.
All I appeal to the hon. Minister is to provide sufficient and required money for land
acquisition, rehabilitation and resettlement for displaced people and for completion
of the project as our hon. CM, Shri Chandrababu Naidu, committed to complete the
project by December, 2027.
The next point I wish to touch upon is Jal Jeevan Mission and Swachh Bharat
Mission which are the two important Centrally-sponsored Schemes of the
Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation. There is no doubt that allocation for
JJM has increased three times for the next fiscal when compared to 2024-25 RE,
but less than 5 per cent when compared to BE of 2024-25’s figure of Rs.70,163
crore. I am surprised that the scheme is yet to achieve 100 per cent household tap
water connections and could achieve only 15 crore against the target of 19 crore
connections, but how come that the Budget, when compared to BE of 2024-25, has
19.03.2025 1339
been reduced.
When it comes to Andhra Pradesh, Sir, only about 74 per cent of households
have got tap water connections and there are still 26 per cent households to get
piped drinking water. So, I appeal to the hon. Minister, since the scheme has been
extended till 2028, to cover all households in my State with tap water connections
by following the water quality standards, such as hardness, alkalinity, presence of
chloride, ammonia, nitrate, etc.
Next, I come to Swachh Bharat Mission which has remarkably transformed
our villages and the figures indicate that more than 96% of villages have been
declared as Open Defecation Free. If one looks at the allocation for this scheme,
Sir, Rs.7,200 crore was given for 2025-26; it is almost same as was given in BE
and RE of 2024-25. But, if one looks at the objectives of the scheme, we have
aimed to make all villages into ODF Plus Model Villages by March this year. But,
we have been able to convert only 73 per cent (4.3 lakh) villages. So, there is work
in progress and I suggest for consideration of the hon. Minister to push hard.
Secondly, under SBM-Grameen, we have covered only 5.62 lakh villages. But, as
per 2011 Census, we have almost 6.5 lakh villages and the present unofficial
figures show that the number of villages have crossed the 7 lakhs mark. So, I
suggest for consideration of the hon. Minister to cover the left out villages under
this Mission, but how he would do is a big challenge since 2022-23 budget
remained constant. So, I also take this opportunity to appeal to the hon.FM for more
allocation to realize the dream of Mahatma Gandhi and goals set by hon. PM Modi
ji.
19.03.2025 1340
My next point is relating to inter-linking of rivers. This project is very
important, because once rivers are interlinked, we can create irrigation potential of
additional 35 million hectares. I compliment the hon. Minister for taking up Ken-
Betwa Link Project with an estimated cost of Rs. 44,605 crores. Sir, similarly, we
have Godavari- Cauvery link project (comprising 3 link projects - Godavari
(Inchampalli) Krishna (Nagarjunasagar) link, Krishna (Nagarjunasagar) Pennar
(Somasila) link and Pennar (Somasila) - Cauvery link)} which has also been
identified as Priority Link Project. I am given to understand that Chhattisgarh which
was opposing the project earlier now agreed in the 17th meeting of the Task Force
held in March, 2023, for giving up its unutilized water share. Of course, there are
also other projects. So, all I would like to know from the hon. Minister the progress
made in DPR for linking above rivers and when the project will see the light of the
day may kindly apprised to the House while replying to the debate.
My next point is relating to Accelerated Irrigation Benefit Programme. Sir, the
Government has set a target of covering 15 million hectares under AIBP. But,
progress is not so encouraging. Out of about 100 projects, we have been able to
complete only 60 projects. Here, I would like to know from the hon. Minister details
of projects identified from AP, status of progress of each of the identified project,
target for creating additional irrigation benefit and details of irrigation benefit so
created.
Atal Bhujal Yojana is another important scheme of the Ministry for which the
World Bank has given Rs. 6,000 crores to implement in over-exploited and water-
stressed areas of the country. Sir, my Rayalaseema region in AP is water-stressed
19.03.2025 1341
and over-exploited region. But, I am given to understand that this region has not
been included under ABY. All I appeal to the hon. Minister to include Rayalaseema
region in AP under ABY. Sir, the Standing Committee in 2023 recommended that
ABY be expanded to include all other water-stressed areas across the country and
hence I appeal for including Rayalaseema under this.
Namami Gange is another important programme of the Ministry which aims
to conserve and rejuvenate Ganga and its tributaries. I am not going into how much
money has been budgeted, how much has been utilized, etc. All I wish to submit is
that Godavari in South covering Maharashtra, Telangana and Andhra Pradesh
travels about 1500 kms and is called 'Dakshin Ganga. Unfortunately, Godavari is
polluted at many places. Sir, Godavari has historical, cultural, religious and other
significance and there is a need to abate pollution, conserve and rejuvenate River
Godavari. So, I suggest for consideration of the hon. Minister that Namami
Godavari be taken up as a Flagship Programme on the lines of Namami Gange by
setting up sewerage treatment infrastructure, river-surface cleaning, afforestation,
river-front development, bio-diversity, bringing public awareness, etc., to bring back
its past glory. I also request the hon. Minister to positively consider appointing
consultant INTACH as was done in the case of Namami Gange for Namami
Godavari. This will go a long way, Sir, in achieving the various objectives once
Namami Godavari is completed.
So, these are some of the issues which I wish to touch upon with a request
to the hon. Minister to ponder over these and see that they are taken up and
considered favourably. With these submissions, I support the Demands pertaining
19.03.2025 1342
to the Ministry of Jal Shakti. Thank you.

MS. BANSURI SWARAJ (NEW DELHI): I strongly support the Demand for
Grants for the Ministry of Jal Shakti. Water is the essence of life, the force that
drives civilization, and the foundation upon which our Nation's progress stands.
Yet, for millions of Indians, access to clean and reliable water has long been a
struggle. Through the unwavering commitment of the Modi Government, we are
changing this reality, ensuring that every citizen has access to safe and clean
water.
Under the decisive and visionary leadership of Hon'ble Prime Minister Shri
Narendra Modi ji, the Government has revolutionized Bharat's approach to water
security, sanitation, and sustainability. Every household with access to piped water,
every farmer benefiting from enhanced irrigation, and every child drinking safe
water in school is a testament to the government's unwavering commitment to a
healthier and more secure future.
Today, I proudly stand before this House in support of the Rs. 2,99,503 crore
allocation for the Ministry of Jal Shakti, a bold and ambitious step towards ensuring
universal access to clean water, sanitation, and sustainable irrigation. This is not
just a budget it is a promise to the people of Bharat that their future will be secure.
Imagine a mother in a remote village, walking miles every day to fetch water
for her children. Imagine a farmer helpless as his crops wither due to water scarcity.
This was the grim reality for millions before the Modi Government launched the Jal
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1343
Jeevan Mission (JJM) in 2019. At that time, only 3.23 crore rural households
(16.8%) had tap water connections. But today, the picture has changed. As of
November 2024, an astounding 15.30 crore rural households, which is 80% of the
target, now have access to clean drinking water! And I am proud to share that as
of January 2025, the states like Goa, Gujarat, Haryana, Sikkim, and Mizoram, along
with the Union Territories of Puducherry, Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu,
and Andaman & Nicobar Islands, have achieved 100% functional household tap
connections (FHTCs). This is not just a statistic, this is the Modi Government
bringing dignity, health, and progress to every doorstep.
To accelerate this mission, the JJM budget has been strengthened to
267.000 crore this year, ensuring that by 2028, every household in Bharat will have
clean, piped water. Moreover, the Nal Jal Mitra Programme has been launched to
train local communities in maintaining water supply infrastructure, creating
thousands of jobs in the process.
Clean water and sanitation go hand in hand. When Prime Minister Modi ji
launched the Swachh Bharat Mission in 2014, it was not just a scheme, it was a
mass movement. Today, because of this mission, 96% of Bharat's villages are
Open Defecation Free. This is a landmark achievement that has transformed public
health, dignity, and hygiene for millions of Indians.
With 27,192 crore allocated for SBM-G, the focus now shifts to waste
management and sustainability. Across Bharat, 978 Plastic Waste Management
Units are operational, ensuring that waste is effectively recycled, and our
environment is protected. This is the Modi Government's commitment not just to
19.03.2025 1344
sanitation, but to a healthier and cleaner Bharat.
Water is the heartbeat of Indian agriculture. Without water, our farmers
struggle, and our nation's food security is at risk. The Modi Government is
committed to empowering our farmers through schemes like the Pradhan Mantri
Krishi Sinchai Yojana (PMKSY), which has received a 25% budget increase this
year, ensuring more irrigation facilities and efficient water use.
Additionally, 21,780 crore has been allocated to the Atal Bhujal Yojana
(ABY), focusing on groundwater conservation, a 197% increase over last year's
revised estimate. This will help water-stressed regions, ensuring that future
generations inherit a water-secure Bharat.
Our rivers are not merely water bodies, they are the lifeblood of our
civilization, deeply woven into our cultural and spiritual heritage. Maa Ganga,
revered by millions, is not just a river but a symbol of purity and sustenance.
Recognizing this, the Modi Government launched the Namami Gange Programme,
a historic initiative dedicated to rejuvenating and restoring the pristine glory of our
sacred river.
Through a multi-faceted approach that includes scientific water treatment,
pollution control, and sustainable riverfront development, the Modi Government
has made significant strides in reviving Maa Ganga's health. This year, the
programme's budget has been increased to 22,400 crore, a substantial 20% rise,
demonstrating the Government's unwavering commitment to preserving this
invaluable lifeline for future generations.
Under the visionary leadership of Hon'ble Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi
19.03.2025 1345
ji, the long overdue revival of Maa Yamuna has begun. A river that has been the
silent witness to our history, our prayers, and our dreams. For years, Maa Yamuna
suffered neglect, but now, its restoration is not just a mission, it is a promise to our
future generations. With unwavering determination, advanced pollution control, and
a collective resolve, we will restore its lost glory, ensuring that this sacred river
flows clean and vibrant once again, as a testament to our heritage and our
commitment to a cleaner, greener Bharat.
As the elected representative of the New Delhi Parliamentary Constituency,
I witness firsthand the severe water crisis that the people of Delhi endure due to
the mismanagement and neglect of the previous Aam Aadmi Party (AAP)
government. Every summer, families in Patel Nagar, Rajinder Nagar, and Karol
Bagh were left to suffer as water shortages reach critical levels. Schools struggled
to provide safe drinking water for children, and entire localities were forced to rely
on private tankers, paying exorbitant amounts for a resource that should be their
fundamental right.
Now, with a BJP government in Delhi, we finally have the opportunity to
correct these past failures and deliver real solutions. I urge the Central Government
to extend its full support in upgrading Delhi's water infrastructure, eliminating tanker
mafias, and ensuring a consistent, clean, and affordable water supply for all
residents. With the decisive leadership of Hon'ble Prime Minister Shri Narendra
Modi ji and the commitment of the BJP-led Delhi government. we will transform
Delhi's water crisis into a story of efficiency, reliability, and good governance.
Under the visionary leadership of Hon'ble Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi
19.03.2025 1346
ji, Bharat is not just ensuring access to clean water, it is securing the future of every
child, every family, and every farmer. Water is life, dignity, and hope. The ₹99,503
crore budget for the Ministry of Jal Shakti is not just a number; it is a promise, a
promise that no child will go thirsty, no village will struggle for sanitation, and no
farmer will watch his crops wither due to lack of irrigation. This is the foundation of
a New Bharat, where every drop of water fuels progress, prosperity. and pride. With
unwavering resolve, we are not just building infrastructure, we are restoring faith,
transforming lives, and shaping a future where every Bharatiya thrives.
Jai Hind! Jai Bharat!

SHRIMATI POONAMBEN MAADAM (JAMNAGAR): I express my views on the
Demands for Grants for the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
Water is the lifeline of a nation. With a growing population and evolving
consumption patterns, the demand for water is rising like never before. Managing
our water resources wisely has been the need of the hour, and this Government
has risen admirably to the challenge. I would like to thank the hon. Minister, Shri
C.R. Patil ji for the transformative initiatives undertaken to ensure equitable access,
sustainable management, and the careful conservation of this precious resource.
At the heart of this Government's governance lies the principle of
'Antyodaya', ensuring welfare reaches the last person in society. This philosophy
shapes the entire approach to water security. A clear testament to this commitment
is the substantial Rs. 99,503 crore budget allocated to the Ministry of Jal Shakti for
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1347
2025-26. This significant investment demonstrates the Government's unwavering
dedication to effective water management, conservation, and accessibility, fully
aligning with the hon. Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi ji's vision of 'Sabka Saath,
Sabka Vikas' There was a time when women and children in rural parts of India
had to walk long distances to fetch water, spending hours on this exhausting task.
However, this Government has been tirelessly working to make this struggle a thing
of the past. Through the Jal Jeevan Mission, this Government has provided tap
water connections to over 15.51 crore households, covering 80.07 per cent of the
total rural population. When the Mission began, only 3.23 crore households had
access to piped water. Today, it is a matter of great pride that eight States and
three Union Territories, including the state of Gujarat, have achieved 100 per cent
tap water coverage. To sustain this momentum, the Jal Jeevan Mission has been
extended till 2028, with a dedicated budget of Rs. 67,000 crore for 2025-26.
However, providing access to water alone is not enough, ensuring water quality is
equally vital. Currently, India has 2,160 water-testing laboratories, including 1,570
NABL-accredited labs, actively monitoring and safeguarding the quality of drinking
water. By ensuring water quality, communities are empowered, and health
outcomes are improving nationwide.
India is one of the world's largest consumers of groundwater, and its
conservation is vital for long-term water security. The Atal Bhujal Yojana is a
commendable initiative undertaken by the Government to address groundwater
depletion across seven water-stressed States through community-led
management and scientific planning. So far, 60 per cent of the targets have been
19.03.2025 1348
met, and over Rs. 3,421 crore has been released to States. Through these efforts,
we are on our way to ensuring groundwater levels are restored and used
sustainably for generations to come.
Clean water and sanitation go hand in hand. The Swachh Bharat Mission-
Grameen (SBM-G), which successfully achieved Open Defecation-Free (ODF)
status in its first phase, is now in phase two, focused on converting villages into
ODF Plus. This means ensuring solid and liquid waste management, eliminating
stagnant water, and maintaining public cleanliness. As of 27th December 2024, over
95 per cent of villages in India have been declared ODF Plus, and more than 69
per cent of villages are designated as ODF Plus Model under SBM-G. Remarkably,
there has been a 460 per cent surge in ODF Plus villages from one lakh villages in
December 2022 to over 5.61 lakh villages in December 2024. This transformation
significantly contributes to making rural India cleaner, healthier, and more
sustainable.
This Government firmly believes in educating and mobilizing communities at
the grassroot level to create a water-secure future. When people take ownership
of their water resources, conservation becomes a collective movement. Annually,
the "Jal Shakti Abhiyan: Catch the Rain" has been implemented, covering all rural
blocks and urban areas in every district across the country. Campaigns such as
this ensure that water conservation is not only a Government initiative but also a
collective responsibility shared by every citizen.
At the same time, technology is transforming the way we manage and
conserve water. Through the National Aquifer Mapping Project (NAQUIM), over 25
19.03.2025 1349
lakh square kilometers have been mapped, providing States with crucial data for
implementing scientific water conservation and recharge strategies. The Bhu-Neer
Portal, launched in September 2024, enhances transparency in groundwater
management, making data accessible for better planning and accountability.
Additionally, the FloodWatch India app (Version 2.0) delivers real-time flood
forecasts from 592 monitoring stations, significantly improving disaster
preparedness and response. These are just some examples of how this
Government is successfully harnessing the power of technology to make water
management more efficient, data-driven, and future-ready.
A decade ago, the water challenges faced by the nation were daunting. There was
limited access to clean drinking water, depleting groundwater levels, poor water
quality, and inadequate sanitation facilities. Today, these challenges are being
successfully addressed through targeted interventions, dedicated community
engagement, and the deployment of cutting-edge technology. As we navigate
towards the future, I am confident we are being steered toward a secure,
sustainable, and water-resilient future for all citizens.
I support the Demands for Grants for the Ministry of Jal Shakti. Thank you!
