SHRI G. SELVAM (KANCHIPURAM): I express my views on Demands for Grants
pertaining to Ministry of Jal Shakti. Water forms the basis of sustainability of life,
livelihood and development. There is an old saying that there is no life without water
in this Universe. While discussing this Budget, we should review that whether the
proposed expenditure is in consonance with the urgent needs and challenges
faced by our country. There is reduced allocation of funds in this Budget. This
year’s budget allocation has been increased Last year it was Rs. 95,109 crore and
this has been increased to Rs. 99,503 crore this year. For Drinking water and
Sanitation, the allocation has been reduced to Rs. 74,226 crore this as against the
last year allocation of Rs. 77,391 crore. I urge that if there is justifiable reason with
the Union Government for reducing the budget for this important department, I
request that information should be shared in this august House. An amount of Rs.
77,391 crore was allocated in the actual Budget and which was later reduced to
Rs. 29,917 crore in the revised Budget, it means that there is a reduction of almost
61 per cent. It comes to a reduction in amount by Rs. 47,474 crore which is a
shocking one. Provision of clean drinking water to the villages of India is still a
distant dream. This year allocation for Jal Jeevan Mission/National Rural Drinking
Water Scheme is less than the allocation for the year 2023-24. This year Rs. 67,000
crore has been allocated by the Union Government for Jal Jeevan Mission. But
during the year 2024-25 the allocation was Rs. 70,000 crore. I must say the actual
 English translation of speech originally laid on the Table in Tamil.
19.03.2025 1354
Budget allocation originally made for the year 2023-24 was also Rs. 70,000 crore.
But during the Budget Estimates it was revised to Rs. 22,694 crore. I fail to
understand why this Union Government is reducing the budget allocation made for
the Schemes which provide basic facilities such as drinking water to our people.
Whether crores of our people should wait for long to get drinking water? Flood
management and Border Areas Programme is an important Scheme for protecting
the infrastructure of the people living in such vulnerable areas. Earlier the Budget
was Rs. 450 crore. Then it was reduced by Rs. 50 crore and made as Rs. 400
crore. Why have you reduced Rs. 50 crore for this Scheme? Will the people living
in vulnerable areas be left in the lurch without support from the Union Government
and at the disposal of nature? These are not just reduction in figures or funds. They
are just making a dent on the hopes and lives of the people of this country. Budget
is not just an economic document. This is a moral commitment for the people.
Water is not a luxury. Sanitation is not a thought for backwardness. Spending on
flood management is not a waste of expenditure. These are spending for ensuring
quality life in our country. Dravidian Model of Governance under the leadership of
hon. Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu has been implementing Kalaignar Women’s
Rights Grant Scheme providing financial assistance to women of the State. This
Scheme has been followed by almost all the States of the country. In the same
way, the Union Government should formulate the Budget of the country protecting
the interest of the people by sufficient allocation of funds in lines with the Budget
presented by the Tamil Nadu Government under the able leadership of our Chief
Minister Thalapathi Thiru M.K. Stalin. Water pollution: crisis beyond scarcity. Even
19.03.2025 1355
if we get water whether it is fit for drinking? Out of the 1920 places in rivers samples
are collected and tested. Out of this, 817 samples failed in the Biochemical Oxygen
Demand test. Industrial effluents, sewage water and agricultural waste water flow
into the water bodies of India making them toxic and dangerous for consumption.
Ground water remains unsafe in hundreds of districts as they are found to contain
Fluoride, Arsenic, Nitrate and Uranium contents. Delay in release of funds from the
Union Government. Water related schemes under the Ministry of Jal Shakti are
facing problems in execution as there is inordinate delay on the part of Union
Government in releasing the funds. As of February, 2025, only 31 percent of the
funds from the share of the Union Government has been released under Jal Jeevan
Mission for the year 2024-25. This creates further delay in providing piped drinking
water connections to rural areas. Why the fund for an important Scheme of the
Government is reduced time and again? I do not know whether under the Revised
Estimates of the Budget, whether the fund allocation be reduced? Atal Bhujal
Yojana ABY is implemented in 7 States of India where there is water scarcity. It
was found by the Parliamentary Standing Committee on Water Resources in the
year 2023 that only 37 per cent of the States are covered under this Scheme.
States having severe water scarcity like Tamil Nadu, Punjab, Himachal Pradesh
and Delhi have been excluded from this Scheme. The Standing Committee of
Parliament recommended for inclusion of all the water scarcity States in the
Programme. This Committee of Parliament has even noted that some States which
have severe water scarcity are not listed under this Atal Bhujal Scheme. Union
Government should allocate special funds for treatment of waste water, rainwater
19.03.2025 1356
harvesting in urban areas, urban river rejuvenation programmes. Although Jal
Jeevan Mission has a set target of providing potable pipe water to all the
households of our country, the successful implementation is delayed due to the
delay in release of funds from the Union Government to the States concerned.
Without proper monitoring and accountability mechanisms in place, there is a risk
of this Scheme being changed into a failure one. Moreover, climate change and
unpredictable monsoon have increased our water woes. It has also created an
impact on both drought and flood. Instead of providing temporary relief at times of
disaster, Union Government should prioritize climate change resilient
infrastructure. Desalination plants at places of water scarcity, exchanges between
reservoirs, big scale afforestation should be given importance. Instead of being a
mute spectator, the Union Government should take urgent corrective measures for
water conservation, equitable distribution of water and in talking climate change for
all the States of the country. Water conservation, beyond all political compulsions,
should act as an important pillar of sustainable development. I urge that in the
footsteps of Dravidian Model of Governance led by our Revered leader Hon Chief
Minister of Tamil Nadu Thalapathi Thiru M.K. Stalin, we should work with an aim of
providing everything for everybody. Thank you.

SHRI BHASKAR MURLIDHAR BHAGARE (DINDORI): In my Dindori Lok Sabha
constituency, Dindoripeth, Surgana and Kalvan are tribal dominated tehsils. It
receives about 2500-3000 mm of rainfall during Monsoon, but many villages do not
get drinking water in summer. There is no water available for agriculture, citizens
are migrating in large numbers. Therefore, funds should be approved and water
supply project should be set up under the Nar-Par-Tapi Scheme to meet the needs
of this area, and also reserve excess water for the locals.
In addition to this, there is a severe drought condition in Chandwad, Deora,
Nandgaon, Yeola and Malegaon Tehsil. Therefore, the water schemes for Girna
and Godavari valley should be approved and excess water should be made
available to the farmers and villagers of this area.
Under “Jaljeevan Mission” water supply schemes were approved in many
 English translation of the speech originally laid on the Table in Marathi.
19.03.2025 1360
villages of my Dindori Lok Sabha constituency. But, while completing the project,
it was necessary to dig a well first and then do the following work if water was
available. However, by doing other work first, like installing a water tank and laying
a pipeline, the contractors, in collusion with the corrupt officials, withdrew the
money for the said work and pretended that the work was completed on paper.
Even today, those villages do not get drinking water Therefore, investigation of the
scheme should be done and strict action should be taken against the culprits.
In 2027, Mahakumbh Mela will be organized on the banks of Godavari river
and billions of devotees will be coming here for ‘holy dip’. But, at present, the river
is completely polluted.
Due to sewage water from Nashik Municipal Corporation area and
contaminated water drained from industries, Godavari river is completely polluted.
The river basin in Nashik and Niphad Tehsil, it is completely covered by waterfowl.
Therefore, there is a foul smell to this water and the colour of the water has also
become black. The fishes and small animals are dying. Drinking water has been
contaminated in the villages near the river. So, your Ministry should allot sufficient
funds for cleaning of Godavari river.
Thank you.

SHRI DHAIRYASHEEL RAJSINH MOHITE-PATIL (MADHA): I express my
views on demands for grants under the control of the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
India's population is 17 per cent of the world's total population. But, we have
 English translation of the speech originally laid on the Table in Marathi.
19.03.2025 1361
access to only 4 per cent of the world's fresh (drinking) water sources. Therefore,
conservation and sustainable use of water resources is very important for us. The
issue of water resources is very important and talking about this issue is a matter
of loss for me as a farmer.
It is said - "Water is life. Where there is no water there is no life." Water is
the most basic need of human life. It is a part of the Constitution of India Article 21
enshrines the right to life and inviolability of human rights. The biggest scheme of
the Ministry is Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM). Prime Minister Narendra Modi launched
this mission on August 15, 2019. It is promised to provide tap water to 19 crore
rural households and public institutions by 2024. Even today if you go to the website
of the Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation, Ministry of Jal Shakti India, it
clearly says- "Water for every household by 2024." But, this is also a part of a long
list of countless unfulfilled promises by the government as we all know. As of 16
March 2025, and this is data taken from your JJM dashboard, only 80 per cent of
households have tap water connections. According to official guidelines, a water
supply is considered fully functional only when the tap connections are working
regularly, there is sufficient water available, and the water quality is up to the
prescribed standards. The JJM guidelines also say that at least 55 liters of water
should be provided per person per day. Water supply will be considered regular
only if it is done daily or on a fixed schedule. Accordingly it should be available
throughout the year. But, how many houses have got the tap water connections
and whether the guidelines being followed?
Most of the rural households are falsely claimed by the government to have
19.03.2025 1362
been given water connection. If you admire its grand dashboard, if you look at it, it
shows that 80 per cent households across India have been given tap water
connection. But, if you go and ask people, millions of people across the country are
still struggling with water scarcity. Sir, the pipelines have been laid, but there is no
water - they are just for show off and have become objects. Taps are installed, but
no water comes out of them. That is contrary to your own instructions. But, all paper
tap connections are shown as "active" on paper. Another problem is - if water does
come, the quality is very poor. A total of 66 lakh samples were tested in 2024-25,
out of which 3.3 lakh (5 per cent) samples were found to be contaminated. I have
to ask - who was this plan ultimately made for? Did not this scheme meant to help
the poor, weak and needy? Or NRR was only for those contractors - so they could
make more money laying pipelines. Can we make our profit by contracting?
In the year 2020-21, the second phase of SBM-G was launched, which aims
to convert all villages from ODF (Open Defecation Free) to ODF Plus by 2024-25.
The model had to be changed in the villages. But, this too turned out to be another
unfulfilled promise. under SBM-G Phase plastic waste management is a major
target. According to this, plastic which cannot be recycled will be sent to Plastic
Water Management Unit (PWMU) at block level. However, by December 2024,
there will be only 978 MWWUs across the country. Operations are performed. Sir,
there are more than 7200 blocks in the country. 13 states and union territories
including Assam, Haryana and Rajasthan have no MWMU. There are only 21
blocks selected in Maharashtra which has more than 300 blocks. These are
physical route management units. How are you planning to achieve the objectives
19.03.2025 1363
of any scheme with such a poor performance? The provision in the budget also
does not show hope. In 2022-23, SBM-G rural budget was reduced by 28 per cent
compared to 2021-22. From 2022-23, the Center will provide SBM-G to the States
and Union Territories to release less than 50 per cent of the amount allocated for
the scheme.
Sufficient funds, in 2022-23, for Maharashtra (including Haryana, Odisha and
Telangana) was not given, how to work without money?
Atal Bhujal Yojna (ABY) aims to provide water resources to seven water
stressed states. Groundwater resource management has to be improved through
community-based techniques. Between 2020 and 2025, only 57 per cent (Rs.
3,421 crore out of Rs. 6,000 crore) of the total sanctioned amount was disbursed
to states. Even Maharashtra, which has utilized 99 per cent of the funds so far, has
only issued 55 per cent of the funds. North Maharashtra and Marathwada have
been suffering from drought-like conditions for a long time. In 2018, NABARD rated
the availability of water resources in Maharashtra as 'critical' and classified as
'critically endangered'. Most of the agricultural areas in the state depend on rain
water. Poor farmers suffer the most from climate change and increased rainfall
uncertainty. Even today, the net irrigated area in Maharashtra is only 31.28 lakh
hectares, while the total sown area is 167.22 lakh hectares. The non-irrigated area
is only 18 per cent, much lower than the national average of 50 per cent. Relief has
been repeatedly called for in drought-stricken areas, but no concrete steps have
been taken yet. According to the official statistics of the National Crime Records
Bureau (NCRB), Maharashtra has the highest rate of farmer suicides. Why has the
19.03.2025 1364
government ignored this serious question so far? I demand that additional funds be
allocated to improve availability and irrigation, which I hope will solve the acute
water crisis in Maharashtra. Another serious issue that I would like to raise is the
pollution of water sources. During 2019-21, 817 out of 1,920 river sites monitored
were unable to meet the biological oxygen demand (BOD) standards set for quality
(3 treatments (not more than 1 mg/litre). 311 river segments (places with frequent
flowing water) were found to be polluted. Additionally, groundwater pollution is a
major concern especially in water-scarce countries. There is a high dependence
on it. In India: 80 per cent of rural drinking water, 50 per cent of urban drinking
water and two-thirds of irrigation Water comes from ground water sources. Ground
water assessment conducted in India shows that contaminants like fluoride,
arsenic, nitrates are present in excess of permissible limits. Any additional money
raised at state level or parliamentary constituency level can be added here. Any
additional financial demand raised at state level or parliamentary constituency level
can be added here.
Jai Hind! Jai Maharashtra.

DR. THOL THIRUMAAVALAVAN (CHIDAMBARAM): The Karnataka
Government's approach to water management on the Cauvery has long been a
contentious issue between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. While Karnataka has been
constructing dams across the river, it is also attempting to impound water under
the guise of hydropower projects. Tamil Nadu has consistently opposed these
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1365
efforts, recognising the potential for Karnataka to claim a lack of water reserves
after impounding water for power generation.
Tamil Nadu's opposition dates back to the 1990s when Karnataka first
proposed setting up hydropower plants on the Cauvery. Fearing that these projects
would reduce the flow of water into Tamil Nadu, the State appealed to the Central
Government. At the time, Karnataka suggested that if the projects were undertaken
by the National Hydroelectric Power Corporation (NHPC), a Central Government
entity, Tamil Nadu's water rights would remain unaffected. However, despite the
Centre's willingness to allow NHPC to manage these projects, Karnataka ultimately
rejected the proposal.
While Karnataka has repeatedly claimed that its hydropower projects will not
impact Tamil Nadu's share of Cauvery water, experts argue otherwise. The Tamil
Nadu Government has submitted evidence to the Centre demonstrating how these
projects could significantly reduce its water supply. Two decades ago, Tamil Nadu
had already raised concerns with the Central Government regarding these projects,
yet Karnataka has continued its attempts to move forward with them.
Karnataka had planned multiple hydropower projects, including a 345 MW
plant in Sivasamudram, a 360 MW plant in Rasimanal, a 400 MW plant in
Mekedatu, and a 120 MW plant in Hogenakkal. If these plants are constructed
solely under Karnataka's authority, the electricity generated will belong entirely to
Karnataka, leaving Tamil Nadu without any share. However, if the Central
Government oversees these projects through NHPC, Tamil Nadu would be entitled
to a portion of the power. Recognising this, the Central Government previously
19.03.2025 1366
proposed that NHPC manage the projects, with Karnataka receiving 60 per cent of
the generated electricity and Tamil Nadu receiving 40 per cent. Karnataka,
however, has continuously opposed this arrangement.
Beyond electricity-sharing concerns, Tamil Nadu has another crucial reason
to advocate for NHPC's involvement. The late Rangarajan Kumaramangalam,
former Union Minister for Power in the Vajpayee Government, had pointed out that
allowing NHPC to manage these hydropower projects would also ensure precise
measurement of water inflows into Tamil Nadu. This, he argued, would help in
resolving the long-standing Cauvery water dispute.
The issue was brought before the Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal, where
Karnataka and Tamil Nadu discussed the possibility of NHPC's involvement. The
Tribunal, in its final award, stated that any hydropower projects on the Cauvery
must not alter or reduce Tamil Nadu's allocated monthly water quota. However,
while the Supreme Court upheld the Tribunal's ruling, it did not provide a specific
opinion on hydropower projects.
Despite these legal stipulations, Karnataka has been actively pursuing the
Sivasamudram hydropower project, even seeking approval from the Central
Government. In December 2017, the Union Environment Ministry's Technical
Assessment Committee reviewed Karnataka's hydropower project. Given that
Tamil Nadu had already challenged the Sivasamudram and Mekedatu projects in
the Supreme Court, Karnataka's persistent efforts-along with the Centre's
willingness to entertain them-amount to contempt of court.
In 2019, Karnataka completed preparatory work for the Mekedatu Dam
19.03.2025 1367
Project, submitting a Detailed Project Report (DPR) to the Central Water
Commission (CWC). The project, estimated to cost Rs.14,000 crore, proposes a
dam capacity of 66 TMC. Karnataka argues that this project would regulate water
flow, prevent excess water from flowing into the Bay of Bengal, and benefit both
states by ensuring a steady release as per the Supreme Court's 2018 ruling, which
mandates that Karnataka release 177.25 TMC annually to Tamil Nadu. However,
Tamil Nadu contends that the dam would disrupt the natural flow of the Cauvery,
severely impacting its farmers and violating both the Cauvery Water Disputes
Tribunal and Supreme Court directives. The project would give Karnataka
increased control over water releases, raising concerns that it could manipulate the
flow to its advantage.
On February 7, 2025, the Union Government clarified that it has not granted
any Inter-State River Water Disputes Act, such projects require the consent of all
basin process the DPR, as approval from the Cauvery Water Management
Authority (CWMA) from the Ministry of Environment, Forest, and Climate Change
is pending, as the project approval for new dams on the Cauvery, a significant
setback for Karnataka. Under the states-especially Tamil Nadu-which has not been
obtained. The CWC has yet to remains stalled due to Tamil Nadu's opposition.
Additionally, environmental clearance would submerge over 5,000 hectares,
including ecologically sensitive areas such as the Cauvery Wildlife Sanctuary.
On behalf of VCK, I demand that the Centre revoke any preparatory
permissions granted to Karnataka for construction of dams in Cauvery. Thank you.


SHRI BALASHOWRY VALLABHANENI (MACHILIPATNAM): At the outset, I on
behalf of my Party Jansena and myself I express my sincere appreciation to the
Hon'ble Finance Minister and the Union Government for the significant allocation
made to the Ministry of Jal Shakti in the Union Budget 2025-26. The allocation of
Rs. 99,503 crore to the Ministry is a testament to the government's unwavering
commitment to ensuring water security, promoting sanitation, and addressing the
critical challenges of water governance in our country.
The substantial provision of Rs. 67,000 crore for the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)
reflects the government's resolve to achieve the vision of "Har Ghar Jal" by
providing functional tap water connections to all rural households.
As on January 2025, 15 crore rural households around 80%-have been covered.
With the extension of the mission until 2028, we remain hopeful that this goal will
soon become a reality.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1372
Similarly, the continued focus on the Swachh Bharat Mission Grameen, with an
allocation of Rs. 7,192 crore, reinforces the government's commitment to rural
sanitation and waste management, strengthening the health and dignity of our rural
population.
I also wish to highlight the allocations made towards river rejuvenation and
interlinking projects. Rs. 2,400 crore for Namami Gange and Rs. 3,400 crore for
river interlinking projects will go a long way in addressing pollution, conserving
rivers, and optimizing our water resources for agriculture and drinking purposes.
I especially welcome the increased outlay of Rs. 8,260 crore under the Pradhan
Mantri Krishi Sinchayi Yojana (PMKSY) and the attention given to groundwater
management through the Atal Bhujal Yojana.
These steps are crucial to sustaining our agriculture and rural economy, particularly
in drought-prone areas.
I would also like to take this opportunity to express my gratitude for the support
extended to my home state i.e. Andhra Pradesh, over the past years under these
flagship programs. Notably, Andhra Pradesh has made significant strides in JJM,
with over 92% of households receiving adequate quantity of water and 90%
receiving potable water, as per the Functionality Assessment Report.
During the financial year 2023-24, Andhra Pradesh received Rs. 794 crore as the
central share for JJM, with a further allocation of Rs. 2,521 crore in 2024-25. We
acknowledge the central government's support, which has been instrumental in
advancing rural water supply projects and sanitation efforts in the state.
In this regard, I would like to place certain concerns related to my parliamentary
19.03.2025 1373
constituency i.e. Machilipatnam, (Krishna District) and my state in general.
It pains me to say that even after 75 years of independence; we are still struggling
to provide safe and clean drinking water to every citizen of our country.
However, I truly appreciate that under the visionary Jal Jeevan Mission, our
government is committed to ensuring that every household receives safe and clean
drinking water.
We are blessed with the Krishna River flowing through our state. Yet, it is
unfortunate that nearly 500 TMC of Krishna river water flows into the sea every
year, going unutilized.
On the other hand, many assembly segments in my district like
* Ganavaram, Gudivada, Pedana, Avanigadda, Pamarru, Penamarulu
* Even my district Head Quarters - Machilipatnam,
face drought-like situations year after year. This irony deeply concerns us while
precious Krishna River Water flows wastefully, into sea and people in our region
suffer from acute water scarcity.
Additionally, Sir, we are also failing to capture and utilize rainwater effectively. My
district receives good rainfall during the monsoon, but due to lack of proper
infrastructure for rainwater harvesting, this water too is lost.
There is an urgent need for a comprehensive plan and central support to develop
rainwater harvesting infrastructure to conserve this resource for drinking and
irrigation purposes.
I request the government to kindly consider this seriously.
One of the most important project in AP is the Polavaram Irrigation Project, which
19.03.2025 1374
is a lifeline for our state.
This project is required to be completed well on time and a close monitoring with
timely sanctioning of financial assistance is much requested.
In addition to above, my Hon'ble Chief Minister Shri Chandra Babu Naidu Garu and
my Deputy Chief Minister Shri Pawan Kalyan Gaur, have met Union Minister for Jal
Shakti and requested the Government of India for additional financial assistance
for completing the right and left canals of the project.
Reg. Cleaning of Rivers,
As everyone of us knows here that The Government of India fully committed
towards river rejuvenation, reflecting a strong commitment to environmental
sustainability.
My Party President And Hon'ble Deputy Chief Minister of AP embraces the same
i.e. underscoring a shared vision for ecological preservation.
Many significant rivers, including the Godavari, Krishna, Pennar, Vamsadhara, and
Nagavali passes through Andhra Pradesh.
Sir, While the Godavari River has benefited from targeted cleaning initiatives, such
as the recent sanctioning of over 288 crore under the National River Conservation
Plan,
It is very important to extend similar efforts to other vital waterways
Rivers like the Krishna and Pennar are essential to the state's agriculture, industry,
and daily life, yet they face pollution challenges that threaten their health and, by
extension, the well-being of communities relying on them.
Therefore, it is my earnest request that comprehensive cleaning projects for these
19.03.2025 1375
rivers be prioritized, with the provision of necessary financial assistance, to ensure
the holistic restoration of Andhra Pradesh's river ecosystems.
These canals are crucial as they are designed with a water transfer capacity of
17,500 cusecs and will benefit vast areas of agricultural land and drinking water
supply.
I sincerely urge the Union Government to kindly give due consideration to this
request and extend the required support to complete this national project at the
earliest.
Reg. Godavari-Banakacherla Interlinking of Rivers Project
Hon'ble President of India in her address (In Para 19 of the Address), early this
session has rightly mentioned about the importance of interlinking of rivers.
As we all know water is big concern for every state; Particularly drinking water;
There is a drought like situation in many states during summer;
This has to be taken up very seriously and immediately,
And Hon'ble President has rightly mentioned it in her speech;
I come from Andhra Pradesh and we have understood its importance
one such interlinking project in AP also needs to be taken up
This would provide irrigation to new Ayacut of 3 lakh hectares and stabilizing 9.1
lakh hectares of area;
Importantly, it will provide drinking water to 80 lakh people of Uttarandhra and
Rayalaseema districts and 20 TMC for industrial use.
This project links Godavari and Krishna rivers and gives immense benefits.
This interlinking is needed, because
19.03.2025 1376
Krishna river does not have reliable inflows due to excessive use by upstream
States and full utilization by the river Krishna Basin States
But, Godavari has reliable inflow and its very saddening that nearly around 3000
TMC (average) of water flows into the sea, every year.
It means, 3000 TMC of water is going waste which is unacceptable.
So, to address the irrigation and drinking water needs of whole state of Andhra
Pradesh, Krishna Delta, Uttarandhra and Rayalaseema,
Govt. of Andhra Pradesh has proposed to divert Godavari flood waters of 200 TMC
at 2 TMC per day from Polavaram to Banakacherla Regulator to overcome drought
in the State.
The project proposed in 3 Phases one is Godavari River to Krishna River; second
is from Krishna River to Bollapalle Reservoir; and, the third one is from Bollapalle
Reservoir to Banakacherla Regulator.
AP has successfully linked Vamsadhara and Nagavali Rivers and now. there is a
need to take up Godavari-Banakacherla Link Project.
CM of AP, Shri Chandrababu Naidu, discussed this project with the hon. FM. The
Concept Paper of this Project being sent to GOI
So, I appeal to the hon. PM and FM to approve this Project and provide required
funds in Grant form to complete this project which makes AP drought-proof State
in the country.
Further, given Andhra Pradesh coastal vulnerabilities a consistent financial
assistance through schemes like the Flood Management and Border Areas
Programme, is much requested.
19.03.2025 1377
Once again as we move forward, I urge the Ministry to ensure timely release of
funds and address bottlenecks in implementation to maximize the impact of these
allocations.
I also request that the state's future needs, especially in the context of our growing
population and water challenges, be duly considered.
With this I once again welcome the budgetary allocations for the Ministry of Jal
Shakti which is progressive and people-centric.
This reflect the government's vision of providing Clearn water to every household.
On behalf of my Party Jansena, Myself and the people of Andhra Pradesh, I once
again place on record our profound gratitude.
Thank you.

ADV. GOWAAL KAGADA PADAVI (NANDURBAR): I thank my party as well as
my party Leaders for giving me this opportunity to express my views on the
demands for grants. I have to state that I come from the tribal dominated
constituency in North Maharashtra. This is bordering Gujrat on one side and
Madhya Pradesh on the other.
This is a tribal constituency where the river Narmada also flows as well as the river
Tapi flows. One may wonder there might be no problems of water in this
constituency. One might have an understanding that my constituency is adequately
irrigated, surplus supply of water but that's not the reality. The reality is actually
more shocking and also disappointing. The truth is that, my Nandurbar constituency
is the poorest district in the state of Maharashtra as per recent surveys. Nandurbar
needs a separate development model, more funds and extra support from the
Central Government for development. It was not like this 11 years ago. There is lot
of help needed which I am here to seek. I seek the government's help in this regard
in almost all sectors.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1384
There were talks about joining the two rivers in my constituency. It is being objected
by people for various reasons. The Joining of rivers seems like a business objective
more than practical objective. Because this govt only keeps business as an agenda
before launching any policy.
In Nandurbar taluka in my parliamentary constituency, there is a huge drought
issue faced by farmers. This is worst during the summers. 35 years ago, one
political leader promised an ambitious project for the farmers under the name of
tapi-burai project. The drip irrigation project which was sanctioned at 70 crores has
now reached 800 crores. Even then the state government offers minimal to
negligible funds for this project. I ask the central government to fund this project
from its own budget, reignite this project, save lives of thousands of farmers and
bring green revolution to the land of north Maharashtra, parts of Gujarat and even
Madhya Pradesh. This will also stop migration of farmers and families in search of
jobs. If this project is operational, then many villages in Nandurbar tehsil, Sakri
tehsil will benefit in my constituency.
Water scarcity problems are causing the suffering of many farmers and
families in my constituency. Also, the State Government of Maharashtra is not
bothered by this. The State government wants to make Nandurbar and North
Maharashtra just like how they have damaged the Marathwada region. From being
another statistic in drought prone area in the state of Maharashtra, the Central
Government needs to pay special attention to this demand.
Secondly, the Narmada tribunal award had announced 10 TMC water to the
state of Maharashtra from the Sardar Sarovar Dam. Unfortunately, since so many
19.03.2025 1385
years, and after years of litigation, it seems that the state of Gujarat refuses to obey
the tribunal's order. I demand from this government that the central government
instruct the Gujarat state government to release 10 TMC of water to the state of
Maharashtra. This share of water rightfully and legally belongs to the state of
Maharashtra.
If the Gujarat Government does not accede to the Tribunal Award then a
contempt case should be filed against the government for disobeying the final
verdict. Strict action needs to be taken against erring officials and penalties should
be imposed as well.
If the water is released then some provisions be made for the tehsil of
Dhadgaon and Akkalkuwa and implement minor irrigation projects. This will keep
the ground water levels in the constituency intact and the rivers will have water
throughout the year. This is because the people of Dhadgaon and Akkalkuwa tehsil
have been first asked to relocate because of the dam. these should be the first to
receive certain benefits from the same. Right now also many in Manebeli don't have
potable water as well.
The central government has on time and again given silly reasons for the
failure of the Jal-Jeevan mission. The Central Government keeps clarifying that
they are only monitoring authority for JJM and the executing authority is the state,
but the Central Government is not doing its supervising as well. The State
Government also does not want to share any blame in the failure to implement the
scheme. There is a huge scam in Maharashtra regarding this and even in my
Nandurbar parliamentary constituency. The works are incomplete, the roads have
19.03.2025 1386
been dug up, there is no tap, there is no pipes, there is no tank, there is no water,
contractors have absconded the work, the works are left incomplete as well and
many more issues relating to the scheme.
My question is, is there going to be funds for repair of the roads damaged for
the JJM scheme? Is there going to be some additional funds for the completion of
the scheme? Is there going to be an addition in the number of villages that will be
covered in the scheme without completion of the previous works?
In my Nandurbar lok sabha constituency there is rampant corruption related
to the scheme, which needs to be investigated. The local body government which
was supposed to implement the scheme has committed several irregularities in the
implementation of the scheme and the same needs to be investigated by a
committee or judicial inquiry.
I request the Government to grant a package under Atal Bhujal Yojana in my
Nandurbar Parliamentary constituency. This would definitely raise the ground water
levels in my constituency and solve the oncoming agrarian crisis. I therefore
request some incentives from the Central Government and more funds for projects
in Nandurbar.
Thank you once again for letting me speak on the demand for grants.
There was an incident where a certain political leader said that the water of
the Holy-river Ganga was potable/drinkable during the Mahakumbh. This statement
comes after the National Green Tribunal in the month of January had given
warnings that the Ganges is more polluted than ever. This is also when the Central
Pollution Control Board has declared that the most parts of Prayagraj the water

was unfit for drinking and due to high levels of bacteria. Despite such warnings the

Can the government explain how does the rivers of the developed nation
remain so clean, with minimal funding, but despite Government spending multi-
crores of money Ganga has never remained clean once?
Cleaning of the River Ganga doesn't seem to be the priority of the double
engine government? Is it always election agenda?

SHRIMATI PRATIBHA SURESH DHANORKAR (CHANDRAPUR): I express my
views Supplementary Demands pertaining to Jalshakti Ministry. There is huge
water scarcity in my Chandrapur city. Dhanora and Pipri Barrage work has been
approved. You are requested to provide sufficient funds and expedite the work of
this Project.
Chandrapur District has many coal mines and due to this natural overburden,
many rivers have changed the flow in this District. Therefore, the flood like
conditions have been developed in the area near to rivers. Therefore, I request
you to pay attention to the depth of the rivers adjacent to the coal mines.
Tenders were floated for Lower Painganga Project but, as of now, the work
has been stopped. You are requested to expedite the work.
It is proposed to provide water from the right canal of Gosekhurd Project for
irrigation to the Pombhuna and Mul Tehsils. But, due to slow pace of work, the
water could not reach to the farmers. In this regard, I urge upon you to complete
this work immediately by using your good offices.
The problem of water in the summer is very serious in Maharashtra. But,
during monsoon, the rain water causes heavy damages to farms and villages. Atal
Bihari Bajpayeeji had proposed the concept of river interlinking to provide water
 English translation of the speech originally laid on the Table in Marathi.
19.03.2025 1391
throughout the year. But, the Govt. is not serious about this Scheme. If all the
rivers get inter-connected under this Scheme, the water problem will be resolved
and the farmers would get enough water for irrigation.
In the rural area, the problem of drinking water and irrigation still persists and
that is why
I would like to request you to construct large ponds in rural areas to provide
irrigation facilities.
Thank you.

CAPTAIN BRIJESH CHOWTA (DAKSHINA KANNADA): I support the Demand
for Grants for the Ministry of Jal Shakti. I would like to begin my speech by quoti
a shloka from the Rig Veda:
"Aps van tara mrutam apsu-bhesha jam”
Which means, "There is nectar in water, there is medicine in water. This verse from
the Rig Veda reminds us that water is not just a resource; it is life itself. The recently
concluded Kumbh Mela, where millions took a holy dip at the confluence of the
Ganga, Yamuna, and Saraswati, underscores the deep reverence that our
civilization holds for water.
Under the visionary leadership of Prime Minister Narendra Modi ji, the Ministry of
Jal Shakti was formed in 2019, integrating various water-related initiatives to
ensure a holistic approach towards water management, availability, and
accessibility of clean water across the country.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
19.03.2025 1392
Budgetary Allocation:
For the year 2025-26, the Ministry of Jal Shakti has been allocated Rs 99,503 crore,
which close to 2% of the total budget, underscoring the government's commitment
to addressing water-related issues.
Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation (DDWS): The DDWS has been
allocated Rs 74,226 crore, a substantial increase from the Rs 29,917 crore revised
estimate of 2024-25.
Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM): Launched in 2019, JIM aims to provide tap water to all
19 crore rural households. As of January 21, 2025, 15 crore households (80%)
have functional tap water connections. In 2025-26. IIM has been allocated Rs
67,000 crore, marking a 195% increase over the revised estimate for 2024-25.
In Karnataka, out of 1,01,31.384 households, 85,03,393 households have been
provided with a tap water connection, which is coverage of 83.93%
In my constituency of Dakshina Kannada, out of 3,34,185 households. 2,95,037
households have a tap water connection, which is 88.29% coverage.
Swachh Bharat Mission - Grameen (SBM-G): Focuses on sanitation in rural
areas, with an allocation of Rs 7,192 crore for 2025-26.
Department of Water Resources, River Development, and Ganga
Rejuvenation:
Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana (PMKSY): Rs 8,260 crore has been
allocated, a 25% increase.
River Interlinking Projects: Rs 3,400 crore has been allocated, a 13% increase.
Namami Gange Programme: Rs 2,400 crore has been allocated, a 20% increase.
19.03.2025 1393
Atal Bhujal Yojana (ABY): Rs 1,780 crore has been allocated, a significant 197%
increase from the previous year.
Achievements of the Government:
80% of rural households now have access to tap water under JJM.
The Namami Gange project has led to a significant improvement in the cleanliness.
and rejuvenation of the Ganga.
PMKSY has improved irrigation coverage across India, ensuring water security for
farmers.
Demands of the Constituency:
while significant progress has been made, I want to bring to the attention of the
House the urgent need for a Multi-Village Drinking Water Supply Scheme for
Dharmasthala and 645 other habitations in Belthangady Taluk, Dakshina Kannada
District.
Belthangady Taluk comprises 48 panchayats, of which only 17 panchayats have
access to multi-village water supply schemes. The remaining 31 panchayats rely
heavily on groundwater sources like borewells and open wells, which are often
contaminated with high iron content and deplete during summer.
To address this crisis, a new Multi-Village Water Supply Scheme has been
proposed. utilizing the recently constructed Minor Irrigation Department Dam
across the Netravathi River at Pajiradka. This project, with an estimated cost of Rs
358 crore, will provide safe and sustainable drinking water to 1.75 lakh residents
across 646 habitations.
1 urge the Hon'ble Minister to approve this project under the jal Jeevan Mission
19.03.2025 1394
and facilitate the preparation of a Detailed Project Report (DPR) at the earliest.
Conclusion: Water is the foundation of life, health, and prosperity. Under the
dynamic leadership of Prime Minister Narendra Modi ji. India has witnessed
unprecedented progress in water management and conservation. His unwavering
commitment to the welfare of the people, particularly in rural India, has ensured
that the vision of Har Ghar Jal is becoming a reality.
I strongly support the Demand for Grants for the Ministry of Jal Shakti and urge the
government to expedite the approval of essential projects like the one in
Belthangady Taluk to truly achieve Har Ghar Jal.
Jai Hind!

Swachh Bharat Mission also falls under this project within the purview of

known to people like us who reside in rural areas. Initially, efforts were made to
construct toilets in exchange for 1000 rupees only. But that was neither healthy nor
realistic. Now the government is planning to construct toilets in exchange for 12000
rupees. The state government is supposed to contribute 3000 rupees and the
central government is supposed to contribute 9000 rupees. We all know that

people can be alleviated. Certainly, this is the need of the hour. Another important

practice for the government to announce special packages to aid people who were
hit by natural calamities such as floods or cyclones in any part of the country.
However, West Bengal has repeatedly been denied fair compensation, during
Amphan or the recent floods. Through you, sir, I want to draw the attention of this
house: rivers are reversing their directions due to global warming. As a result of
which the river embankment is getting damaged. River dams have broken in
different places. I do not know whether the government or the ministry has any
plans for the reconstruction of the river dams that are broken due to the change in
the course of the river, just as the government arranges for its reconstruction in
case of natural disasters. Sir, if the government doesn't have any such plans, the
government should immediately adopt a plan for this and should take measures to
reconstruct the river dams so that they can ensure the safety of the people living in
coastal areas*.

SHRI BAPI HALDAR : As an example, I need to cite the river erosion in
Gobardhanpur village near Patharpratima, which falls under my constituency, and
a hundred acres of agricultural land from many villages are under threat of getting
submerged in the riverbed. Cargo ships commute through the Budhiganga River
near Mousuni Island in the Sagar assembly constituency. As the navigability of the
river decreases, the ship changes its course and moves closer to the banks of the
river. As a result, river erosion is increasing. As a result, agricultural land,
residential houses, schools and health centres stand the threat of going into the
river. Notable in this regard is the Gangasagar Pilgrimage, which houses the temple
of Lord Kapil Maharaj, one of the traditional pilgrimage sites of West Bengal and
India. Where lakhs of pilgrims gather on the auspicious day of Makar Sankranti
and also throughout the year. Gangasagar Mela is the second-largest human fair
in India. Our Hon. Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee has repeatedly requested the
Central Government to declare Gangasagar Mela as a National Fair. The central
government has not yet shown any foresight in that regard. Currently, due to river
erosion, Gangasagar and the nearby areas of the Kapil Maharaj Mandir are headed
towards destruction. We have seen in recent years that the central government has
spent crores of rupees for the beautification of the riverbanks in Kedarnath,
Haridwar, and Varanasi. But Gangasagar was deprived because it is situated in
West Bengal. No steps are taken to protect Gangasagar even in the present
budget. The beloved chief minister of West Bengal has tried to protect Gangasagar
*…*.English translation of this part of speech originally delivered in Bengali.
19.03.2025 1403
and she has taken measures for its beautification. She has already facilitated the
travel of pilgrims by building the Gangasagar bridge over the river Budhiganga. Sir,
through you, I request the government to protect Gangasagar in a similar vein to
the other pilgrimages. The government should immediately take steps for its
beautification. Declare the Gangasagar Mela as a national fair in recognition of the
immediate dignity and merit of the Gangasagar. The river erosion in Gangasagar
should also be declared as a national sister. The government should announce a
special package to protect Gangasagar. In conclusion, through you, I would
request the Jal Shakti Ministry to protect the lives in Sundarbans and to protect the
river embankments in Sundarbans. They should turn the river-banks into concrete
in Ghoramara Island, Gobardhanpur Coastal area, Mousuni Island, Kakdwip,
Namkhana, Bakkhali, Raidighi, Goshaba, Basanti, Kultali to provide safety to the
people living in this huge expanse of the coastal area. Thank you. Jai Hindi. Jai
Bangla.*

SHRI BASTIPATI NAGARAJU (KURNOOL): Thank you hon. Chairperson.
I stand here to express my views on demands for grants for the ministry of
the Jal Shakti for the year 2025-26. I also thank honourable Prime Minister Sri
Narendra Modi, Finance Minister Smt. Nirmala Sitharaman and our honourable
Chief Minister Nara Chandrababu Naidu for coming up with a bright and positive
budget. I praise them for their far sightedness and vision.
Jal Shakti ministry has been allocated with ₹ 99,503 crores for the financial
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Telugu.
19.03.2025 1404
year 2025-26. This budgetary allocation clearly reflects the Union government's
commitment towards solving water problems in our country. I would like to discuss
four issues pertaining to my state. Firstly, Jal Jeevan mission has effectively
tackled the drinking water crisis in rural areas by providing piped drinking water to
numerous households, which has improved health and sanitation in our villages.
Though this scheme is being implemented throughout the country at a faster pace,
our state Andhra Pradesh could not even touch the national average when it comes
to coverage of this scheme.
Due to the negligence of the previous government in our state, we are
staring at darkness. I thank the Union Government for extending this scheme till
2028 and allocating ₹67,000 crores for this scheme. This step will help Andhra
Pradesh grow stronger.
The second point is regarding Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana which
has been introduced to strengthen the irrigation infrastructure in our country, but in
Andhra Pradesh, out of eight approved projects only one project could be
completed. Though 53 crores have been sanctioned by the union government,
since 2019-20 not even a single rupee was released for these projects. I request
the union government to release these funds immediately. Similarly, I request the
union government to include Andhra Pradesh in Har Khet ko Pani scheme and
improve irrigation facilities in our state. Coming to the third Point, Atal Bhujal
Yojana has been implemented in states like Rajasthan, Karnataka and Gujarat
which improved groundwater levels. That scheme should be implemented in our
state as well, especially in Rayalaseema region. Though ₹600 crores have been
19.03.2025 1405
allocated for this scheme, only ₹76 crores could be utilised till December 2024.
Therefore, I request the government to release these funds on time so that we can
improve and save our groundwater levels.
In our state Andhra Pradesh our chief minister Nara Chandrababu Naidu is
taking ahead our prestigious Polavaram project with a lot of commitment, for which
I appreciate our leader Nara Chandrababu Naidu. This project will not only ensure
water security for our state but also will result in agriculture revolution. For the
financial year 2024-25, Polavaram project was granted ₹ 5,512 crores, whereas for
the financial year 2025-26 this has been increased to ₹ 5,936 crores, which is
commendable. But there is so much to be done. The union government has
allocated ₹12,157 crores for Polavaram Project, but funds are being released at a
slower pace and as a result the project is getting delayed. I request the union
government to release funds on time. Once we complete the Polavaram project,
this will provide irrigation facilities to lakhs of farmers and this will also increase our
agriculture produce.
We need robust water planning for the Rayalaseema region as water
scarcity is increasing day by day. Thousands of families are migrating to other
places from Rayalaseema. In this region only 25% of agricultural land is getting
irrigation facilities. Other farmers depend on rainfall for their agricultural needs. As
a part of the long-term solution, a plan for Polavaram Banagacherla river
interlinking project has been planned. In line with our Prime Minister's vision of
Vikasit Bharat by 2047, our chief minister resolved for Swarna Andhra Pradesh by
2047 and as a part of interlinking rivers Godavari, Krishna and Tungabhadra waters
19.03.2025 1406
will be linked. For this, there is a need to construct a reservoir at Ballapalli in
Guntur district and at Banagacherla in Nandyal district, which will become a lifeline
for the farmers of Rayalaseema region.
The main objective of this project is to provide drinking water for 80 Lakh
people and irrigation facilities to 7.5 Lakh acres of agricultural lands. During the
rainy season we can conserve 2 TMCs of water for 100 days. This project will cost
₹ 80,112 crores. We need 40,500 acres of agricultural land and 17,000 acres of
forest land for this project. This financial burden cannot be borne alone by the state
of Andhra Pradesh, therefore I request the union government to share this burden
by allocating additional funds so that this project can be completed on time. This is
not something confined to water projects, this is something which pertains to the
survival of Rayalaseema.
The NDA government is following the principles of federalism by giving equal
importance to all states. I am hopeful that under the leadership of our honourable
Prime Minister Narendra Modi, this project will be given priority. Our chief minister
Nara Chandrababu Naidu has provided clarity in direction for our state. The
Rayalaseema region can only prosper when the union government and state
government work collectively. Therefore, I request the union government to
provide immediate support for this important water project and bailout the
Rayalaseema region from the problem of water scarcity. Let us ensure that our
farmers need not stare at clouds for rain. Thank you, sir.

SHRI RAJABHAU PARAG PRAKASH WAJE (NASHIK): Hon’ble Chairman Sir,
thank you very much for allowing me to participate in the discussion on Demand
for Grants of the Ministry of Jal Shakti for 2025-26. Water is very essential for
farming and industry as well as all the human beings. To ensure water
conservation, river rejuvenation and to provide clean drinking water, an effective
implementation is a common responsibility for all of us. For Jal Shakti Ministry Rs
99503 crore have been allocated in this year’s budget. I hope this would help to
take care of water scarcity, ground water recharge, irrigation facilities and clean
drinking water in our country. To boost river linking projects, water table recharge
and also to improve sewage system and water treatment, a better and efficient
management must be ensured in Maharashtra as well as all over India. A priority
should be given to Godavari rejuvenation and better infrastructure development,
in view of Maha Kumbh, 2027 at Nashik.
Sir, Maharashtra is agriculture dominated state but, still the irrigation
coverage area is mere 23% and it is very less in comparison to the other Indian
States. There is a 3000 mm of rainfall every year in Konkan region but, most of
the parts of North Maharashtra, Marathwada and Vidarbha region remain dry and
face water scarcity.
To tackle this problem, Jal Shakti Ministry should boost the river interlinking
projects and also undertake special programmes for balanced redistribution of
water resources. If irrigation facilities increase, it would also help to increase
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
19.03.2025 1410
farmers’ income and help to eradicate water scarcity as well. Sir, to increase
irrigation coverage area in Maharashtra through river interlinking, following 4
projects should be declared as projects of national importance and 90% funding
should be given by Union Government for these projects.
These 4 projects are, first, Damanganga-Dare-Godawari link for the
development of Nasikh having capacity of 5 TMC. Second, Gargi-Vaitarna-
Kalbadevi link for development of Sinnar, Shirdi Third, Wainganga-Nalganga link
for the development of Vidarbha region and fourth, Nar-Par-Girna link for the
development of Jalgaon area to ensure generation of total 12.5 TMC water
through these 4 river linking projects.
Sir, for cleaning of Gadavari river, we will have to stop dumping of waste
and sewage water into it through 29 small Nallas flowing through Nasik city. We
need to control and treat it. All the main sewage line within municipal boundaries
should be repaired immediately and new sewage treatment plants should be set
up at Makhmalabad and Kamatwade. By using latest technology, modern STP
plants should be developed to minimize industrial waste.
The population of Nasik city is increasing rapidly, so the burden on water
and sewage treatment plants is also increasing. To solve this problem, the Union
Government should sanction funds for effective implementation. To repair and
improve main sewage lines, to set up new STP centers at Makhmalabad and
Kamatwade, renovation of Godavari and other ghats, and for development of
modern STP plant for treatment of polluted water, we need funding worth Rs 903
crore. Through these projects, pollution of Godavari river would be minimized.
19.03.2025 1411
Sir, improvement of water pollution of Godvari river, water management for
Kumbh Mela and to setting up STP plants, are very important issues and that is
why, I would like to request Union Government to provide sufficient funds for this
purpose immediately.
Sir, water tables have been decreased by 50% in most of the districts of
Maharashtra. To improve implementation of Atal Bhujal Mission, water
conservation projects should be expedited. Modern technology should be used
for water table recharge. Government should encourage farmers for the
cultivation of drought-tolerant crops. Rules and regulations should be framed for
water extraction and it should be implemented strictly. Jal Jeevan Mission ensures
to provide drinking water to every household but, today only 15 crore households
have been benefitted out of it. Water contamination is also a big issue. So, water
testing labs should also be set up and an uninterrupted water supply should be
ensured. Thank you.
16.00 hrs

SHRI NILESH DNYANDEV LANKE (AHMEDNAGAR): Hon’ble Chairman Sir,
Jay Shivray. I would like to thank you and my party leadership for allowing me to
speak on Demands for grants of Ministry of Jal Shakti for 2025-26. Mahatma
Gandhi has referred water as a natural resource of vital importance and it must be
conserved. Following his advice, the Union Government brought Jal Jeevan
Mission scheme and Prime Minister of India referred it as a dream project under
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
19.03.2025 1412
this scheme, 60% is central and 40% is state government share.
Har Ghar Jal is also a very important scheme of our Prime Minister and a
target of providing 55 litre water to every household has been set under this
scheme. Around 830 schemes have been sanctioned in my constituency and 972
villages have been covered under this scheme. Officials claimed that out of 830,
works of 230 schemes have been completed.
But, the ground reality is different and not even 50 schemes have been
completed yet. Approximately Rs. 1338 crore have been sanctioned for 830
schemes. Through Maharashtra Jeevan Pradhikaran for 112 schemes, Rs. 3200
crore have been sanctioned. It means more than Rs. 4538 crore have been
sanctioned for my Ahmednagar district. But, a lot of corruption cases came to light
and local villagers have sent me written complaints about it. These complaints are
about the tendering process, quality of work and payment of bills and corruption is
quite visible at first sight. works have been awarded to the ineligible contractors.
Bills have been generated of incomplete works. To lay pipes, it is necessary to dig
at least 4-5 feet, but pipes have been fitted at the depth of 1 feet only.
I have got photos and videos of these corrupt activities. I have sent a pen-
drive and photo copies to Hon’ble Minister too.; They charged for hard surface
even when the soil is soft for laying pipelines and in this way, they recovered more
amount from Government. The contractors did not purchase the desired quality
pipes and a low quality pipes have been used. These corrupt contractors charged
full amount for the construction of water tank which is having a capacity of 50
thousand liters in place of 1 lac litre. The work is still incomplete after time lapse
19.03.2025 1413
but, no action has been taken against defaulters. Their bills are cleared on time.
These kind of corrupt practices came to light at many places like at Aajnuch
in Shrigonda Tehsil, Pargaon in Nagar Tehsil, Bhagwangarh in Pathardi Tehsil and
in other 146 villages of this region.
In addition to this, at places like Meeri Tisgaon, Godhegaon, Satgaon,
Danewadi, Koregaon, Karjat, Koradgaon, Anjanwadi, Malibabhulgaon, Mathani,
Adhalgaon, Narayandoh, Tambere, Daradgaon, Shri Kolar, Nighoch etc. rampant
corruption cases are quite visible. When I seeked information’s from Zilla Parishad
in this connection, it is found that the works in only 18 villages have been
completed. The Government is protecting and supporting corrupt officials and
contractors in these cases. They have made an additional provision of Rs. 25 crore
for these schemes. If this schemes are completed, why are they making additional
provisions?
In my constituency, these schemes are not being implemented in Scheduled
Caste and tribal dominated areas . I demand to implement it urgently in these areas
too.
Lastly, I would like to request Hon’ble Jal Shakti Minister Shri C.R. Patil, to
initiate an enquiry of Jal Jeevan Mission works by appointing a Director level officer
and punish the corrupt officials and contractors by registering FIRs against them.
Thank you. Jai Shivray.

SHRI E. T. MOHAMMED BASHEER (MALAPPURAM): Madam, today we are
discussing the vital subject that is Jal Shakti. If you conduct a critical analysis, you
will be convinced of a fact. What do the authorities, authentic reports and forecasts
reveal? They reveal that India is going to face terrible water scarcity and crisis in
the coming years. You may even foresee an explosive situation in this regard. What
exactly are the reasons? There are many reasons like depleting groundwater,
erratic rainfall, climate change, river pollution, urban water stress in the major cities,
inefficient water management and destruction and vanishing of traditional water
19.03.2025 1430
sources.
Madam, I am not blaming any Government. Whether it is the State
Government or the Central Government, one fact remains unchanged, and that is
that things do not move fast. This should be taken very seriously.
Take for example, Jal Jeevan Mission in the State of Kerala. I will speak
about that only. We are trying to make a milestone in the history of its kind. The
target is to reach each household and provide drinking water. The target is to
complete it by 2028. Now extension has been given for four years.
What is the present position in Kerala? Kerala is currently at the lowest
bottom in India as far as this scheme is concerned. Both the Governments whether
it is the State Government or the Central Government are responsible for that. Now,
our goal is extended to 2028.
Even within this time, there is no guarantee that things will be all right. We
may still lag behind. During this period, there may be different kinds of discussions,
like who will do this or who will do that.
For example, in the second phase itself, guidelines from the Central
Government have not been given to the Kerala Government. That kind of disputes
are continuing there.
Similarly, the State Government now shows helplessness. They are saying
that there is no direction from the Government of India. Work is ready to tender,
but the tender process is not taking place. They are saying this and improper
approach is taken by the Government. Now, there is a terrible financial crisis. The
Central Government is not giving their share, and the State Government is also in
19.03.2025 1431
crisis; they are not providing the State Government’s share either. Those who are
entrusted with the work of the JJM are now in a very worried state. They are not
doing anything, saying that there is no money and that we are not getting the bill
passed. In that way, a kind of controversy is also going on. If we are really honest,
funds can be allotted. This kind of issue will have to be eradicated; only then will
we be able to attain our goal.
Another thing which I would like to inform you is about road cutting, which is
very necessary for the pipeline. There is time constraint, and I do not want to say
much about that. Many representations from the State Government are pending,
and that is not moving. Not only that, the roads opened for this kind of work are
remaining as they are. Restoration is not taking place. The dispute is about who
will do it and which department should handle it. The Panchayat’s President and
authorities are running from pillar to post. They are not getting money for that. That
kind of controversy is continuing. The local bodies are totally complaining about
this, and it seems to be a very serious situation in this case.
Madam, we have to think about what exactly we can do. It is a very serious
concern. Considering this, war footing level activities should be done. As I
mentioned in the beginning, it is a lifeline. Stronger regulation on groundwater use
and saving our lakes and rivers is also important. That is also very good. We have
written poetry and stories about the river, its beauty, and cleanliness. All the rivers
are polluted. That is to be taken as a very serious issue. We have to regain the
glory of our lakes and rivers. A regulatory mechanism is necessary for groundwater
usage, as I mentioned earlier. That is also an important issue. These issues will
19.03.2025 1432
have to be addressed: fund constraints, infrastructure challenges, coordination
problems, and bureaucratic and administrative hurdles. All these things will have
to be removed. Only then will we be able to attain our goal.
Coming to other matters, a public awareness campaign on how to control
water wastage should also be encouraged. We must learn lessons from past
experiences. We have had enough schemes: Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee
Yojana (PMKSY), Har Ghar Jal, Per Drop More Crop, Watershed Management,
Amrut, River Basin Management Programme, R&D, and similar initiatives. As far
as R&D is concerned, it is a very important subject with regard to Kerala. I humbly
suggest that we must have a national institute for this. There are some institutes.
My learned friend, Shri M. K. Raghavan, is sitting behind me. In his constituency,
there is CWRDM, which is doing wonderful work. I suggest that we must have a
very large institution to tackle R&D matters. Worldwide, such initiatives are
ongoing.
Madam, I do not want to take much of your time, but one thing I would like to
say is that if we ignore this, the entire mankind, not only mankind but the entire
setup of the country, will face serious consequences. We must find ways to save
our State from these difficulties. I humbly submit that the Government should take
this very seriously and find solutions for it. Thank you very much.

SHRI Y. S. AVINASH REDDY (KADAPA): Madam, Polavaram is a multi-purpose
19.03.2025 1435
irrigation project envisaged to irrigate lakhs of acres in Godavari district and parts
of Uttar Andhra. This project diverts 80 tmc of water to the Krishna River Basin to
meet the needs of the people living in Krishna Delta region, and this saving in the
Krishna Basin will be utilized for Rayalaseema project from Srisailam Dam. This
project provides 24 tmc of water for drinking and industrial needs of Vizag City. All
the above benefits can be achieved only if the Polavaram project is constructed as
per its original design of 45.72 metres height and 194 tmc of storage capacity.
Recently, the Government of India, with the consent of the present
Government of Andhra Pradesh has reduced the project height to 41.15 metres
and reduced the storage capacity from 194 tmc to 115 tmc only. The total project
cost is reduced to Rs. 30,000 crore from the previously approved amount of Rs.
59,000 crore, which is a loss of grant for Andhra Pradesh to the tune of Rs. 29,000
crore. We strongly oppose this decision, and we urge the Government to construct
the project as per its original design of 45.72 metres height and 194 tmc storage
capacity, without which all the objectives of the Polavaram project cannot be
achieved. We also request for a quick disbursement of R&R package to all the
families displaced and affected by the project. The budget allocation for the project
this year 2025-26 is Rs. 5,936 crore. We request the Government to enhance the
allocation for the speedy completion of the project.
Around five days ago, the EC was rejected by the Government of India for
the Rayalaseema Lift Irrigation project due to gross negligence and lack of
persuasion by the present Government of Andhra Pradesh. Apart from allocation
to Nagarjuna Sagar and Krishna Delta, there is an allocation of 19 tmc for SRBC,
19.03.2025 1436
29 tmc for Telu Ganga, 38 tmc for GNSS, and 15 tmc from Srisailam Dam for
drinking water purposes of Chennai. For the above projects from Pothireddypadu
head regulator, 7,000 cusecs of water can be drawn when the water level is 854
ft., whereas 44,000 cusecs can be drawn at 880 ft. level during flood days. Madam,
our neighbouring State, which is on the left side of Srisailam Dam, is generating
power at 798 ft. level. And it has also constructed two lift irrigation projects,
Palamuru Rangareddy project and Dindi List Irrigation Scheme, under which
22,000 cusecs of water is being pumped every day at 800 ft. level. Please think, if
power is generated at 798 ft. level and if 22,000 cusecs is pumped every day by
our neighbouring State at 800 ft. level, when will the water reach 880 level and how
will the Greater Rayalaseema project get its share of water? To overcome the
above problem, the foundation was laid for the Rayalaseema Lift Irrigation project
in the year 2020 by the then CM, Sri Y. S. Jagan Mohan Reddy Garu, and a
significant amount of work is completed.
This project is aimed to pump three TMCs of water everyday at 800 feet level
for Rayalaseema and Nellore projects and also for Chennai drinking water purpose.
Madam, we demand the Government of India to revisit and review and grant
permissions for this project without which Greater Rayalaseema projects will be put
to severe injustice.
Thirdly, we demand the Government of India to provide financial assistance
for Gundrevula project. This project is aimed to stabilise the historical K.C. Canal
ayacut and also provide drinking water to western mandals of Kurnool district.
Madam, at present, for 2.65 lakh acres of K.C. Canal ayacut, there is only one dam
19.03.2025 1437
on Tungabhadra, that is, Sunkesula Dam which has the capacity of only 1.5 TMC.
It is nowhere sufficient to irrigate 2.65 lakh acres of land. So, we are requesting the
Government to give financial assistance to Gundrevula Project with 15 to 20 TMC
capacity so that the K.C. Canal ayacut can be stabilised.
Recently, the NHAI sanctioned a new river overbridge to connect Nandyala
of Andhra Pradesh to Kalvakurthy of Telangana State. There is a demand from the
farmers to construct a weir, an ayacut along with this bridge. We request the hon.
Jal Shakti Minister and the hon. Minister for National Highways to consider this
proposal for construction of weir along the river overbridge at Siddheswaram, which
would not require any additional land acquisition. All it requires is, change of design
and increase in strength.
Madam, we request the National Dam Safety Authority to undertake
immediate repairs at Srisailam dam, Nagarjuna Sagar Dam and other important
dams of Andhra Pradesh. We request the Government of India to increase the
Central support for minor irrigation projects in Andhra Pradesh and also request
the Government to increase the allocation of funds under Jal Jeevan Mission for
Andhra Pradesh. With these demands, I conclude my speech.
SHRIMATI PRATIMA MONDAL (JAYNAGAR): Madam, on behalf of the All India
Trinamool Congress, I rise to speak on the Demands for Grants of the Ministry of
Jal Shakti for the year 2025-26. I would like to express my concern regarding the
functioning of the Ministry of Jal Shakti. It was envisioned that this Ministry would
be effective in addressing India's multifaceted water challenges, including water
scarcity, sanitation and the provision of clean drinking water. Madam, water is the
19.03.2025 1438
driving force of nature. It is the most precious resource and abundant compound
on earth's surface, covering more than 70 per cent of the planet. But India has only
four per cent of water whereas it is home to 17 per cent of the world population.
Now, it is evident that the ratio of availability and usage is not at the desired level.
The solution lies in smart usage. Unfortunately, it has clearly failed in doing so. It
is highlighted in the 2018 report published by the NITI Aayog that at least 21 major
Indian cities, including New Delhi, were projected to exhaust their groundwater
resources by 2020. If that is the case, I would like to know from the hon. Minister
how we are going to move forward from here.
Madam, the Ministry has seen a significant rise in the funds allocated to it.
For the financial year of 2024-25, Rs.98,418 crore was given. In the Union Budget,
for the fiscal year 2025-26, the Ministry of Jal Shakti has been allocated Rs.99,503
crore. These allocations represent approximately 1.964 per cent of the total
Government expenditure, positioning the Ministry of Jal Shakti among the key
sectors in terms of budgetary priority.
Madam, despite this, there is a significant underutilization of this fund. So, I
would like to ask the hon. Minister the reason for non-utilization of the fund.
The ambitious Har Ghar Jal initiative that aims to provide every rural
household with tap water by 2024 has now been extended till 2028. I would like to
know from the hon. Minister why the Government needs four years more to fulfil its
target. I would also like to highlight an important issue. The Ministry claimed that
78 per cent of Anganwadi centres had access to clean tap water under the Jal
Jeevan Mission. However, this was based on a total of 11.11 lakh centres, while
19.03.2025 1439
the Ministry of Women and Child Development reported that there are 13.87 lakh
operational centres as of March 2021. Is there a valid explanation for this glaring
mismatch in number? Such differences raise doubt about the accuracy of the
Ministry's data and the transparency of its reporting. This means that about 2.75
lakh centres were left out, bringing the actual coverage down to around 70 per cent.
The Ministry's focus on large-scale water infrastructure projects, such as river
interlinking project, which aims to connect 37 rivers and extensive dam
construction, gives rise to various environmental and social concerns. These
projects can harm ecosystems, displace communities and lead to loss of
biodiversity. I urge the Ministry to focus on sustainable community-based water
management instead of large infrastructure projects that could cause long-term
damage. … (Interruptions) Madam, my Party has given 15 minutes for me. …
(Interruptions) I would like to know from the hon. Minister whether the waters of the
Ganga River in Prayagraj, Uttar Pradesh, during the ongoing Maha Kumbh were
completely clean? As per the National Green Tribunal's directives, millions of
devotees should have been informed about the quality of the water they were about
to dip into. We have all seen the reports showing dangerously high levels of
bacteria in the water, making it unsafe for bathing. If the Minister has received this
information, I would like to know from the Government what steps the Government
is taking regarding cleaning the Ganga Triveni Sangam.
Madam, I belong to the Sundarbans Constituency. The Sundarbans river
embankment issue is a major challenge due to the region's fragile ecosystem. The
region faced constant threats from cyclones like Amphan, Aila, Bulbul, Yaas and
19.03.2025 1440
many more. The Government of India sanctioned Rs 2,103.64 crore for FMP
schemes, namely Sundarban Embankments Reconstruction Project, Kaliaghai-
Kapaleswari-Baghai Basin Scheme etc., The Government of West Bengal
submitted a reimbursement project worth Rs. 204.07 crore, but only Rs. 44.15 crore
has so far been reimbursed. So, I would like to request the hon. Minister to release
the balance amount, that is, Rs. 159.92 crore at the earliest.
I would like to request the hon. Minister to implement the Ghatal Master Plan
which is a long-pending demand of the people in the two flood-affected districts …
(Interruptions) The dredging of Bhagirathi, Hooghly and Ganga at the upstream of
Farakka Barrage to maintain navigability at Kolkata port was the main objective of
construction of Farakka Barrage and Ganga Water Treaty, 1996. So, I would like
to request the hon. Minister to release the fund.
The three North Bengal districts, namely, Cooch Behar, Alipurduar and
Jalpaiguri, which are criss-crossed by more than 50 rivers having their origin in
Bhutan, are mainly responsible for erosion and flood.
So, I would like to request the hon. Minister that since the Government of
West Bengal has already sent a proposal and requested for sanctioning of fund on
8th August, 2024, you should constitute an Indo-Bhutan Joint River Commission at
the earliest to formulate and recommend flood control works and flood warning
system.
Being a representative of Sundarbans, I would like to request the hon.
