SHRI TAMILSELVAN THANGA (THENI): Hon. Speaker Sir, Vanakkam. At the
outset, I thank you for allowing me to speak on the demands for grants pertaining
to the Ministry of Water Resources (Jal Shakti). There is no world without water.
Long live Tamil language. Water Resources for the basis of our life, livelihood and
development. You have allocated Rs 99, 503 Crore for the Jal Jeevan Mission. If
we compare, it is Rs 4,500 Crore less than the last year’s allocation. While speaking
in this august House, Hon Finance Minister informed that 98 per cent of the
households in India have been provided with potable tap water under the Jal
Jeevan Mission. Hon. Prime Minister too was listening to this in this House. You
bring several schemes like Jal Jeevan Mission. The Union Government should
keenly observe to the fact that which State of this country is implementing the
Schemes very effectively. If you talk about Jal Jeevan Mission, it is the Dravida
Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK) Government in Tamil Nadu led by Thalapathi Thiru
M.K. Stalin which has made this Scheme successfully reach 98 per cent of
households in the State of Tamil Nadu. When this State Government of Tamil Nadu
demands for release of funds, you have been denying to do so. You refuse to
provide flood relief assistance. You refuse to release funds meant for education.
Similarly for Flood Management and Border Area Programme (FMBAP) you earlier
allocated Rs. 450 crore. This year you have further reduced this flood management
fund by Rs.50 crore and made it as Rs. 400 crore. Why did you reduce this amount?
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
19.03.2025 1442
Will there not be any floods this year? If such a flood comes, will this Government
should inform us on how to stop that flood. Air and water are being polluted. Water
bodies are polluted. We come to know from the Reports of the Union Government
about water pollution, This Biochemical Oxygen Demand test report further about
the 1920 samples of water taken in different places explains how far this water is
worst polluted. There is Biochemical Oxygen deficit in 817 places. It means the
water pollution is very high in these 817 places. This Union Government after
finding this should act swiftly for ensuring the protection of all water bodies. There
is very high pollution of water in 311 basins. What are you doing to rectify this?
There is depletion of ground water level. If we inspect in 100 districts, there is
depletion of ground water table. There are chemicals like fluoride, arsenic and
uranium contained in water making it unfit for drinking. I urge the Union
Government to devise a Scheme to address this issue. This Government is
allocation Rs. 99,503 crore to the Department of Water Resources for the year
2025-26. This Government should also immediately release funds timely and in full
to the States like Tamil Nadu which are implementing several Schemes
successfully. But so far not even 40 percent of the funds are not released. Why are
showing hesitation to release the remaining funds? Union Government should
clarify on this. These are the details of rivers which are polluted. You have been
allocating huge funds for cleaning Ganga and addressing the issue of pollution in
river Ganges. Similarly you should allocate funds for other rivers in the various
States of the country. Particularly in Tamil Nadu, people take holy dip like
Kumbhmela in rivers like Thamirabharani, Cauvery and Vaigai. Even water is
19.03.2025 1443
polluted in these rivers. You should allocate funds for cleaning such rivers of Tamil
Nadu as well. Hon Prime Minister spoke in this august House even yesterday. He
said that more than 60 crore people took holy dip during Mahakumbh in Uttar
Pradesh. I want to ask you if 60 crore people take holy dip in a river, will that not
be polluted? What is the fate of that river and the quality of that river water? How
will you clean it? How long will it take? This situation should not continue. There
should be a water management Committee which should look into the issue of
Kumbhmela types of holy dip in large numbers in various rivers of the country and
submit reports to the different State Governments with a follow-up action in this
regard. Similarly in the coastal districts of Tamil Nadu, there is issue of mixing of
saline water with in drinking water. There should be coastal management
programmes for addressing this issue drinking water becoming saline and water
should be protected. In Tamil Nadu, if we talk about a new Scheme, it is the
Combined Water Supply Scheme (CWSS). There are 56 Combine water Schemes
through which drinking water is provided to Crores of people of Tamil Nadu. You
should provide adequate funds to this Integrated Drinking Water Scheme. Why
Union Government is not releasing funds to Tamil Nadu. Under Jal Jeevan Mission,
so far, there is a due amount of Rs. 1707 crore to be released by the Union
Government for the year 2024-25. This amount is yet to be released to Tamil Nadu.
You should release this immediately. Under fund sharing pattern under Jal Jeevan
Mission (JJM), should be revised. You have not increased the share of the Union
Government. It should be increased. Only then the water resources can be well
protected. Similarly, under 19 new Combined water Schemes you should allocate
19.03.2025 1444
Rs. 21,231 crore to Tamil Nadu. We have given new water Schemes. This Scheme
is the need of the hour. Through this Scheme, Crores of people of Tamil Nadu will
get integrated drinking water supply. In order to protect our water bodies there
should be monitoring of five dams in Tamil Nadu. Cauvery, Kollidam, Vellaru dams
should be protected. There should be separate funds allocated for operation and
maintenance of these dams. Union Government should understand that it is Tamil
Nadu led by Hon Chief Minister Thalapathi Thiru M.K. Stalin which is pioneer in
implementing successfully the water related schemes of the Union Government
without any delay and hence I urge that adequate funds should be allocated Tamil
Nadu without further delay. With this I conclude. Thank you.

देने क(cid:236) योजना बनाई गई थी । वष(cid:170) 2025 तक महारा(cid:213)(cid:367) म (cid:164) 88 (cid:255)ितशत से अिधक घर(cid:332) म (cid:164) नल कन(cid:179)े शंस

SHRI ANTO ANTONY (PATHANAMTHITTA): Thank you, Chairperson, for giving
me the opportunity to participate in this discussion. Jal Jeevan Mission, a flagship
programme of the Government, aims at providing clean and sustainable drinking
water to every rural household in India.
While we appreciate the vision and commitment behind this initiative, its
execution in many States, including Kerala, remains deeply concerning. Jal Jeevan
Mission has been allocated Rs. 67,000 crore in 2025-26, which is five per cent
lower than the Budget Estimate of 2024-25. In 2024-25, the Revised Estimate of
this Scheme is only Rs. 22,694 crore, as opposed to a budget allocation of Rs.
70,163 crore. This is the lowest expenditure on this Scheme in any year since 2020-
21.
In Kerala, the coverage of Jal Jeevan Mission is the lowest, with only 54 per
cent of households having access to tap water. The Mission, which was supposed
to ensure piped water access to every rural household by 2024, has faced severe
delays in execution.
Nearly half of the project remains incomplete, mainly due to slow tendering
process and approvals. This bureaucratic delay has delayed infrastructure
development.
The slow pace of implementation has had a direct impact on local
19.03.2025 1450
communities. Roads that were dug up for laying pipelines have been left unfinished
for months, even years, causing significant economic losses and posing serious
safety hazards. Accidents due to unfinished roads and trenches are becoming
more common.
Instead of improving quality of life, the Mission's poor execution is creating
new infrastructure challenges. Since the Scheme's funding structure mandates that
50 per cent of the cost be borne by the Centre, 25 per cent by the State
Governments, 50 per cent by the local bodies and 10 per cent by the beneficiaries,
Members of Parliament have a minimum role over its execution. This has led to
States renaming, and implementing the project under different local schemes,
which reduces Central Government involvement, and scrutiny. The States like
Kerala purposefully try to evade the Centre’s role in this project. They try to
implement these projects as their own by avoiding the Members of Parliament.
As a Central Government project, MPs have the right to monitor the Jal
Jeevan Mission project. The Government of Kerala has even published a book
consolidating Jal Jeevan Mission work in 140 Assembly Constituencies, exempting
Central Government’s contributions and the MPs' concern, owning it as their own
project.
Across many States, people are receiving tap connections, but no water
flows through them. In some cases, the taps are yet to be fitted. Issues of water
crisis have been raised in Parliament multiple times. A common issue regarding Jal
Jeevan Mission has been the lack of water at the source. The Government reports
an increase in household tap connections, but having taps does not mean having
19.03.2025 1451
water. The data may suggest rapid progress, but what matters to people is whether
water actually reaches their homes.
Madam, another major issue is the quality of water being supplied. The
Functionality Assessment Report 2022 revealed that States like Kerala, Sikkim and
Tripura have over 40 per cent of households receiving water that does not meet
quality standards. Safe drinking water is not just about access, but also about
reliability. The Standing Committee on Water Resources also highlighted several
issues, including inadequate water quality testing, unhygienic storage facilities, and
insufficient testing labs. It is not enough to just provide connections. We must
ensure that the water supplied is clean, regular, and safe for consumption.
The financial constraints are another major hurdle. Many States have
struggled to meet their share of funding due to cost overruns and delays in statutory
approvals. The Standing Committee has pointed out that difficult terrains, scattered
rural populations, and adverse climatic conditions often slowdown progress. As a
result, Kerala and several other States have been unable to fully implement Jal
Jeevan Mission at the desired pace. If the Centre claims to have Jal Jeevan Mission
as one of its major schemes, that they boast about, then they should also take
responsibility and accountability for ensuring that the scheme is properly
implemented, taking into consideration the diversities and complexities of
implementation of the scheme across regions.
Finally, if we truly want Jal Jeevan Mission to be a success, we need urgent
corrective measures. I urge the Government to prioritise source sustainability to
ensure that every tap connection is backed by a dependable water source. Kerala,
19.03.2025 1452
with its lowest Jal Jeevan Mission coverage, must receive special attention to
address gaps in implementation. Furthermore, the Centre must work closely with
the States to address financial bottlenecks, accelerate fund releases, and
strengthen infrastructure.
This Mission is not just about numbers on the dashboard, it is about ensuring
every citizen gets access to safe and sustainable drinking water. Water is the most
fundamental human need. The people of Kerala, and indeed all of India, deserve
not just tap connections, but running water which is clean, and accessible every
day.
I urge the Government to take immediate, and decisive steps to bridge the
implementation gap in the Jal Jeevan Mission as water security is not a privilege
but a right. Thank you, Madam.

SHRI GURMEET SINGH MEET HAYER (SANGRUR): I thank you hon. Madam
Chairperson for giving me the opportunity to speak on Demands for Grants under
the control of Ministry of Jal Shati for the year 2025-26.
Ma’am, it has been said in Guru Granth Sahib that water is an essential
element in life and due to water, there is greenery and prosperity in life around us.
So, water is indispensable for life. As far as Punjab is concerned, our state was
irrigated by five rivers earlier. The word Punjab means ‘land of five rivers’. Two
rivers are now located in Pakistan after division of India in 1947. Three rivers
remained in Punjab. But, the waters of Punjab’s rivers were over-exploited. Our
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Punjabi.
19.03.2025 1453
river-water was given to even those states that should not have been given our
river-water under Riparian State law.
Secondly, we had to make India self-sufficient in production of foodgrains.
We ushered in the Green Revolution. We had to over-exploit the under-ground
water resources in Punjab. For paddy cultivation, lot of water was needed. So, due
to over-exploitation of ground-water resources, condition has deteriorated in
Punjab.
Madam Chairperson, I want to place 4-5 important points pertaining to
Punjab. When Punjab was divided and Haryana came into existence, it created
problem for Punjab. As per 1954 Yamuna river-water agreement, 2/3 rd of Yamuna
river’s water was supposed to be granted to Punjab. However, after Haryana came
into existence, it was not mentioned as to how much river-water will be given to
Punjab. I urge upon the Central Government to reconsider the provisions of this
treaty and Punjab’s share of river-water should be given to our state.
Second matter pertains to my constituency Sangrur as well as Punjab. The
embankment of river Ghaggar from Khanauri to Makror Sahib has eroded. As a
result, in 2023, floods were rampant in this area. From Makror Sahib to Karail, the
17 kilometres stretch needs to be widened. An embankment needs to be built here
so that thousands of acres of land of farmers can be saved from destruction. So, I
urge upon the hon’ble minister Sir to do the needful in both the states.
Thirdly, Punjab became the first state to complete government’s Jal Shakti
mission in 2022. Pipes were laid, boring was done and water reached the houses
of people via taps. But, that water is not worth drinking. Today, in every second or
19.03.2025 1454
third house of Punjab, cancer patients are there. The underground water in Punjab
is badly contaminated. I urge upon the Central Government that for the region of
Malwa and my area, including Sangrur, Dhuri, Barnala, Malerkotla, Sunam etc.,
provision should be made for providing Bhakra Canal water to these areas. This is
essential for providing clean drinking water to people of these areas.
Fourthly, Madam, there are dams in Kandi region. They are 30 years old
dams. A lot of silt has accumulated in these dams. It leads to flooding. Extra water
flows into Pakistan. The need of the hour is to de-silt these small dams. The State
Government has sent proposals for the same to the centre. Three projects have
been approved but five other projects are still pending with the Centre. I urge upon
the hon. Minister Sir to give the approval for the remaining projects at the earliest.
Silt can be used for the construction of highways in Punjab and excess flood water
will no longer flow into Pakistan. Nowadays, fertile top soil is being used for
construction of highways in Punjab.
The ‘Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchaai scheme’ is there. For renovation and
extension of canals, Punjab had been asked to submit its proposal. We have sent
a proposal of Rs. 1163 crores to the Centre. 10 years have passed but the entire
amount has not yet been given to Punjab. Only a partial amount has been given to
Punjab. Full money should be paid to Punjab.
The Standing Committee on Water Resources has taken cognizance in its
tenth report that the underground water of Punjab is badly contaminated. Punjab’s
name has been included in the list of seven such states. We withdraw ground water
at the rate of 27.80 bcm but the recharge of ground water is only 18.84 bcm. So,
19.03.2025 1455
Punjab was added in the list of Atal Scheme. Money was to be provided to Punjab.
But, Punjab has not yet been given this amount. An official notice should be given
to us that Punjab has been included in this scheme.
Hon. Madam Chairperson, I do hope that the problems of drinking water and
other water related issues of Punjab will be solved by the Central Government.
Thank you.

SHRI SUBBARAYAN K. (TIRUPPUR): Hon. Chairman, Vanakkam. I wish to
speak briefly on the Demands for Grants pertaining to the Ministry of Water
Resources. There is a Tamil proverb which says that even if the temple is small in
area, the bell sound should be bigger than normal. In the same way, those in the
Government are talking big and doing nothing. Their activities are in that fashion. I
am not saying this with any bias. This is the actual state of affairs prevalent in this
country. This Government has failed on three counts. They failed miserably. I do
not have any hesitation to say so. They have failed to protect the water resources
of India without water being wasted. This is utter failure on the part of Union
Government. They have also failed to use water for our benefit. Thirdly, they have
miserably failed to protect our water bodies and water resources including ground
water from being polluted. Therefore, this Government, I must say, is just functional
on mere words with no concrete and appropriate action in protecting the interests
of our country. I want to cite an example. Flood and drought are perennial issues.
Losses due to floods and drought are to the large extent. Why cannot you link the
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
19.03.2025 1456
rivers of Mount Himalayas to compensate this loss? Why cannot you link the rivers
of south India? There was a Scheme to link the rivers of our country. There was a
Scheme called as Garland Canal Scheme. I fail to understand why this Scheme
could not be completed? They say that they do not have funds. Is it true that they
do not have funds? This Government wants to waive-off the loans to the tune of
Rs. 25 lakh crore taken by corporates like Adani and Ambani. If this Government
had collected that loan, it would have been easily implemented the interlinking of
Himalayan rivers. It would have interlinked the rivers of our country. It would have
made our arid lands fertile. They did not do this. That is why I say that this Union
Government has miserably failed. Ground water table has gone down so deep to
a dangerous level. It has been polluted as well. There is no policy to streamline this
or to implement corrective measures in this regard. Chemical discharges are
polluting the rivers of the country. River Yamuna has become the worst polluted as
we cannot even stand on the shore of this river. No river including Ganges is pure
without being polluted. They gave assurance with the slogan “Truth alone can
Triumph” for cleaning Ganges within a particular period of time. Whether they have
fulfilled this assurance? No, they failed. They just betray the people of this country
by mere words and no action is visible concretely on ground. This is the ground
reality. I want to talk about industrial effluents. Who is to be blamed for discharge
of effluents from Industries? Is it the industry which is the culprit? Is that the labourer
to be blamed for this debacle? No. It is the Government which gave permission for
these Industries is to be blamed. Even a drop of water in this land should not be
polluted. There is a gap in the planning of such a Scheme and its execution. There
19.03.2025 1457
is a sharing pattern of funds for these water related Schemes. At the time of giving
permission to set-up industries in Textile hubs, if the integrated schemes are
implemented in preserving water, theissue of water pollution would have been very
well addressed. They should have ensured that not even a single drop of water will
be polluted. But the Union Government failed to do so. Similarly, sea water should
be made drinkable. Priority should be given to desalination plants to convert saline
water to drinking water. They have not shown any concern towards this aspect of
preservation of water as well. There are ways to do it efficiently without any loss.
Not only on three counts, have they failed on many counts. I have a long list of the
failures of this Government. Finally, I want to say this Parliament should have more
number of sittings in a calendar year. This can ensure availability of more time for
the MPs belonging to the Opposition parties to speak on different issues. What is
the loss for this Government in this regard? Why it is not doing so? I want to warn
about this dangerous trend where this Government is just disallowing the
democracy to function in its normal course.
Thank you.
SHRI ASADUDDIN OWAISI (HYDERABAD): The Ministry has been allocated Rs.
99,503 crore in BE 2025-26. This marks a meagre increase over Rs. 98,714 crore
allocated in BE 2024-25. I stand to oppose this because the Ministry had faced
severe under-utilisation of funds in 2024-25 with the Revised Estimate being 48 per
cent lower than the allocation at just Rs. 51,558 crore. The Department of Drinking
Water and Sanitation has been allocated Rs. 74,226 crore in 2025-26. This is a
reduction in allocation compared to Rs. 76,570 allocated in BE 2024-25. This trend
19.03.2025 1458
of under-utilisation has persisted with only 74 per cent utilisation in 2020-21; 72 per
cent utilisation in 2021-22; 88 per cent utilisation in 2022-23; and only 31.47 per
cent funds utilised till January in 2024-25.
In Jal Jeevan Mission, the allocation has been reduced from Rs. 70,162 crore
in BE 2024-25 to Rs. 67,000 crore in 2025-26. The scheme has also seen limited
utilisation in 2024-25 with only 31.35 per cent funds utilised till February 2025.
While the Jal Jeevan Mission may declare victory on paper, the ground reality tells
a different story. Reports from Maharashtra’s Solapur, Aurangabad, Palghar, and
Palghani districts reveal persistent water shortages. In drought-stricken
Bundelkhand in UP, people across multiple districts are still desperately searching
for water. The Government’s tall claims mean little when taps remain dry and basic
survival remains a daily battle.
Regarding the Swachh Bharat Mission, again the Government can boast
success. I will give examples how these funds have been utilised. In 2020-21, 49
per cent of the allocated Rs. 9,994 crore was utilized. In 2021-22, 31 per cent of
the allocated Rs. 9,994 crore was utilized. In 2022-23, 68.5 per cent of the allocated
Rs. 7,192 crore was utilized. In 2023-24, 94.6 per cent of the allocated Rs. 7,192
crore was utilized. In 2024-25, 32.7 per cent of the allocated Rs. 7,192 crore
utilized. Even more concerning is from 2020-21 to 2022-23, less than 50 per cent
of funds allocated by the Centre to States for Swachh Bharat Mission has been
released. Under Solid Waste Management, over four years, that is, 2021 to 2025,
only 4,96,610 villages have been covered out of a total target of 9,81,827, which is
a shortfall of nearly 50 per cent. Under Liquid Waste Management, since 2020-21,
19.03.2025 1459
only 4,25,625 villages have been covered out of a total target of 8,30,345.
I will now come to the specific issues of Telangana. My Government has long
been demanding the national status for the Palamuru-Rangareddy Lift Irrigation
Scheme under the Ministry of Jal Shakti.

SHRI BASAVARAJ BOMMAI (HAVERI): Madam, I thank you very much even
though it has been a little late in giving me an opportunity to speak on the Demands
of Grants of the Jal Shakti Department. Thank you once again, Madam.
I must congratulate our hon. Prime Minister and Jal Shakti Minister for one
simple reason that even though water is in the Concurrent List, a lot of
responsibilities have been taken by the Government of India since 2017, especially
with the kind of society we are living in where even after more than 75 years of our
19.03.2025 1461
Independence we are not able to give minimum quality drinking water to our people.
Yes, it is a challenge. Water problem is a dynamic problem. It is no more a
static problem. If there is good rain, the underground is charged and water is there
in rivers, ponds, everywhere. If rain is not there, then the next year itself can be a
drought year.
18.03 hrs (Shri Dilip Saikia in the Chair)
With great experience, jeeva jala, the lifeline of our entire world is water.
However, the fundamentals are not very clear. For example, to whom does water
belong? Does it belong to an individual, an institution, a Government or a society?
This question is yet to be answered throughout the world. Hence, there are
disputes, and there are a lot of dispute solutions from Helsinki Award to our Inter-
State Water Dispute Act.
Therefore, the next question that automatically arises is this. Is water
problem a local problem or a global problem?
For example, our nation was under a green category about 30 years back.
Now, India has come into an ‘orange’ category. If there is a neglect of water, which
we think is a natural resource, unless and until the natural resource becomes a
national resource, both conservation of water and utilization of water have to be
balanced, along with recycling and reutilization of water. All these years, we have
been concentrating only on the storage of water, to build dams and different kinds
of storages. We have focused solely on the augmentation of water.
Sir, we never thought of utilizing distribution and managing the water. The
time has come now. It is already late for this country to think as a whole. The time
19.03.2025 1462
has come now to look into the management of water. How is the water managed
that is stored? How is it distributed? A lot of wrong concepts are also going around.
Many States, organizations, and even political parties and politicians, despite being
taught in our high schools regarding the water cycle, tend to forget. Many leaders
speak as if water is going waste to the sea. If sweet water does not go to the sea,
only the salt water will rise because of the heat, and come down as water vapour,
as rain. Thirty per cent of the sweet water should go to the sea. When the first
droplets of sweet water go up, along with that, salt water also goes up. Then there
will be rainfall. It is a magnificent water cycle. This is just an introduction.

Sir, I will come straight to the point because you are in a hurry. The greatest

been following since independence and that is the Interstate Water Dispute Act.
This Interstate Water Dispute Act has created more disputes than solving the
disputes. Section 3 of this Act clearly says that if there is a dispute between two
States, and one of the States goes to the Central Government, a tribunal has to be
formed. Tribunals are there in Punjab water dispute, in southern States like
Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Andhra, and in Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat
and other places. It is really a shame. We are all sitting here but the disputes have
not been solved for over 50 years. The Tribunals have been working for 30 years
or 40 years but the disputes have not been solved. It is bringing a real bloodbath
among States, and there is a huge financial loss. A scheme, which was worth Rs.20
crore, has reached Rs.20,000 crore now. Who is going to pay for this? It is justice
denied, not justice delayed. It is a criminal waste. Therefore, I just don't want to talk
about the problems. I want to highlight the solution also.
Sir, in 2012, as a Water Resource Minister, in the conference, I had made a

have been sitting for the last 20 years with all the facilities in Delhi. All the States
have to come to Delhi. What a legal penalty the States are paying! I proposed that
19.03.2025 1464
there should be a water tribunal or only one single tribunal, headed by a sitting

Dr. Bhupen Hazarika often addressed the Brahmaputra River as the Luit, as
most of us in Assam do. It stands as the lifeblood of Assam, intertwining itself with
the very essence and identity of its people.
According to the Assam State Disaster Management Authority’s daily flood
reports in 2022, 5-14 districts report significant erosion daily, thereby making it a
silent yet devastating crisis, including my Parliamentary Constituency, Dhubri.
For Majuli in Assam – the world’s largest inhabited river island – erosion is
an existential threat that already its area was reduced from 1000 square kilometres
to around 500 square kilometres. If this trend continues, Majuli could disappear by
2040, thereby raising urgent concerns about the future of its displaced population.

Through the Chair, I request the Government of India to declare the flood
and erosion problems of Assam as a national problem.
Sir, it is time that the Government recognizes that a separate and dedicated
Centrally-funded River Erosion Management Programme must be introduced to
identify, map, and prioritize erosion-prone areas, including my Parliamentary
Constituency, Dhubri and across Assam, and other affected States. Moreover, the
Brahmaputra Board, which is responsible for river management in Assam, must be
strengthened through increased financial support.
Erosion is not just an environmental issue but it is a humanitarian crisis that
has forced lakhs of people into perpetual displacement, pushing them into
economic insecurity and landlessness.
19.03.2025 1471
As a State that bears one of the heaviest burdens of erosion and flooding,
Assam deserves a comprehensive, long-term solution rather than piecemeal
projects that fail to address the root cause.
I urge this House to strongly recommend that the Government take
immediate action to safeguard the land, livelihoods, and people of Assam by
allocating appropriate Budget for flood and erosion management before this crisis
worsens further.
Thank you, Sir.

DR D. RAVIKUMAR (VILUPPURAM): Hon Chairperson Sir, Vanakkam. I must
say those who die due to water-borne diseases are large in number as compared
to the people who die in wars around the world. We should also look at an important
aspect that 97.5 per cent of water flows into sea. Only 2.5 per cent of water is
available for human consumption. Out of this, if we exclude water in glaciers of
Himalayas and ground water, then only a meagre 1 per cent of the total water is
available for human consumption. We should think as to how we use this 1 per cent
of water that is available in a judicious manner.
We are worshipping our perennial rivers as Gods. We should also think
whether, in the name of worshipping these rivers, we can pollute them.
Governments have a key role to play in equitable distribution of this 1 per cent of
available water among all human beings. With that in mind this Government
brought Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) to provide water to all the people in equal
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
19.03.2025 1472
manner. A target was set by this Government to provide potable tap water to all the
households in India by 2024. Now this target has been extended to the year 2028.
During the year 2024-25, an amount of Rs 70,000 Crore was allocated for this
Scheme. An amount of Rs. 67,000 has been allocated for this Scheme for the year
2025-26. There is a reduction of allocation by Rs. 3000 Crore. But it is a matter of
concern that out of the total allocation only Rs 22,000 Core could be spent last
year. In the revised Budget the remaining amount has been marked as unspent.
As a result, there is a big financial burden on the State Governments.
The sharing pattern of JJM is 50:50 between the Union and the State
Governments. But during last year only 31 per cent of funds were released by the
Union Government. Although water pipes have been fixed in many places, due to
non-availability of bore-wells, there is no water supply through these pipes. Union
Government claim that they have given drinking water to the people of this country.
But it is pitiable to say there is no supply of drinking water through these pipes.
Union Government claims that drinking water facility has been provided almost 100
per cent in 2.5 lakh villages of the country. But statistics give a different picture.
Only 1.5 lakh villages have been provided with drinking water facility. I urge upon
you that in order to address this shortcoming, the funds allocated for this Scheme
should be completely utilized by releasing it to the fullest extent. In my Viluppuram
parliamentary constituency, the ground water table has gone down drastically.
Viluppuram district is one among the districts of Tamil Nadu where there is scarcity
of drinking water. They tried a Scheme to convert sea water into drinking water.
This was renamed as Integrated Drinking Water Scheme of Tiruvannamalai and a
19.03.2025 1473
proposal was made for providing drinking water to more than 2500 villages at a
cost of Rs 2060 Crore. Only when the Union Government releases adequate and
necessary funds to this Scheme, we can provide uninterrupted drinking water to
the people living in Viluppuram and Kallakkurichi districts. Therefore, I urge upon
the Union Government to release funds under JJM without any further delay to
Tamil Nadu.
Experts suggest desalination plants as an alternative to address the
problem of drinking water scarcity. There are several Schemes in this regard. We
have a long coastal line for our country. We have to utilize the sea resources to
best possible extent. But what are we doing? We have started polluting the sea
water as well. Due to the effluents flowing into sea, it becomes dead and hence it
is Dead Sea. If it is Dead Sea, we cannot make use of that sea water. Not only the
sea life is affected, we are just digging our own grave. Besides increasing the
efforts of desalination of sea water making it drinkable, we should also protect the
underwater flora and fauna of the sea. It is necessary that we should prevent the
sea being polluted in different ways. I urge upon the Union Government to act in
this regard as well.
Swach Bharat Mission-Clean India Programme is another scheme under
this Ministry. We had set targets for making India open defecation free. We devised
several programmes in this regard. But the fund allocated for construction of toilets
under this Scheme is very insufficient. As per the price rise, this amount of
assistance should be increased. Only then we can implement this Scheme with a
conviction to reach its core objectives. With this I conclude. Thank you.
19.03.2025 1474


SHRIMATI HARSIMRAT KAUR BADAL (BATHINDA): I thank you, Hon.
Chairman Sir. A lot of Hon. Members have spoken here and emphasized that water
is vital for life. I hail from an agriculture-based State. Water is the life and soul of
farmers of Punjab. Irrigation water is needed for production of foodgrains. Our
economy is dependent on it. The future of vast number of families is dependent on
agriculture.
I oppose the Demands for Grants under the control of the Ministry of Water
Resources for the year 2025-26. The budget allocation this year is Rs. 99,503
crores. In 2024-25, it was Rs. 98,724 crores. Out of this amount, Rs. 644 crores
were granted to Punjab in the last 5 years. It is hardly 0.65% of the total budget.
It means, hardly 125 crores were given to Punjab each year. Punjab provides 40%
…. English translation of this part of speech originally delivered in Punjabi.
19.03.2025 1479
foodgrains in the Central pool for people of the country. We have hardly 2% of
country’s land but we provide 40% foodgrains to the country. The amount given
annually to Punjab is peanuts. This is a very sad state of affairs*.
As per the report of the Ministry, 78 per cent of our groundwater blocks are
in critical condition. Eighteen out of 23 districts are over-exploited. We are
extracting 65 per cent more, over and above what we can possibly do. But despite
this, the Government gives nothing for a 150-year-old irrigation network, which
needs urgent repair.

Why money has not been provided to us to repair and upgrade our 150
years old canal systems so that water can be saved?
Punjab is the only State where Riparian principle regarding sharing of river-
water was not followed. The earlier Congress Govts. unjustly gave our water to
our neighbouring States. Our rights were denied to us. This country has suffered
due to politics of vote. Our rightful share of river-water was given to neighbouring
States due to vote-politics. Our farmers do not have sufficient water for irrigation.
Clean drinking water is a casualty in Punjab.*

ADV. DEAN KURIAKOSE (IDUKKI): Thank you, Sir, for giving me this opportunity
to participate in the Demands for Grants discussion under the control of the Ministry
of Jal Sakti.
The biggest project of this Government after the formation of this Ministry is
the Jal Jeevan Mission. Everybody now accepts that the Jal Jeevan Mission has
19.03.2025 1482
become a big failure due to the mismanagement of this project. The Standing
Committee critically evaluated the failure of this project in States like West Bengal,
Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Kerala, and Jharkhand. This Government is only
concentrating on publicity. The slogan, ‘Har Ghar Jal’, is turning into ‘Har Ghar
Vayu’ through pipelines, and that is all that is happening. For the proper
implementation of this project, water source development is very important, as is
the development of treatment plants and distribution. All these three aspects have
seen significant failure under this Government, especially in the State of Kerala.
Even though the Union Budget has extended the Jal Jeevan Mission’s deadline to
the year 2028, the project in Kerala will remain incomplete unless the State
mobilizes Rs. 17,250 crore by the year 2028. That is the truth. The sluggish project
could come to a standstill if the State fails to clear outstanding dues of Rs. 4,500
crore to project contractors by March 31st this year. Due to this negligence and
financial mismanagement in Kerala, only 57 per cent of the targets have been met
as of February, and by this month, nearly 32.40 lakh families in the State are still
waiting for water supply under the Jal Jeevan Mission. That is the reality. However,
the Government have kept elected representatives like MPs away from the
deliberations. This also should be rectified. My serious suggestion to this
Government is that they should either fully fund the scheme and take full control of
the implementation or at least adopt a 90 to 10 funding ratio, as is being done
currently in the North-Eastern States.
Regarding the National Dam Safety Authority, I must point out some
important issues here. It is very unfortunate that despite the Dam Safety Act coming
19.03.2025 1483
into force on 30th December, 2021, the National Dam Safety Authority (NDSA),
which is the crucial body constituted under the Act for ensuring the safe and secure
operation of specified dams, has not become fully functional. As per the Demands
for Grants, the National Dam Safety Authority was allocated Rs. 25 crore in the
previous year, which has now been raised to Rs. 9.41 crore. In the present budget,
it has been allotted Rs. 23.91 crore. However, nothing has taken place from the
side of the NDSA. I would request that full utilization of the allocated amount be
ensured for the better functioning of this organization.
According to Section 9(1), read with the second schedule, the duties of the
NDSA are prescribed. These main duties include maintaining standards of dam
safety and preventing dam failure-related disasters by implementing the policies
made by the National Committee.
Secondly, the NDSA should resolve any issues between the States’ dam
safety organizations or between a State dam safety organization and any owner of
a specified dam in that State. Thirdly, it should maintain a national-level database
of specified dams, including any serious distress conditions that may be notified.
Fourthly, it should maintain relations with State dam safety organizations and the
owners of specified dams for the standardization of dam safety-related data and
practices, as well as provide related technical or management assistance. These
are the main duties of the NDSA. However, the NDSA has not taken any steps in
this direction, as mandated under the provisions, even after one year of coming
into operation. Therefore, I am respectfully urging this Government that the NDSA
should be competent enough to discharge its duties vested under the Dam Safety
19.03.2025 1484
Act in connection with the Mullaperiyar dam in my constituency. This dam falls
within the definition of a specified dam as defined under Section 2(x) of this Act.
According to the court verdict on 8th April, 2022, the responsibilities and duties
assigned by the hon. Supreme Court to the Supervisory Committee have gradually
merged with the NDSA. However, neither the Government nor the authority has
taken any steps to place the Mullaperiyar Dam under the control and surveillance
of the NDSA in accordance with this Act.
19.00 hrs
In a Question answered on this floor, the hon. Minister has replied that the
responsibility for comprehensive safety audit is not the duty of NDSA. It is quite
unfortunate. It is the duty of NDSA. As per Section 16 of the Act read with proviso
to Section 24(1), it is clear that the duty of NDSA is to keep perpetual surveillance,
carry out inspection and monitor the operation and maintenance of a specified dam
located in one State and operated by another State. So, it is the duty of NDSA.
In conclusion, I would like to mention that we are passing this kind of
legislation for resisting disasters like dam breaking, but the biggest disaster like
dam failure will happen if we do not take precautions. Mullaperiyar is the biggest
threat for the Kerala State. … (Interruptions)

SHRI PHANI BHUSAN CHOUDHURY (BARPETA): During the discussion on the
Demands for Grants of the Jal Shakti Ministry, I would like to speak on some
problems faced by our state. Hon'ble Chairman Sir, please don't press the bell.
I will conclude within 5 to 7 minutes because if you press the bell, my
concentration might get distracted. Hon'ble Chairman Sir, the total land area of our
state is 78,438 square kilometres. The National Flood Commission, after
conducting a survey, has stated that Assam's 31,500 square kilometres are flood-
prone area, which means that 39.5% of Assam's land is flood-prone.
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Assamese.
19.03.2025 1498
The total flood-prone area of our country is 10.2%, while 39.5% of our state
are flood-prone. If we compare the average of our country, Assam's flood-prone
area is four times higher than that of the entire country. These figures show how
serious the problem of flood is for the state of Assam. The number of villages in
Assam is 26,395. In 2022-23, the number of flood-affected villages were 11,227. In
2023-24, the total number of affected villages were 3,094. In 2024-25, 7,747
villages were affected by devastating floods.
Hon'ble Chairman Sir, we have 64 lakh households in Assam. Among these
households, in 2022-23, the total number of affected households were 1,08,444.
These figures show how many people have been terribly affected by the furious
floods in Assam. If we look at the death toll, we will find that during the period 2019-
24, a total number of 880 people lost their lives in floods. Altogether, 9,901 houses
have been completely damaged in Assam floods, and the number of houses that
have been partially damaged are 1,38,441. All these figures depict how badly our
state has been affected by floods.
I would like to speak a few words on erosion as well. Since 1950, Assam has
lost over 4.27 lakh hectares of land to riverbank erosion, which means 7.47% of
the total land of Assam has been washed away due to erosion. If we look at this
grim picture, we will find that, on average, 8,000 hectares of land are being washed
away by floods in Assam every year.
Sir, I will take 2 minutes. It may be mentioned that a survey was conducted
on the river Brahmaputra during the period 1912-1928. At that time, the total area
of the river Brahmaputra was 3,817 square kilometres. In 1963, 1967, and 1975,
19.03.2025 1499
when surveys on the Brahmaputra were conducted, it was found that the total area
of the river Brahmaputra was 4,850 square kilometres. Again, in 2006, another
survey was conducted, which showed the total area of the river Brahmaputra as
6,080 square kilometres. All these figures contribute only to the Brahmaputra
River's widening, and its overall size increase. Erosion has caused the widening of
the river Brahmaputra by 2,210 square kilometres. In fact, the size of the river
Brahmaputra has increased by 15 kilometres.
I am very happy to say that in the last Budget, the Hon'ble Finance Minister
made certain announcements, and I quote, "Assam grapples with floods every year
by the Brahmaputra River and its tributaries originating outside India. We will
provide assistance to Assam flood management and related projects." We thank
the Hon'ble Finance Minister. We want a permanent solution to Assam's flood and
erosion problems. We all give the slogan, "Modi Hai to Mumkin Hai." So we hope
that under the leadership of Prime Minister Modi Ji, there will be a permanent
solution to Assam's perennial problems of flood and erosion. We have full faith in
the Hon'ble Prime Minister Modi Ji. Assam has received many benefits from the
Modi Government. The Congress, despite being in power for so many years, could
do nothing. If we compare the Congress rule and BJP's rule at the Centre, we will
find a vast difference between the two. That is why I request the government under
the leadership of Modi Ji to solve Assam's flood and erosion problems permanently.
With these words, and thanking you for giving me an opportunity to speak, I
conclude. Thank you.

SHRI BHAUSAHEB RAJARAM WAKCHAURE (SHIRDI): Hon. Chairman Sir,
thank you very much for allowing me to speak on Demands for Grants of Jalshakti
Ministry for 2025-26. This year, total allocation made is Rs. 99503 crore and it was
around Rs. 98714 crore during 2024-25.
I think, considering the talent and competence of Hon. Minister Shri C.R.
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
19.03.2025 1502
Patil ji. This is very less budget allocation for the Ministry and the Union Govt. must
look into it. The total allocation for Jal Jeevan Mission is also very less.
Sir, I would like to draw your kind attention towards various issues pertaining
to my Lok Sabha constituency, Shirdi. Water scarcity is a prominent issue in Shirdi.
People have to fight for each drop of water. There is no water available for farming
here. Niti Aayog had explained in last year’s report that 60 crore people in India
are facing drinking water scarcity problem and demand for water would be doubled
till 2030. Around 2 lac people are loosing their lives due to contaminated water
every year and this is a matter of grave concern for all of us.
Most of the parts of India are drought ridden but on other side, some rivers
have more water. We have sufficient rain ever year but we lack in rain water
harvesting and water management. This rain water flows to drains and create
water scarcity. To tackle this problem, interlinking of river is a welcome step. River
Basins and ravines of Mula, Pravara, Godavari and Tapti river in Sahyadri mountain
range should be interlinked. These rivers have their origin in this area and river
water flows towards west Konkan region. We can stop and store this water in
reservoirs and dams and prevent it from flowing into sea.
In this regard, a detailed action plan should be fixed at local level and it
should be implemented effectively.
Sir, I want to raise a serious concern about Jal Jeevan Mission Scheme.
Some MPs talked about its success. But the reality is very different. I want to flag
the irregularities and corruption under this Scheme. This Scheme is being
implemented even in those areas where no water source is available. It would be
19.03.2025 1503
shown completed on paper but, without developing any water bodies.
Govt. should undertake a survey of these works because rampant corruption
is everywhere. They are only spending public money which is siphoned off. I would
request Hon. Minister to take it seriously.
It is pertinent to mention here that most of the Gram Panchayats are
financially weak and they are not in a position to pay electricity bills. No village can
ensure water supply without electricity and this is reality. So, Union and State
Govts. should come forward to pay these electricity bills for uninterrupted water
supply in villages.
There is a small town called Kopargaon in my constituency and it is still
deprived of water connections. The villagers are leaving this town. So, the Govt.
should take an initiative to secure water supply here. You can draw water from the
dams in Nashik district. British Era canals are there in this area and they should
be repaired immediately.
One big dam is also there but people have many concerns about it. These
concerns should be addressed immediately. Irrigation facilities should also be
made available. These are some geographical problems too and ground leveling
is needed. So, the concerned officers should look into it.
Left and Drip irrigation is also important and it should also be promoted by
the Ministry.
Sir, equal water distribution should be done by the Govt. 8TMC water has
been diverted towards other regions and hence, farmers in my area are facing
irrigation problems. A Committee has been constituted in this connection and I
19.03.2025 1504
hope this Committee would look into it.
The Union Govt. should take water scarcity issue seriously and take
necessary steps immediately.
Thank you.

SHRI ISHA KHAN CHOUDHURY (MALDAHA DAKSHIN): Hon. Chairperson Sir,
today I would like to bring to your attention some salient features with regard to the
Government's Jal Shakti Ministry’s current Budget regarding flood and erosion
control.
There are four schemes for flood and erosion control in the Budget, namely
flood, flood management programmes, Flood Management and Border Areas
Programmes, and the river basin management. These particular schemes deal with
embankment protection and flood control.
I want to compare the effectiveness of this year's Budget by comparing the
current Budget allocation of the Narendra Modi-led NDA Government to the Budget
allocation during the time of the last UPA Government.
Here are some vital conclusions. First, despite a 12-year gap, this year's
NDA Government's flood and erosion control Budget allocation of Rs. 800 crore is
actually Rs. 298 crore less than what the UPA had allocated in 2013-2014 during
its last term, which was an amount of Rs. 1,098 crore. This is not just a reduction.
It is a deliberate cut in funds in absolute terms, on river and coastal flood control
19.03.2025 1507
and embankment spending throughout our nation.
Second, the cost of disaster mitigation has skyrocketed due to inflation and
in the last decade our nation is facing more natural disasters, more floods and more
erosion due to the worsening impact of climate change. Yet, against all logic, the
Modi Government has slashed flood and erosion control budgets, instead of
increasing them. Factoring in inflation, this amount of Rs. 1,098 crore that the UPA
Government allocated in 2013 is worth nearly Rs. 2,000 crore today, but instead of
increasing investments in disaster management, the NDA Government has cut it
down to a meagre Rs. 800 crore. This is a let-down of vulnerable communities,
especially in States like Assam and West Bengal where the people lose their
homes, farm lands and their entire villages to riverbank erosion and floods every
year.
Hon. Chairperson, Sir, I will now move to my State of West Bengal. Here are
the most important facts about West Bengal. First of all, about 42.5 per cent of
West Bengal is flood-prone, which makes it one of the most flood-affected States
in the country. Flood satellite maps estimate the total flood-affected area in West
Bengal at 19.69 lakh hectares with the most affected districts being Purba
Barddhaman, Murshidabad, Purba Medinipur, Hooghly, and Maldah.
Secondly, I would like to share some numbers provided by the Ministry of Jal
Shakti to an Unstarred Question in the Rajya Sabha, from 2023. The following
numbers were in response to the year-on-year count of the total financial losses
incurred by West Bengal due to flood and erosion. The following are the losses
incurred:- in 2017, it was Rs. 17,000 crore; in 2018, it was Rs. 54 crore; in 2019, it
19.03.2025 1508
was Rs. 1,269 crore; in 2020, no data available; and in 2021, it was Rs. 16,079
crore.
These are not just numbers; these figures represent the destruction of
farmland, agricultural crops, fisheries, homes, roads and schools, community
hospitals, mosques and temples washed away as land is eroded, and literally falls
into the Ganges and its tributary rivers. These figures represent only some of the
crushing losses on the livelihood of West Bengal farmers, labourers, and families.
What these figures do not show is the effect on landless families who then become
landless refugees, without a permanent home. The consequences of floods are
short-term and economic recovery is possible, but the effects of the slow river bank
erosion are of permanent nature, where the entire socio-economic structure is
damaged and the affected population has to move, settle and create a livelihood
somewhere else.
Thirdly, the Farakka Barrage, a Central project built in the 1970s to divert
water flowing into Bangladesh into our Bhagirathi and Hooghly rivers to maintain
Kolkata port navigability, is directly responsible for worsening erosion crisis in the
districts of Malda and Murshidabad, where my Parliamentary Constituency of
Malda Dakshin lies. The presence of the Barrage causes the build-up of 64 crore
tonnes of silt to accumulate annually and this has changed the course of the
Ganges and has resulted in devasting erosion to both these districts.
After the construction of Farakka Barrage, more than 350 km of fertile land
has been completely swallowed by the Ganges in Malda district, and 500 sq.kms
of fertile land has been wiped out in Murshidabad district, displacing lakhs and
19.03.2025 1509
lakhs of people. During the UPA regime, from 2005 onwards, the Centre took direct
responsibility for anti-erosion measures caused by the barrage – 40 kms upstream
into Malda and 80 kms downstream into Murshidabad from the Farakka Barrage
itself. Under the UPA Government's, the budget allocation was Rs. 214 crore in
2013-14 for the Farakka Project. The current NDA Government has abandoned
this policy which is shown in this year's small allocation of only Rs. 69 crore.
HON. CHAIRPERSON : Please conclude.
SHRI ISHA KHAN CHOUDHURY : Sir, while the Centre boasts of providing West
Bengal with Rs. 1,051.96 crore under the Flood Management Programme
component of FMBAP, let us dissect the reality. Out of 17 projects sanctioned for
West Bengal, only four have received funds, amounting to a meagre Rs. 42.80
crore, which is less than one per cent of the promised allocation. This is a drop in
the ocean when compared to the devastation endured by the people of West
Bengal. What about the remaining 13 projects? Where is the timeline for their
completion? Funds are announced for optics, but not disbursed in time; projects
are sanctioned but they never see the light of the day.
In conclusion, in the Finance Minister's Budget Speech, she states that the
Budget continues the NDA Government's ‘continued efforts to secure inclusive
development’ and that the Government wants the growth of all regions of India
through ‘Sabka Vikas’. However, the budget allocation of the Jal Shakti Ministry for
flood control and erosion measures across the nation paints a different picture with
a seeming indifference and neglect towards millions of people who face the
destructive power of river erosion. The facts are clear and the numbers undeniable.
19.03.2025 1510
SHRI N. K. PREMACHANDRAN (KOLLAM): Mr. Chairperson Sir, I rise to strongly
oppose Demand 62 and 63 in the Demands for Grants of the Ministry of Jal Shakti

this Ministry when they are not utilizing the funds? Hon. Jal Shakti Minister, Patil Ji,
what is the purpose of providing grant by this House to your Ministry when you are
not utilizing the funds? Sir, I will substantiate it. The total Demands for Grants under
Demands 62 and 63 for the financial year 2025-26 is Rs.99,503 crore. What is the
budget allocation for the financial year 2024-25? It is Rs.98,714 crore.
Hon. Chairperson, Sir, you may kindly see that the Revised Estimate goes
to show that the total money, which was spent, was Rs.51,558 only. That means,
48 per cent lower than that of the budget allocation, which is the money spent by
this Ministry during the year 2024-25.
Coming to the Jal Jeevan Mission budget proposals 2025-26, Rs.67,000
crore have been allocated and granted by this House as Demand for Grants. It is
five per cent lower than that of the Budget Estimate of 2024-25. Sir, the Budget
Estimate 2024-25 is Rs.70,163 crore. The Revised Estimate goes to show Rs.
22,694 crore. That means the budget allocation is Rs.70,163 crore out of which
your Ministry has spent just Rs.22,694 crore on Jal Jeevan Mission. That is the
lowest expenditure since the implementation of scheme in the year 2021. What is
the purpose of granting money or granting finance to your Ministry if you are not
able to spend? I will come to each and every provision. I know there is the paucity
of the time. Kindly see the report of Comptroller and Auditor-General of India and
Audit Accounts for the financial year 2021-22. Kindly see Demand 62 for Jal Jeevan
19.03.2025 1511
Mission. The total money sanctioned is Rs.16,485 crore out of which the total
disbursement is just Rs.4,972 crore. That means 69 per cent saving.
Coming to the Swachh Bharat Abhiyan, there is 74 per cent saving, and in
Jal Jeevan Mission Gram, there is 79 per cent saving. This 79 per cent saving
means they have only utilised 21 per cent of the money. That means non-utilisation
of the money granted by the Parliament, which in turn means, the Ministry is not
performing. That is why, I am saying that I strongly oppose the Demands 62 and
63.
Sir, I am talking about the CAG report. There is an observation also in the
report. I am not going to read the observation. The justification given by the Ministry
has not been accepted by the Comptroller and Auditor-General of India because

reasons is that the States are not able to give their share. That means they have
to spend the money. This is regarding the Demands for Grants.
Sir, I was the Minister for Water Resources for five years in the State of
Kerala. During my time in the year 2007-2008, we had formulated a water policy
towards which I would like to draw the attention of the hon. Minister. The right to
safe drinking water is a Fundamental Right enshrined in Article 21 of the
Constitution. The right to have safe drinking water does not mean the right to have
tap water. You are giving focus to the right to have tap water. It is not at all possible.
There is no doubt about it. So, you have misconceived the issue. You have to
ensure the availability of safe drinking water. The right to access water is to be
ensured. Instead of ensuring right to access to water, you are giving a chance to
19.03.2025 1512
the right to give tap water. Is it possible? The target was 2024. Now it has been
extended to 2028. The main problem is unavailability of potable water. The second
problem is the quality of the water. You have to address these two issues. Instead
of addressing these two issues, you are going for the tap water.
Sir, this is going to be an engineering project. This is going to be a big
misappropriation. Most of the Members from Kerala has already stated that Rs.
4,500 crore are in arrears for the contractors. All the roads are in dilapidated state.
It is everywhere. It is going to be a project which is not up to the expectation. Kindly
have a review on this issue because there is no water. The source of water has to
be made. Water transmission has to be made. Treatment plant has to be made.
This is simply digging the roads, putting the pipes, and giving a tap connection, but
there is no water. In place of water, you are getting only air. What is the meaning?
That means this Jal Jeevan Mission project is a good, innovative project, but
unfortunately, it has become an engineering project. The person, who will be
benefited, will be the contractors and other partners. Nothing else is going to
happen. I am making sure, please keep it on record. You kindly have a review. I
am saying this out of my experience.
There is one more point regarding the Members of Parliament. Mr. Patil Ji,
what is the role of the MPs? Is it a Centrally-Sponsored Scheme? I am sure this is
a 200-page booklet published by the Kerala State Government. This is a Centrally-
Sponsored Scheme in which 50 per cent of the contribution is from the Central
Government, and only 25 per cent contribution is of the State Government, 15 per
cent is the contribution of local bodies, local panchayats and 10 per cent is the
19.03.2025 1513
contribution of the beneficiary. The Chief Minister is there. The Water resources
Minister is there. All the 140 MLAs are there. Each and every legislative
constituency is well explained. This is a Centrally- Sponsored Scheme. There is
not even a mention that this is a Centrally- Sponsored Scheme. The MPs have no
role. You go through the booklet. You kindly review it. Last time also, I have said
this. You are saying that this is a Centrally-Sponsored Scheme and 50 per cent
contribution is from the Centre. But unfortunately, the Members of Parliament has
no role in the implementation, in the formulation or even in the review. Nothing is
there. So, I would like to request the hon. Minister to kindly review the Jal Jeevan
Mission. It shall not remain as a project. Otherwise, it shall become a failed project.
So, a review has to be done. Appropriate changes have to be made so that the
money is utilised for the poor people. This will benefit the common people.
With these words, I conclude. Thank you very much
ADV. FRANCIS GEORGE (KOTTAYAM): Sir, I too strongly oppose this Demand
raised by this Jal Shakti Ministry. It is because this Ministry is not able to handle
properly even the flagship programme. So, that is very evident. What was the aim?
The Jal Jeevan Mission's aim was to provide functional household tap connections
to provide 55 litres per capita per day, bringing water to all rural households in the
country by 2024. But nothing has happened. Now, the scheme has been extended
to 2028. But at this rate, I doubt, even by 2028, nothing is going to happen because
in the last year, out of Rs. 70,000 crore allotted for this particular project, Rs. 50,000
crore was not spent. I do not know what is happening with this Ministry. It is not
happening not only in Kerala, but also throughout the country. I request the Ministry
19.03.2025 1514
and the hon. Minister, who is here, to do a proper review of this project throughout
the country. In all States, what is happening? Only pipeline is being laid. Nobody
knows where the source is. Nobody knows where the tank is. Only pipes are being
laid. All the panchayat roads have been dug up and now the people are asking us
to provide the MP funds to repair these roads. No repair work is being done. The
whole thing is in a mess. So, I would request the Ministry that it has to do a complete
review of this flagship program.
Sir, due to paucity of time, I am going straight to another very important thing.
Under the repair, renovation and restoration of water body scheme, I would like to
draw your attention to Vembanad Lake. The Vembanad Lake is India's longest
lake. It stretches over 96 kilometres, encompassing an area of approximately 230
square kilometres. This vast water body traverses multiple districts of Kerala,
including Alappuzha, Kottayam and Ernakulam, serving as a lifeline for the State's
ecology and economy. The lake's unique mix of salt water and freshwater fosters
a thriving ecosystem, supporting diverse flora and fauna. Notably, it hosts the
renowned Nehru Trophy boat race also, attracting enthusiasts worldwide.
The degradation of Vembanad Lake is not a localised issue. It reflects
broader environmental and public health concerns that warrant national attention.
There is microplastic contamination; there is biodiversity loss; and there are
pathogenic threats. The lake’s pollution levels have led to the proliferation of
harmful bacteria, including Vibrio species associated with diseases such as
cholera. The Thanneermukkom Bund is a double-edged sword. Constructed in
1974, the Thanneermukkom Bund was designed to prevent salt water intrusion into
19.03.2025 1515
the Kuttanad lowlands, the Rice Bowl of Kerala, facilitating agriculture. However,
it has disrupted the natural salinity gradient leading to ecological imbalances such
as the proliferation of invasive species like water hyacinth and a decline in fish
population.
The imperative for national intervention is integrated water resources
management, pollution control initiatives, ecological restoration projects,
community engagement and education, scientific monitoring and research. I
request that the degradation of Vembanad Lake is a clarion call for immediate
action. This crisis transcends regional boundaries reflecting broader environmental
and public health challenges that our nation is confronted with. I request that a
Vembanad Lake Authority should be created. There are a lot of funds lying there
unspent. A Vembanad Lake Authority should be constituted and sufficient funds
should be granted for the restoration of Vembanad Lake.
Our Tamil Nadu friends are here. Adv. Dean kuriakose had mentioned it. We
have no quarrel with our Tamil Nadu friends. The Mullaperiyar Dam is 130 years
old. It warrants urgent attention. Our only request is this. We have no problem in
sharing this water with Tamil Nadu. We have been giving it for the last 130 years
almost free. We are not taking any money. We would like to have the security
aspects being very properly considered by the Central Government. The Central
Government should call the leaders of both the States and discuss and settle this
matter once and for all. This is because if something happens, it is not going to be
the issue of Kerala or Tamil Nadu issue. The whole world will blame the country.
More than 50 lakh people are going to be affected. So, please considerer it. We
19.03.2025 1516
want the Prime Minister himself to intervene in this matter. Call and settle it
amicably. We have no problem in sharing water with Tamil Nadu. Our erstwhile
Chief Minister Shri Oommen Chandy coined a slogan saying ‘water for Tamil Nadu,
and security for Kerala’. So, it is always water for Tamil Nadu first. We are taking
the security aspect second only.
So, I most humbly request that the Water Resources Minister should take
the lead. The Prime Minister should intervene and settle this matter once and for
all. So, I strongly again oppose this Demand because the Ministry has not been
functioning efficiently.
Thank you.

SHRI GOVIND MAKTHAPPA KARJOL (CHITRADURGA): Sir, I would like to
speak a few words. I would like to place some issues before the House and thereby
seek approval of Central Assistance for the Upper Bhadra Project of Karnataka,
19.03.2025 1524
through your kind self, from the hon. Prime Minister and also from the hon. Minister
of Jal Sakti.
“हे भोजनेन् जीवतम् भवतु, िबना जल(cid:198)तो: सव(cid:170)म् ही न(cid:212)यत् ।”
One can live without food, but without water, everything perishes. The farmers of
the drought-prone area of central Karnataka have been deprived of irrigation
facilities for more than five decades. After five decades of struggle, the Upper
Bhadra Project is being implemented in the State of Karnataka, which creates an
atchkat of 2,25,000 hectares in the drought-prone area of central region of
Karnataka through micro irrigation with an intent to fill approximately 367 tanks in
the command area. The command area spreads in Chikkamagaluru, Chitradurga,
Tumkuru and Davanagere districts. The project was started in the year 2008 and
the works are under progress.
In the Union Budget for the year 2023-2024, an amount of Rs. 5,300 crore
had been announced as Central Assistance for the Upper Bhadra Project. I
sincerely extend my gratitude for announcing Rs.5,300 crores as Central
Assistance.

Hon. Chairperson, Chitradurga district is my Lok Sabha constituency.
Majority of the people in my district are poor, small and marginal farmers. This
region is deprived of irrigation facilities. Around 30 to 40,000 people from each taluk
are migrating to big cities such as Bangalore, Davanagere, Hubli and Goa in search
of their livelihood. Their farm fields are left uncultivated as a result there is no crops
….. English translation of this part of speech originally delivered in Kannada.
19.03.2025 1525
grown. When such a situation was there during the year 2019 -2023, when our
government was in power, we had allocated Rs. 7,200 crores for taking up relief
measures in this region. However, the ruling Congress government is not giving
even a single rupee to address the problems of the people, So, all the
developmental works are stalled in this area. Our farmers are agitating over there
for fulfilling their demands.
Hence, through you, I would like to request our hon. Prime Minister and also
Hon’ble Minister for JAL Shakti to come forward and take necessary measures to
help the people of my region. Rs. 5,300 crores were announced for Upper Bhadra
canal Project in the Union budget for the year 2023-24. I urge upon the Union
government to release the amount immediately under the Pradhan Mantri Krishi
Sinchayi Yojana (PMKSY) and Accelerated Irrigation Benefits Programme (AIBP).
The government of Karnataka has not allocated any funds for the irrigation
schemes in the state for the last two years. As a result, all the works have been
stopped. I would like to draw the attention of the Union government with regard to
the Upper Bhadra project, which falls under in my Lok Sabha constituency itself.
Sir, Rs. 2400 crores that has to be paid to the Contractors. Since the amount is
not paid to the contractors, all of them have stopped their works in the proposed
projects in midway and they have no way but to migrate to other places.
Hon'ble chairperson, through you I want to draw the kind attention of the
Union government to the fact that the water in this area contains more Fluoride,
Nitrate and Iron contents, which are causing health hazards among the farmers
and poor people. Underground water has become poisonous as it contains
19.03.2025 1526
dangerous level of Fluoride. As a result of which our people are suffering from
various health issues.
Hence, I urge upon the Union government and Hon’ble Minister of Jalshakti
to take immediate steps to release the amount of Rs. 5,300 crores immediately that
was announced in Union budget 2023-24.
Thank you*.

DR. BACHHAV SHOBHA DINESH (DHULE): Hon. Chairman, Sir, thank you very
much for allowing me to speak on Demands for Grants of Jal Shakti Ministry for
2025-26. I would also like to thank our Leader Shri Rahulji Gandhi. Protection and
conservation of water resources is very important. Sir, I would like to share this
information regarding water conservation and irrigation projects under PM Krishi
Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) in my State Maharashtra. In my Lok Sabha
constituency Dhule, thee is an acute water scarcity and drinking water was supplied
once a week in Dhule city last year. It was supplied through Tapti pipeline and now
water is being supplied twice a week. This pipeline is 40-year-old pipeline and this
is in bad condition. So, it leaks continuously and water supply also get disturbed.
This 40 kilometre pipeline should be replaced immediately and for replacement
work, Rs. 225 crore should be provided through PMKSY. There is a dam called
Akkalpara and this dam is like a boon for Dhule district. But this dam have water
just 65 per cent of its maximum capacity. The remaining 35 per cent water flows
away. If we want to store 100 percent water, we would have to acquire 200 hectare
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
19.03.2025 1527
land in that area and we need Rs. 500 crore for land acquisition. This fund should
be given from PMKSY. I will help to ensure availability of water for drinking and
irrigation purposes.
Sir, at least 10 per cent water of Akkalpara Dam must be reserved as a
drinking water for Dhule city. For the locals of Shinkheda and Nandurbar, Prakasha
Burai lift irrigation scheme is very important. This project would ensure availability
of drinking and irrigation water for more than 28 villages and also provide irrigation
facility to 7025 hectare of land. This project would help for the overall development
of farming and horticulture in this area. But, it needs funding of Rs. 793 crore and
hence, I request you to provide it through PMKSY.
Haranmall Dam is located in Satana Tehsil and it is quite useful for drinking
and irrigation water in that area. But, 12 km long right side canal work is still
pending. So, it should be started immediately by providing Rs. 37 crore from
PMKSY.
Sir, Kelzar Dam is built on Aram river near Satana. It is also known as
Gopalsagar Dam. But the canal needs to be extended through the villages like
Shardeyash and Satmane and an extended DPR should be presented as early as
possible.
Zodge village in Malegaon Tehsil is a well-known market place and it is
located on Mumbai-Agra Highway. This village is facing water scarcity problem.
Jal Jeevan Mission work is underway since last two years. This water scarcity
problem is everywhere in my constituency and people are facing hardships.
So, I would like to request you to initiate an enquiry of the Jal Jeevan Mission
19.03.2025 1528
works. Rs. 25 crore should be provided for different works in Dhule rural,
Shinkheda. Rs. 198 crore should be given for Malegaon rural and Satana from
PMKSY. Sufficient funds should be given under Amrit Yojana for urban areas.
I request you to kindly look into it seriously and provide relief and assistance
for distressed people of my constituency Dhule. Thank you.

SHRI M. MALLESH BABU (KOLAR): Hon. Chairperson, Sir, thanks for giving me
the opportunity to discuss the Demands for Grants under the control of the Ministry
of Jal Shakti for the year 2025-26.
As we know, water plays an important and vital role in people’s life. As it is
very much essential for daily domestic activities, it is also important for agriculture
and allied activities like irrigation, enabling crop production, and food security of not
 Expunged as ordered by the Chair.
19.03.2025 1532
only human lives but also animal lives. Jal Jeevan Mission launched in 2019 aims
to provide functional tap water connections to all rural households by 2024. The
hon. Finance Minister has announced that the total budget outlay for Jal Jeevan
Mission has been enhanced to Rs. 67,000 crore for 2025-26 and the Mission is
extended until 2028, which is a welcome move. Today over 15 crore households,
representing 80 per cent of India’s rural population, have functional tap
connections, a monumental leap from just three crores in 2018.
Coming to my parliamentary constituency, Kolar, 2,72,456 households would
be benefitted by the Mission. Kolar lies just 60 kilometres from Bengaluru and for
more than a century its legendary gold mines fuelled not only Karnataka’s treasury
but also strengthened the very foundations of India’s economic backbone. Before
and after Independence, Kolar’s wealth helped build this nation, funding progress,
economic development, and the aspirations of the nation. But today, the legacy of
its gold is history, and in its place, a stark reality looms. The same land that once
flowed with prosperity now thirsts for something as fundamental as water. Kolar
has given gold to this nation; today, it asks only for water. Kolar is popularly known
as the land of silk, milk, mango, and tomatoes, which are being exported to various
countries. The district is also known for having large number of tanks, numbering
2,479 tanks.
21.00 hrs
Here, the farmers are purely dependent on groundwater where borewells are
sunk up to 1,500 feet depth since we do not have any perennial sources for
agriculture and allied activities. The entire district is facing a threat of groundwater depletion
19.03.2025 1533
thereby classifying the entire district as ‘over exploitation of groundwater’.
In view of this, the Karnataka Government has implemented KC-Valley and HN-Valley
projects thereby releasing the treated sewage water from Bangalore to the tanks in Kolar and
Chikkaballapur districts to improve the groundwater levels. This has resulted in various health
issues among women and youths and even indirect effects on crops.
The Yethinahole project is under implementation since 2007-2008 with an estimated
cost of Rs. 23,000 crore to provide clean and pure water to Bangalore Rural, Chikkaballapur
and Kolar districts. The project is being delayed due to various reasons.
Since, Kolar and Chikkaballapur districts are adjoined with Andhra Pradesh, I would
urge the Ministry of Jal Shakti and Prime Minister, Modi ji to intervene and allocate a minimum
quantity of water during the excess flow at Almathi Dam diverting Krishna River water which
has been flowing in the adjoining districts of Andhra Pradesh, namely Annammai and Chittoor
districts.
With these words, from my Party, Janata Dal Secular, I am concluding my speech on
the Demands for Grants of the Ministry of Jal Shakti for 2025-2026, and I support it. Thank
you.
माननीय सभापित : सभा क(cid:236) काय(cid:170)वाही ग(cid:321)ु वार, िदनांक 20 माच(cid:170), 2025 को (cid:255)ात: 11 बजे तक के िलए (cid:214)थिगत क(cid:236)
जाती है ।
21.01 hrs
The Lok Sabha then adjourned till Eleven of the Clock
on Thursday, March 20, 2025/Phalguna 29, 1946 (Saka).

INTERNET
The Original Version of Lok Sabha proceedings is available on Parliament of India Website at the
following address:
www.sansad.in/ls
LIVE TELECAST OF PROCEEDINGS OF LOK SABHA
Lok Sabha proceedings are being telecast live on Sansad T.V. Channel. Live telecast begins at 11
A.M. everyday the Lok Sabha sits, till the adjournment of the House.
